concise:
Ayah 1–3 · linked video: 01. Yusuf (Ayah 1-3)

Surah Yusuf: Commentary on Ayahs 1-3

Ayah 1

الٓر ۚ تِلْكَ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ٱلْمُبِينِ

Alif, Lam, Ra. Those are the miraculous signs of the clear and clarifying book.

Surah Yusuf stands as one of the most distinctive chapters in the Quran. Its uniqueness lies in presenting a complete narrative in a dedicated, chronological fashion. While the story of Musa (AS) receives extensive coverage across the Quran, particularly in Surah Ta-Ha, no other story is delivered with the sustained focus and narrative structure that we find here.

This surah was revealed during one of the most difficult periods in the Prophet’s ﷺ life, known as the Year of Grief (عام الحزن). He had lost his beloved wife Khadijah (RA) and his protective uncle Abu Talib, facing immense personal sorrow alongside the persecution from the Quraysh. Interestingly, the name Yusuf itself shares linguistic roots with the Arabic word أسف, which expresses sorrow and grief—the same emotion the Prophet ﷺ was experiencing when this surah was revealed.

The revelation also addressed specific challenges posed by the Jewish community in Madinah. They had instructed the Quraysh to ask the Prophet ﷺ certain questions designed to test his prophethood: How did the sons of Yaqub (AS) come to Egypt? What caused them to migrate from Syria to Egypt? These were historical details unknown even to many learned People of the Book. The response came in the form of this surah, while Surah Ta-Ha would later address the complementary question of why the Children of Yaqub (AS) left Egypt, completing the historical narrative.

The surah opens with the mysterious letters الر (Alif, Lam, Ra). As with other disjointed letters in the Quran, their appearance typically precedes a discussion about the Quran’s magnificence. Here, Allah ﷻ immediately draws attention to “those ayat of the clear and clarifying book”—the Kitab al-Mubin. This description emphasizes not just that the Book is clear in itself, but that it serves to clarify and make things plain for those who approach it sincerely.

Ayah 2

إِنَّآ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ قُرْءَٰنًا عَرَبِيًّا لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ

Indeed, We have sent it down as an Arabic Quran so that you may understand.

Allah ﷻ emphasizes the Arabic nature of the Quran with the phrase إِنَّآ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ (Indeed, We have sent it down). This carries profound theological implications. The use of “We”—the royal plural—underscores that these are the actual words of Allah ﷻ, revealed in Arabic. They are not the Prophet’s ﷺ paraphrasing of divine inspiration, nor are they his own eloquent expression of concepts given to him. The revelation (وحي) is distinct from the Prophet’s ﷺ own wisdom (حكمة), which he expressed in his own words as recorded in the hadith. The Quran stands apart as the literal speech of Allah ﷻ.

This principle establishes that no translation of the Quran can hold the same sacred status as the Arabic original. Any translation is merely an interpretation of meanings, not the Quran itself. The Arabic Quran alone carries the miraculous quality and sacred value that Allah ﷻ intended.

The phrase قُرْءَٰنًا عَرَبِيًّا (an Arabic Quran) appears eleven times throughout the Quran, repeatedly drawing attention to this fundamental characteristic. But why emphasize the Arabic nature specifically in this context? The answer lies in understanding the audience. The Jewish community that posed these questions to challenge the Prophet ﷺ were Arab Jews, not Hebrew speakers from Palestine. They had access to non-Arabic sources and traditions—Hebrew texts and oral histories passed down through generations—that the Arabs of Makkah could never access.

By revealing this detailed narrative in Arabic, Allah ﷻ presents undeniable proof: Muhammad ﷺ could not have learned this from any available source. The Arabs had no access to this knowledge. An unlettered prophet from among an unlettered people was receiving detailed historical accounts that only the learned among the People of the Book possessed. The Arabic revelation itself became evidence of its divine origin.

The phrase لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ (so that you may understand) carries multiple layers. Certainly it means linguistic comprehension—you understand the words being recited. But more profoundly, it means understanding the proof being presented: this knowledge coming through an Arabic Quran, from an Arab prophet with no access to Jewish sources, could only come from Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 3

نَحْنُ نَقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ أَحْسَنَ ٱلْقَصَصِ بِمَآ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْكَ هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانَ وَإِن كُنتَ مِن قَبْلِهِۦ لَمِنَ ٱلْغَـٰفِلِينَ

We relate to you the best of narratives through what We have revealed to you of this Quran, though before this you were among those unaware.

The term نَقُصُّ (We narrate) comes from the root قَصَّ, which originally meant to trace footsteps, following tracks in the sand. From this imagery emerged its meaning as narrating stories that are traced and passed down through generations. Stories told from parent to child, from teacher to student, following a chain of transmission. Among the Jewish community, the story of Yusuf (AS) was particularly beloved. Children would gather around elders to hear it, adults would reflect on its lessons, and it held a special place in their tradition.

Allah ﷻ declares this as أَحْسَنَ ٱلْقَصَصِ (the best of stories). This isn’t merely a compliment or religious hyperbole. By calling it “the best,” Allah ﷻ enters it into competition with every story ever told—every novel, every film, every epic, every tale of adventure or wisdom. This presents a challenge: How do we recognize this as truly the best story when we live in a world saturated with entertainment?

The issue lies in how we approach stories. Modern culture trains us to value stories primarily for entertainment. We watch movies to be thrilled, read novels to be captivated, and once we know the ending, the story loses its appeal. Entertainment depends on novelty, on not knowing what comes next. This poses a problem for religious narratives that we’re meant to return to repeatedly. If we approach the Quran’s stories with the same mindset we bring to entertainment, we’ll inevitably think, “I already know this story” and lose interest.

But the Quran’s purpose in narration transcends entertainment. Later in this surah, Allah ﷻ will reveal that لَقَدْ كَانَ فِي قَصَصِهِمْ عِبْرَةٌ (in their stories there is ’ibrah—lessons that move you). The word عِبْرَة comes from عَبَرَ, meaning to cross over, like crossing water. The term evolved to mean a lesson so emotionally powerful that tears cross over from your eyes—you cannot contain them. These are stories meant to move us deeply, to make us see our own lives reflected in them, to extract wisdom that transforms us.

This is why Allah ﷻ includes only relevant details in Quranic narratives. We’re not told the names of Yusuf’s brothers, his mother’s name, the minister’s name, or the specific city in Sham where the story begins. These details would add nothing to the lessons we need to learn. The Quran mentions Egypt specifically because it answers the historical question posed by the Jews, but otherwise focuses purely on the elements that teach us about human nature, family dynamics, patience, trust in Allah ﷻ, and redemption.

The ayah concludes with a reminder to the Prophet ﷺ: وَإِن كُنتَ مِن قَبْلِهِۦ لَمِنَ ٱلْغَـٰفِلِينَ (though before this you were among those unaware). The term غَافِلِينَ refers to those in a state of ghafla—unawareness or heedlessness. This unawareness can be spiritual (being unmindful of Allah ﷻ despite knowing better) or intellectual (simply not knowing certain information). Here it refers to intellectual unawareness: before this revelation, the Prophet ﷺ had no knowledge of these historical events.

The phrasing لَمِنَ ٱلْغَـٰفِلِينَ (among those unaware) is particularly significant. Allah ﷻ doesn’t merely say “you were unaware,” but “you were among those unaware”—emphasizing that the Prophet ﷺ and his entire community had no access to this information. None of the Arabs around him knew this story. This collective ignorance makes the detailed revelation all the more miraculous. The knowledge came purely through divine revelation—بِمَآ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْكَ (through what We have revealed to you).

As we prepare to explore this narrative, we should approach it not as passive observers of an ancient tale, but as students seeking to understand ourselves through it. The story of Yusuf (AS) offers profound insights into human psychology, family relationships, jealousy, patience, forgiveness, and divine wisdom. It teaches us about different personality types and how people respond to trials. Every character serves as a mirror, reflecting aspects of human nature that we need to understand and navigate in our own lives.

Ayah 4–6 · linked video: 02. Yusuf (Ayah 4-6)

Surah Yusuf: Ayahs 4-6

Ayah 4

إِذْ قَالَ يُوسُفُ لِأَبِيهِ يَـٰٓأَبَتِ إِنِّى رَأَيْتُ أَحَدَ عَشَرَ كَوْكَبًا وَٱلشَّمْسَ وَٱلْقَمَرَ رَأَيْتُهُمْ لِى سَـٰجِدِينَ

When Yusuf said to his father, “O my father, indeed I have seen eleven stars and the sun and the moon; I saw them prostrating to me.”

The Quran takes us into one of the most intimate moments of this profound story: a child waking from sleep, approaching his father to share a dream. Allah ﷻ could have begun with the dramatic events to come, but instead He shows us something quieter, something smaller, something that reveals the very foundation of this family.

The Respectful Address

Notice how Yusuf (AS) addresses his father: يَـٰٓأَبَتِ - “O my beloved father.” This isn’t a casual “Dad” or even a simple “Father.” The term carries deep affection and respect. In a household of twelve children, getting a parent’s attention requires wisdom. Yusuf (AS) doesn’t simply burst in and start talking. He first addresses his father respectfully, seeking his attention before sharing what’s on his heart.

This small detail reveals remarkable character training. Here is a child so mindful of adab that even in his excitement or nervousness about an extraordinary dream, he pauses to honor his father properly. The way we address our parents matters, and the Quran preserves this moment to teach us that lesson.

The Emphasis of Truth

Yusuf (AS) begins with إِنِّى- “Indeed I.” This particle serves multiple purposes. First, it removes doubt. What he’s about to share is so unusual that he anticipates disbelief, so he affirms it from the start: “I really did see this.” Second, رَأَيْتُ emphasizes “I am the one who saw it” - this isn’t secondhand information or imagination. He witnessed it himself.

The Vision Unfolds

أَحَدَ عَشَرَ كَوْكَبًا - eleven stars. The word كَوْكَب can refer to planets or stars, while نَجْم specifically means stars. Then وَٱلشَّمْسَ وَٱلْقَمَرَ - the sun and the moon.

The grammatical gender here is significant. الشَّمْس is feminine, while الْقَمَر is masculine. Many scholars, including companions of the Prophet ﷺ, understood this to represent his mother and father respectively.

The Profound Conclusion

Then comes the moment that takes his breath away: رَأَيْتُهُمْ لِى سَـٰجِدِينَ - “I saw them prostrating to me.”

Notice the repetition: رَأَيْتُ appears twice in this ayah. After mentioning the eleven stars, the sun and the moon, there’s a pause, as if the young Yusuf (AS) can barely continue. In a comic book, this would be represented by “…” - that trailing off when you’re so shocked you can’t find words. It’s as though his father had to encourage him: “It’s okay, you can tell me.”

So he starts again: “I saw them” - and then the shocking part - “prostrating to me.”

The normal word order in Arabic would place لِى at the end: رَأَيْتُهُم سَاجِدِينَ لِى. But he brings it forward for emphasis, showing his disbelief at what he witnessed. This is how children speak when they’re excited or nervous - they repeat themselves, they stammer, they struggle to express what overwhelmed them.

The Organization Reveals Understanding

The order itself tells us something profound. First the eleven stars (his brothers), then the sun (his mother), then the moon (his father who he knows is a prophet). This progression reveals increasing levels of shock. He’s surprised his brothers would prostrate to him, even more surprised his mother would, but absolutely astonished that his father - a prophet of Allah ﷻ - would honor him this way.

There’s another subtle indicator that Yusuf (AS) already grasps the meaning. The word سَـٰجِدِينَ uses the masculine plural, the form used for beings with intellect and will. For inanimate objects, Arabic typically uses the feminine singular or feminine plural. By using the masculine plural for the stars, sun and moon, Yusuf (AS) reveals he understands these celestial bodies represent people. This isn’t just about lights in the sky - this is about his family.

The Father-Son Bond

This entire interaction teaches us something beautiful about fatherhood. This is still just a dream, and it’s the dream of a child. How many of us dismiss our children’s words as mere ramblings? How often do we cut them off because they take too long to express themselves, because we have “important” things to do?

Yet here is Prophet Yaqub (AS), who doesn’t dismiss, doesn’t hurry, doesn’t interrupt. He sits and listens to every word. He gives this dream - this child’s dream - his complete attention and respect.

And from the child’s side, what kind of relationship has been built that his first instinct is to go to his father? Boys, especially as they grow older, often become less communicative. They don’t open up easily. But Yusuf (AS) feels so safe, so comfortable, so confident in his father’s love that this is exactly where he runs with his most vulnerable moment.

Yaqub (AS) is the role model father of the Quran, and this moment shows us why.

Ayah 5

قَالَ يَـٰبُنَىَّ لَا تَقْصُصْ رُءْيَاكَ عَلَىٰٓ إِخْوَتِكَ فَيَكِيدُوا۟ لَكَ كَيْدًا ۖ إِنَّ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنَ لِلْإِنسَـٰنِ عَدُوٌّ مُّبِينٌ

He said, “O my son, do not relate your vision to your brothers or they will contrive against you a plan. Indeed, Shaytan, to man, is a manifest enemy.”

The Tender Response

Yaqub (AS) doesn’t simply say يَا ابني(“O my son”). He says يَـٰبُنَىَّ - “O my dear son,” “O my beloved son.” You can picture him pulling the child close, perhaps embracing him, sitting him down with an arm around his shoulder. This is comfort and affection in action, not just words.

The first thing a father does when his child shares something vulnerable isn’t to lecture or analyze - it’s to provide emotional safety.

The Urgent Warning

لَا تَقْصُصْ رُءْيَاكَ عَلَىٰٓ إِخْوَتِكَ - “Do not relate your vision to your brothers.”

This includes even his younger brother Binyamin. A young child doesn’t understand discretion. If Yusuf (AS) mentions “eleven stars, the sun and the moon” to his little brother, that child will innocently repeat it. The other brothers will overhear. So the warning is absolute: tell no one.

Understanding the Plot

فَيَكِيدُوا۟ لَكَ كَيْدًا - “they will contrive against you a plan.”

The word choice here is remarkable. Yaqub (AS) doesn’t use مَكْر, which implies deceptive plotting meant to harm. Instead, he uses كَيْد, which can be either good or bad. If he were entirely pessimistic about his sons, he would have said فَيَمْكُرُوا (“they will scheme against you”). But كَيْد softens it slightly.

This reveals the heart of a father who still maintains some hope in his children. He’s saying, “They will do something - I have a strong hunch about this - but I’m not entirely certain of their intentions. It might not necessarily be purely against you.” He has no guarantees about what they might do, but he senses something, and he’s warning his beloved son to be cautious.

The Wisdom of a Realistic Father

Here we learn something profound about parenting: Yaqub (AS) is not delusional about his children. He doesn’t say, “All my children are the sons of a prophet, grandsons of a prophet, great-grandsons of Ibrahim (AS) - they would never do wrong.” He doesn’t hide behind their lineage or his own righteousness.

He sees them clearly. He loves them, but he sees them.

This is a critical lesson: our parenting is one thing, but who our children become as adults is something else entirely. These are two different matters. No one can argue that Nuh (AS) was a bad father, yet his son refused to board the ark. No one can argue that Yaqub (AS) was a negligent father, yet he has sons who will soon commit terrible acts.

We do our absolute best to raise our children with love, guidance, discipline and dua. But ultimately, they are not our possessions - they are servants of Allah ﷻ, and we are shepherds Allah ﷻ has appointed over them for a time. We don’t give up on them, but we also cannot control their every choice, especially as they grow into adults.

Blaming the Real Enemy

إِنَّ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنَ لِلْإنسَـٰنِ عَدُوٌّ مُّبِينٌ - “Indeed, Shaytan, to man, is a manifest enemy.”

Look at where Yaqub (AS) directs his concern. He recognizes his sons have gone astray, but he doesn’t say “your brothers are evil.” He says Shaytan is the enemy. His animosity grows not toward his children but toward their true enemy - the one who is leading them astray.

They are victims of Shaytan. This is the mindset of a prophetic father: recognize wrong, warn against it, but maintain hope and direct anger toward the real enemy.

Ayah 6

وَكَذَٰلِكَ يَجْتَبِيكَ رَبُّكَ وَيُعَلِّمُكَ مِن تَأْوِيلِ ٱلْأَحَادِيثِ وَيُتِمُّ نِعْمَتَهُۥ عَلَيْكَ وَعَلَىٰٓ ءَالِ يَعْقُوبَ كَمَآ أَتَمَّهَا عَلَىٰٓ أَبَوَيْكَ مِن قَبْلُ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَإِسْحَـٰقَ ۚ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ

And thus will your Lord choose you and teach you the interpretation of narratives and complete His favor upon you and upon the family of Yaqub, as He completed it upon your fathers before, Ibrahim and Ishaq. Indeed, your Lord is Knowing and Wise.

The Meaning of Divine Selection

وَكَذَٰلِكَ يَجْتَبِيكَ رَبُّكَ- “And thus will your Lord choose you.”

The word يَجْتَبِي comes from the root ج-ب-ي, which means to select or choose based on qualifications, on merit, on something that makes one worthy of that selection. This is different from اِصْطَفَى, which is selection based purely on divine will without regard to qualifications.

But here’s the question: what has Yusuf (AS) accomplished? He’s a child who saw a dream. Anyone can see a dream. Seeing a true dream isn’t an achievement in itself - it’s a gift from Allah ﷻ. Later in this surah, we’ll see that even Firaun sees true dreams, and prisoners see true dreams. So what qualifies him?

Yaqub (AS), being a wise father and a prophet, recognizes what we recognized in the ayah before: the way Yusuf (AS) described the dream shows he has already understood its meaning. The respectful manner in which he addressed his father, the organization of his words (brothers, then mother, then father), the grammatical choices he made (using the masculine plural for beings with intelligence) - all of this reveals a child with remarkable wisdom, insight and character.

Allah ﷻ didn’t just show him a dream; He gave him the early wisdom to interpret it and the adab to share it properly. That is what qualifies him. Yaqub (AS) sees this and affirms it: your Lord has chosen you because you have shown you are worthy of that selection.

The Gift of Interpretation

وَيُعَلِّمُكَ مِن تَأْوِيلِ ٱلْأَحَادِيثِ - “And He will teach you the interpretation of narratives.”

This phrase requires careful examination. There are three words for interpretation that appear in this surah, and understanding their distinctions enriches our comprehension.

تَأْوِيل is interpretation of something confusing or unclear - getting to the deeper meaning beneath surface appearances.

تَفْسِير is explaining something that may be complicated or difficult but not necessarily confusing.

إِفْتَاء - giving a fatwa - is addressing difficult, complex issues that require deep knowledge.

The word used here is تَأْوِيل, and it’s specifically connected to أَحَادِيث - narratives, stories, accounts, conversations. Yaqub (AS) is telling his son: you won’t just interpret dreams. You’ll be able to understand people through their speech. When people talk to you, you’ll grasp what’s beneath their words. You’ll be able to gauge their character, their intentions, their hearts just by conversing with them.

This is a profound gift - the ability to read people, to understand the unspoken, to perceive truth beneath the surface.

Completing the Favor

وَيُتِمُّ نِعْمَتَهُۥ عَلَيْكَ - “And He will complete His favor upon you.”

Notice the language: complete to perfection. This isn’t just about receiving blessings; it’s about those blessings reaching their fullest manifestation. The dream is just the beginning. Allah ﷻ has started this journey with you, and He will see it through to completion.

وَعَلَىٰٓ ءَالِ يَعْقُوبَ - “And upon the family of Yaqub.”

This is where Yaqub (AS) makes something beautiful clear to his son: “This isn’t just about you, my child. Your honor is our honor. Your elevation is our elevation. By coming to me this morning and sharing this dream, you haven’t just secured your own future - you’ve brought blessing to this entire family.”

Imagine the impact of these words on a young boy. His father is taking pride in him for seeing a dream. His great accomplishment happened while he was asleep, yet it warrants celebration, affirmation and prophecies of greatness.

This is the power of encouragement in parenting. When your child accomplishes something - even something that seems small or beyond their control - acknowledge it. Celebrate it. Build them up with it.

The Legacy of the Prophets

كَمَآ أَتَمَّهَا عَلَىٰٓ أَبَوَيْكَ مِن قَبْلُ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَإِسْحَـٰقَ - “As He completed it upon your fathers before, Ibrahim and Ishaq.”

“You are going to live up to the legacy of your great-grandfathers. You’re going to make them proud. Ibrahim (AS) and Ishaq (AS) - your grandfather and great-grandfather - would have been so proud of you.”

Yaqub (AS) connects his son to his lineage, to a legacy of prophets, to a standard of excellence. He doesn’t use this legacy to burden him (“why can’t you be more like them?”) but to inspire him (“you are worthy of walking in their footsteps”).

The Balance of Warning and Encouragement

Notice the structure of Yaqub’s (AS) response. He begins with a warning - don’t tell your brothers. Warnings can be frightening, especially for a child. But then he showers Yusuf (AS) with encouragement, affirmation and prophecies of greatness.

Our parenting shouldn’t be entirely negative - constant criticism and comparison. But neither should it be entirely permissive. There’s a balance between guiding away from danger and building up toward excellence.

Divine Knowledge and Wisdom

إِنَّ رَبَّكَ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ - “Indeed, your Lord is Knowing and Wise.”

Yaqub (AS) concludes by attributing everything to Allah ﷻ. “Allah ﷻ has given you a droplet of His knowledge through this dream, and His plans for you are full of wisdom.”

The profound irony is that Yaqub (AS) doesn’t know the tragedies about to unfold. His outlook for Yusuf’s (AS) future is entirely positive. From his perspective, this is the beginning of an immediate rise to greatness. He has no idea his son will be thrown in a well, sold into slavery, falsely accused, imprisoned for years.

Yet his words remain absolutely true. Allah ﷻ is All-Knowing - He knows the path this child must walk. And Allah ﷻ is All-Wise - every step of that painful journey will serve a purpose.

This ayah is full of hope, celebration and joy. It reads like a father’s blessing over his beloved son. And that makes what follows in the story even more striking - a reminder that Allah’s ﷻ wisdom often unfolds in ways we cannot predict, through trials we wouldn’t choose, toward destinations more magnificent than we could have imagined.

Ayah 7–11 · linked video: 03. Yusuf (Ayah 7-11)

PRIMARY_AYAH: 7

Ayah 7

۞ لَّقَدْ كَانَ فِى يُوسُفَ وَإِخْوَتِهِۦٓ ءَايَـٰتٌ لِّلسَّآئِلِينَ

Certainly, in Yusuf and his brothers there are signs for those who ask.

This ayah establishes a fundamental principle for approaching the entire surah: both Yusuf (AS) and his brothers serve as vessels of divine instruction. Allah ﷻ explicitly tells us that lessons will emerge not only from the righteous protagonist but equally from those who wrong him. This is a sophisticated pedagogical approach—Muslims are being told to pay close attention to both characters, to learn from virtue and vice alike.

The phrase لِّلسَّآئِلِينَ (for those who ask) carries historical weight. The Jewish community had posed questions to the Prophet ﷺ about how the Children of Israel ended up in Egypt. Allah ﷻ responds that the very telling of this narrative—the revelation of private conversations that occurred within the walls of Ya’qub’s (AS) home—contains miraculous signs sufficient to confirm the truth of Islam. How could Muhammad ﷺ know these intimate family details except through divine revelation?

The word ءَايَـٰتٌ (signs) appears in the plural, indicating that the lessons are numerous and layered. We will extract wisdom from Yusuf’s (AS) patience, from his brothers’ jealousy, from their father’s grief, and from the intricate workings of divine decree that transforms family tragedy into national deliverance.

Ayah 8

إِذْ قَالُوا۟ لَيُوسُفُ وَأَخُوهُ أَحَبُّ إِلَىٰٓ أَبِينَا مِنَّا وَنَحْنُ عُصْبَةٌ إِنَّ أَبَانَا لَفِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ

When they said: “Surely Yusuf and his brother are more beloved to our father than we are, while we are a strong group. Indeed, our father is clearly mistaken.”

The camera shifts now to the brothers. We enter a scene of conspiracy, a moment when jealousy crystallizes into something darker and more dangerous.

Notice the deliberate structure of their complaint: لَيُوسُفُ وَأَخُوهُ (Yusuf and his brother). The name Yusuf is mentioned with emphasis, almost spat out, while his full brother remains unnamed—reduced to “his brother.” This linguistic choice reveals where their anger is truly concentrated. Though they resent both children of their father’s beloved wife, Yusuf (AS) bears the brunt of their hatred because his exceptional qualities make their own shortcomings unbearable by comparison.

The brothers say أَحَبُّ إِلَىٰٓ أَبِينَا مِنَّا (more beloved to our father than we are). By framing it this way—emphasizing their father’s affection rather than Yusuf’s qualities—they redirect their anger toward Ya’qub (AS). If they had said “Yusuf and his brother are more beloved to us than our father,” the rage would have been directed at the children. But by saying “to our father,” they begin to blame their parent for what they perceive as unjust favoritism.

This is a pattern tragically familiar in dysfunctional families: children who disappoint their parents often blame their failures on the sibling who succeeds. “You always loved him more—that’s why I turned out this way.” It becomes an excuse, a way to avoid self-accountability. Instead of looking inward and asking why they don’t share the same relationship with their father, they externalize all responsibility.

There may have been an element of truth in their perception. Perhaps Ya’qub (AS) did show more affection to Yusuf (AS)—not out of injustice, but because different children elicit different responses. Yusuf (AS) was born with extraordinary beauty, wisdom and a naturally respectful disposition. His brothers, from what we can infer, had developed troubling character traits. A parent naturally gravitates toward the child who is obedient, kind and easy to nurture, while the child who constantly argues, complains and rebels requires more discipline. This doesn’t excuse favoritism, but it explains the human dynamics at play.

Yet a person of maturity looks at himself first before blaming others. The brothers failed this fundamental test of character. They couldn’t accept that perhaps their own behavior had created distance between themselves and their father.

Then comes their self-description: وَنَحْنُ عُصْبَةٌ (while we are a strong group). The word عُصْبَةٌ refers to a band of men, specifically numbering between five and ten, bound together in strength. It carries connotations of toughness, of being a group that doesn’t get pushed around. There’s an implied threat in this word—“We’re the strong ones, the ones people fear, the ones who stick together.”

From this same root comes the word عَصَبِيَّة—tribal prejudice, the arrogance that comes from group strength. They saw themselves as a formidable unit, and this collective identity fed their sense of entitlement. How dare their father prefer two younger boys over this powerful band of brothers?

Their conclusion is shocking: إِنَّ أَبَانَا لَفِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (Indeed, our father is clearly mistaken). The word ضَلَـٰلٍ means to be lost, to go astray, to be in error. In its strongest usage, it means misguidance. They’re essentially saying their father—a prophet of Allah ﷻ—has lost his sound judgment. He’s become senile, obsessed with this one child to the point of irrationality.

This represents a dangerous psychological state. When jealousy takes root, it distorts perception so completely that even a prophet appears misguided in the eyes of his own sons.

Ayah 9

ٱقْتُلُوا۟ يُوسُفَ أَوِ ٱطْرَحُوهُ أَرْضًا يَخْلُ لَكُمْ وَجْهُ أَبِيكُمْ وَتَكُونُوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ قَوْمًا صَـٰلِحِينَ

“Kill Yusuf or cast him out to some land so that your father’s attention will be only for you, and after that you may become righteous people.”

The suggestion erupts with chilling suddenness: ٱقْتُلُوا۟ يُوسُفَ (Kill Yusuf). The Quran doesn’t tell us which brother first voiced this thought, and that ambiguity is significant. It suggests that the idea hung in the air among all of them, that perhaps multiple voices echoed the same darkness. This is the nature of mob mentality—individuals who might hesitate alone become emboldened in a group, their conscience submerged beneath collective emotion.

This is the second murder in human history rooted in jealousy. The first was Cain killing Abel, and here we see the pattern repeat among the sons of a prophet. Jealousy, left unchecked, metastasizes into violence.

The alternative they propose: أَوِ ٱطْرَحُوهُ أَرْضًا (or cast him out to some land). The verb طَرَحَ means to throw away something worthless, like tossing a candy wrapper into the trash. It conveys utter disregard, disposing of something you consider garbage. They don’t even specify which land—أَرْضًا, just “a land,” anywhere, it doesn’t matter. The vagueness reveals their callousness. They haven’t thought this through; they just want him gone.

Their twisted rationale follows: يَخْلُ لَكُمْ وَجْهُ أَبِيكُمْ (so that your father’s attention will be only for you). When someone turns their face toward you, it means they’re giving you their attention. The brothers believed that once Yusuf (AS) was eliminated, their father’s face—his focus, his affection, his approval—would turn exclusively to them.

This is magical thinking at its most delusional. They imagined that murdering their father’s beloved son would somehow make him love them more. The logic is absurd, yet this is what unchecked emotion does—it obliterates rational thought.

The final phrase is perhaps the most disturbing: وَتَكُونُوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ قَوْمًا صَـٰلِحِينَ (and after that you may become righteous people). One brother—likely the one proposing this plan—offers reassurance to the others: “Yes, this is terrible, but afterward we’ll repent. We’ll make our father proud. We’ll be good sons from that point forward.”

This reveals the ultimate self-deception. They planned to commit a heinous crime and then simply “become righteous” afterward, as if moral transformation could be scheduled around their convenience. They thought their father would see them as good because there would be no one left to compare them to. This was a crime of passion wrapped in a promise of reform—a promise they had no intention of keeping.

This ayah demonstrates how twisted conceptions of love can drive people to commit terrible acts. When love becomes possessive, when it demands exclusivity, when it cannot tolerate the beloved’s affection for anyone else, it ceases to be love and becomes a form of violence.

Ayah 10

قَالَ قَآئِلٌ مِّنْهُمْ لَا تَقْتُلُوا۟ يُوسُفَ وَأَلْقُوهُ فِى غَيَـٰبَتِ ٱلْجُبِّ يَلْتَقِطْهُ بَعْضُ ٱلسَّيَّارَةِ إِن كُنتُمْ فَـٰعِلِينَ

A speaker among them said: “Do not kill Yusuf, but throw him into the bottom of the well; some caravan will pick him up, if you are going to do anything.”

One voice breaks through the murderous consensus. The Quran identifies him only as قَآئِلٌ مِّنْهُمْ (a speaker among them). The word قَآئِلٌ suggests someone with the ability to speak persuasively, someone whose voice carries weight in the group. Classical sources identify this as the eldest brother, the one with enough authority to potentially halt the violence.

His intervention begins with لَا تَقْتُلُوا۟ يُوسُفَ (Do not kill Yusuf)—a clear prohibition. Yet notice what he doesn’t say. He doesn’t say “Leave him alone” or “This is wrong” or “Our father would be devastated.” He offers no moral argument against their plan. His concern is practical, perhaps tinged with just enough conscience to avoid outright murder.

His alternative: وَأَلْقُوهُ فِى غَيَـٰبَتِ ٱلْجُبِّ (throw him into the bottom of the well). The word أَلْقُوهُ (throw him) still carries violence. This isn’t gently lowering someone down; this is casting someone in. The phrase غَيَـٰبَتِ ٱلْجُبِّ is particularly significant. غَيَـٰبَتِ comes from the root غ-ي-ب, meaning concealment, hiddenness, the unseen. It refers to the darkest depths of the well, the bottom where no light reaches.

The word ٱلْجُبِّ specifies a particular type of well—one without the brick construction that rises above ground. Unlike a بِئْر, which has a visible structure marking its location, a jubb is simply a hole in the ground, often hidden, neglected, far from civilization. By using this term, Allah ﷻ emphasizes the remoteness and concealment of the location they had in mind.

The brother’s reasoning continues: يَلْتَقِطْهُ بَعْضُ ٱلسَّيَّارَةِ (some caravan will pick him up). The verb يَلْتَقِطْ is used for finding something by chance, stumbling upon something unexpected. It’s the word used when you pick up a lost child wandering on the road or when you find a wallet someone dropped. There’s an element of accident, of unplanned discovery.

But this was pure speculation. They were leaving Yusuf (AS) in an isolated jubb in the wilderness. What caravan would pass through such a desolate area? Caravans follow established trade routes, and on those routes you would find proper wells with brick construction, not hidden pits. This brother was offering false hope to ease everyone’s conscience—including his own.

The phrase إِن كُنتُمْ فَـٰعِلِينَ (if you are going to do anything) is revealing. It’s almost a guilt trip: “Look, if you’re bent on doing something, at least do this instead of murder.” He recognized he couldn’t stop them entirely, so he negotiated for a lesser evil.

This is groupthink in action—what social psychologists call mob mentality. When a group becomes emotionally charged and unified around a terrible idea, even dissenting voices don’t truly dissent. They just try to minimize the damage while going along with the group’s direction. The fear of standing completely apart, of having the group’s anger turn against you, is too powerful.

According to some classical narrations, the brothers threatened this eldest brother: “If you try to stop us, we’ll kill you too.” Faced with nine angry brothers, he chose compromise over confrontation.

Ayah 11

قَالُوا۟ يَـٰٓأَبَانَا مَا لَكَ لَا تَأْمَ۫نَّا عَلَىٰ يُوسُفَ وَإِنَّا لَهُۥ لَنَـٰصِحُونَ

They said: “O our father, why do you not trust us with Yusuf while indeed we are his sincere well-wishers?”

The brothers return to their father, and now we witness their manipulation in full display. The dialogue shifts from conspiracy to performance.

They address him with يَـٰٓأَبَانَا (O our father)—contrast this with how Yusuf (AS) addressed his father earlier with يَـٰٓأَبَتِ (O my dear father), a term of endearment and intimacy. The brothers use the more formal, distant يَـٰٓأَبَانَا. The difference is subtle but significant.

Their opening gambit employs reverse psychology: مَا لَكَ لَا تَأْمَ۫نَّا عَلَىٰ يُوسُفَ (Why do you not trust us with Yusuf?). The phrase مَا لَكَ (What is with you?) can mean “What’s wrong with you?” depending on tone. It carries a note of injury, of wounded feelings.

The brilliance of Allah’s ﷻ word choice here is that لَا تَأْمَ۫نَّا can be read two ways. Read one way, it’s a sarcastic statement: “Fine, don’t trust us then. See if we care.” This is the tone children use when they’re trying to guilt their parents: “Fine, don’t love me. Don’t talk to me. I don’t care.”

Read another way, it’s a straightforward question: “Why don’t you trust us? What have we done to make you doubt us?” Both meanings resonate simultaneously in the Arabic, capturing the layers of their manipulation.

Then comes the assertion: وَإِنَّا لَهُۥ لَنَـٰصِحُونَ (while indeed we are his sincere well-wishers). The double emphasis—إِنَّا (indeed we) and the lam of emphasis in لَنَـٰصِحُونَ—makes this an emphatic oath. “We swear we want what’s best for him. We love him. Who else would care for him if not us, his own brothers?”

The word نَـٰصِحُونَ comes from نُصْح, meaning sincere advice, genuine concern for someone’s welfare. They’re claiming to be his most devoted guardians.

This is the language of predators. They protested too much, swore too emphatically, declared their love too forcefully. Ya’qub (AS), with a prophet’s insight, surely sensed something amiss. His hesitation to trust them wasn’t paranoia—it was wisdom.

The scene ends here, suspended in tension. The brothers have made their request. The conspiracy has been set in motion. And Yusuf (AS), innocent and unaware, stands at the threshold of a trial that will reshape not just his life, but the destiny of nations.

Ayah 12–18 · linked video: 04. Yusuf (Ayah 12-18)

Surah Yusuf: Ayahs 12-18

Ayah 12

أَرْسِلْهُ مَعَنَا غَدًا يَرْتَعْ وَيَلْعَبْ وَإِنَّا لَهُۥ لَحَـٰفِظُونَ

The conversation between Prophet Yaqub (AS) and his sons continues as they present their carefully planned request. They say أَرْسِلْهُ مَعَنَا غَدًا - “Send him with us tomorrow.” Notice they’ve already laid out the entire schedule. This isn’t a casual invitation - they’ve thought this through, making it sound like the perfect day trip.

The word يَرْتَعْ is particularly telling. Literally, it describes how animals graze freely. They’re essentially saying, “He’s going to feast, he’s going to eat so much!” The image is of someone enjoying food with both hands, completely at ease. Then they add وَيَلْعَبْ - “and he will play.” They’re painting a picture of pure enjoyment and relaxation.

But the most critical part of their persuasion comes at the end: وَإِنَّا لَهُۥ لَحَـٰفِظُونَ - “And indeed, we will be his guardians.” The emphasis here is extraordinary. They’re saying, “We will watch over him specifically. We’ll drop our attention from everything else. This entire trip is just for him.” The irony is devastating - these are the very brothers who’ve been consumed with jealousy, now claiming they’re the ones who truly love him and will protect him.

Ayah 13

قَالَ إِنِّى لَيَحْزُنُنِىٓ أَن تَذْهَبُوا۟ بِهِۦ وَأَخَافُ أَن يَأْكُلَهُ ٱلذِّئْبُ وَأَنتُمْ عَنْهُ غَـٰفِلُونَ

The response of Prophet Yaqub (AS) reveals extraordinary wisdom and captures two distinct emotional states in a single ayah. He says قَالَ إِنِّى لَيَحْزُنُنِىٓ - “He said: Indeed it saddens me…” The word حَزَن specifically refers to grief and sadness. But notice something profound here - sadness is typically about what has already happened, while fear relates to what hasn’t occurred yet. Yaqub (AS) is discussing something that hasn’t happened yet, so the expected emotion should be fear, not sadness.

Why does he express sadness about a future event? Because in his heart, he already knows it’s inevitable. Once his sons began this conversation, once he saw how insistent they were being, how unusually kind and persuasive - something they’d never shown toward Yusuf (AS) before - he realized his fears were coming true. This is the intelligence of a father who understands his children. He sees through their act. The sadness he feels is because he knows: they will take Yusuf (AS) away from him.

Then he mentions his fear: وَأَخَافُ أَن يَأْكُلَهُ ٱلذِّئْبُ - “And I fear that a wolf could eat him.” Why mention the wolf specifically? Because Yaqub (AS) is too wise to directly accuse his sons. He understands that confronting psychologically unstable people head-on can make their condition worse. So he offers what seems like a desperate excuse, but even in this excuse, he doesn’t lie.

The word الذِّئْبُ includes the definite article - “the wolf,” not “a wolf.” He knows which wolf he’s talking about. His sons are the wolves. From a figurative perspective, he’s expressing the absolute truth. They are the predators, the danger. But he phrases it in a way that gives them an out, that doesn’t force a direct confrontation.

The ayah ends with وَأَنتُمْ عَنْهُ غَـٰفِلُونَ - “while you are of him unaware.” This phrase works on multiple levels. On the surface, it’s part of his excuse about the wolf. But deeper, it’s the reality - they truly will be unaware of what becomes of Yusuf (AS). They’ll laugh about what they’ve done, completely heedless of the actual consequences of their actions.

Ayah 14

قَالُوا۟ لَئِنْ أَكَلَهُ ٱلذِّئْبُ وَنَحْنُ عُصْبَةٌ إِنَّآ إِذًا لَّخَـٰسِرُونَ

The brothers respond with indignation: قَالُوا۟ لَئِنْ أَكَلَهُ ٱلذِّئْبُ - “They said: If a wolf should eat him…” They’re almost offended by the suggestion. The word عُصْبَة refers to a strong band or group - they’re essentially saying, “We’re a gang, we’re a crew, we’re a team.”

وَنَحْنُ عُصْبَةٌ إِنَّآ إِذًا لَّخَـٰسِرُونَ - “while we are a group, indeed we would then be losers.” Their argument is: “Come on, Dad. We’re not losers like that. How could a wolf possibly get to him when there are so many of us?”

The tragic irony is that they will indeed be losers - but not in the way they’re claiming. They’ll be losers in a far more profound sense, having committed a crime against their innocent brother that will haunt them for years to come.

Ayah 15

فَلَمَّا ذَهَبُوا۟ بِهِۦ وَأَجْمَعُوٓا۟ أَن يَجْعَلُوهُ فِى غَيَـٰبَتِ ٱلْجُبِّ ۚ وَأَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْهِ لَتُنَبِّئَنَّهُم بِأَمْرِهِمْ هَـٰذَا وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ

فَلَمَّا ذَهَبُوا۟ بِهِۦ - “So when they took him away…” The ayah doesn’t specify the exact time, but the context from the previous ayah (غَدًا - tomorrow) tells us this happened the next day, in the morning. The brothers took Yusuf (AS) and went.

وَأَجْمَعُوٓا۟ أَن يَجْعَلُوهُ فِى غَيَـٰبَتِ ٱلْجُبِّ - “And they came to a consensus to put him in the depths of the well.” The word أَجْمَعُوا indicates unanimous agreement - they all united in this decision. The word غَيَـٰبَت refers to something hidden, concealed, unseen.

Now notice the word الْجُبّ - this is important. A jubb is specifically a well that doesn’t have very deep water, where the water level is shallow. This detail matters because it meant Yusuf (AS) could survive. Allah ﷻ was already arranging the means of preservation even in the midst of this terrible crime.

Then comes one of the most powerful moments in the entire surah: وَأَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْهِ لَتُنَبِّئَنَّهُم بِأَمْرِهِمْ هَـٰذَا وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ - “And We revealed to him: You will surely inform them of this affair of theirs while they perceive not.”

Picture this scene: Yusuf (AS) is a young boy, terrified, being lowered into a dark well by his own brothers. He doesn’t know if he’ll survive. He doesn’t know what’s going to happen next. He’s experiencing unimaginable trauma. And in that precise moment of terror, Allah ﷻ sends revelation to calm him down, to give him hope, to tell him: “Don’t worry. You’re going to have a reunion with them. You’re going to tell them all about this day. They won’t even realize it’s you when it happens.”

This is a profound lesson about how Allah ﷻ supports those who go through trauma. Just as He sent revelation to comfort Musa’s mother (AS) when she had to put her infant in the river, He sends revelation here to comfort this child in his moment of greatest fear. The revelation itself becomes a source of peace and assurance in the midst of catastrophe.

Ayah 16

وَجَآءُوٓ أَبَاهُمْ عِشَآءً يَبْكُونَ

وَجَآءُوٓ أَبَاهُمْ عِشَآءً - “And they came to their father at night…” They spent the entire day away and returned عِشَآءً - in the evening, at nighttime. The timing itself is significant - they had the whole day to prepare their story, to work on their act.

يَبْكُونَ - “weeping.” They come crying. But this crying had to be manufactured, worked on, made to look genuine. The image is almost darkly comical - imagine them punching each other to make the tears flow, practicing their performance. They had to make it look real.

The ayah is deliberately brief, almost cinematic. We see them approaching in the darkness, tears streaming down their faces, about to deliver their rehearsed lines to their father.

Ayah 17

قَالُوا۟ يَـٰٓأَبَانَآ إِنَّا ذَهَبْنَا نَسْتَبِقُ وَتَرَكْنَا يُوسُفَ عِندَ مَتَـٰعِنَا فَأَكَلَهُ ٱلذِّئْبُ ۖ وَمَآ أَنتَ بِمُؤْمِنٍ لَّنَا وَلَوْ كُنَّا صَـٰدِقِينَ

Their fabricated story begins: قَالُوا۟ يَـٰٓأَبَانَآ - “They said: O our father!” إِنَّا ذَهَبْنَا نَسْتَبِقُ - “Indeed we went racing.” The word نَسْتَبِقُ comes from the root meaning to compete, to try to get ahead of one another. They’re setting up the scene: “We were just having fun, racing each other.”

وَتَرَكْنَا يُوسُفَ عِندَ مَتَـٰعِنَا - “And we left Yusuf with our possessions.” They’re trying to make it sound so innocent - they left him to guard their picnic supplies, their belongings. “Watch our stuff, we’ll be right back.”

فَأَكَلَهُ ٱلذِّئْبُ - “And a wolf ate him.” Notice they use their father’s own words, the very excuse he gave them. They’re throwing it back at him: “You know that wolf you mentioned, Dad? You were right. That’s exactly what happened.”

But then something remarkable occurs - their guilty conscience begins to speak: وَمَآ أَنتَ بِمُؤْمِنٍ لَّنَا وَلَوْ كُنَّا صَـٰدِقِينَ - “But you would not believe us, even if we were truthful.”

Yaqub (AS) hasn’t said a word yet. He’s just looking at them. Any parent knows that look - that penetrating gaze that says, “I know exactly what you’re doing.” And under that gaze, they crack. They incriminate themselves. They basically say, “We know you’re not buying this. We know you don’t believe us at all.”

The phrase وَلَوْ كُنَّا صَـٰدِقِينَ - “even if we were truthful” - is particularly revealing. It’s almost a complaint: “Even if we were telling the truth, you wouldn’t believe us anyway!” But by saying this, they’ve essentially admitted they’re not telling the truth. A truly innocent person doesn’t preemptively defend against disbelief. Their words expose the lie they’re trying to maintain.

Ayah 18

وَجَآءُو عَلَىٰ قَمِيصِهِۦ بِدَمٍ كَذِبٍ ۚ قَالَ بَلْ سَوَّلَتْ لَكُمْ أَنفُسُكُمْ أَمْرًا ۖ فَصَبْرٌ جَمِيلٌ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُسْتَعَانُ عَلَىٰ مَا تَصِفُونَ

وَجَآءُو عَلَىٰ قَمِيصِهِۦ بِدَمٍ كَذِبٍ - “And they brought upon his shirt false blood.” The word قَمِيص - shirt - appears three times in this surah, and each time it’s connected to a significant event in Yusuf’s (AS) life. Twice in his life, shirts will get him into trouble with emotionally disturbed people.

The phrase بِدَمٍ كَذِبٍ - “false blood” - is understood to mean they came with his shirt that had false blood on it. They’d probably taken some animal blood and sprinkled it on the garment. But here’s what’s devastating: if a wolf had actually attacked and eaten someone, the shirt would be torn, ripped to shreds. This shirt had blood but no tears, no rips from claws or teeth. The evidence itself contradicted their story.

Yaqub (AS) responds with profound wisdom: قَالَ بَلْ سَوَّلَتْ لَكُمْ أَنفُسُكُمْ أَمْرًا - “He said: Rather, your souls have enticed you to something.”

The word سَوَّلَتْ is fascinating. It comes from a root that literally refers to the soft, jiggly part of the belly below the navel. From this physical meaning comes the figurative sense: to make something squishy, to make something appear soft and easy, no big deal. It means to rationalize, to convince yourself that something terrible is actually acceptable or even good.

This is what psychologists call rationalization - how criminals justify their behavior in their own minds until they become completely convinced they were doing the right thing. Kidnappers go on trial claiming they were the best thing that happened to the child, that they were protecting them, caring for them. They rationalize until the crime becomes virtuous in their own twisted perception.

بَلْ سَوَّلَتْ لَكُمْ أَنفُسُكُمْ أَمْرًا - “Your own deeper souls, your own twisted personalities convinced you that this terrible thing was no big deal.” Yaqub (AS) recognizes what has happened. When people become this delusional, when they’ve rationalized evil to this extent, there’s no point in arguing with them. You cannot reason with people who have convinced themselves that wrong is right.

And so he makes a choice that seems shocking at first: فَصَبْرٌ جَمِيلٌ - “So patience is most fitting.”

For a moment, consider what any parent would do if their child came home and confessed to destroying something valuable, let alone harming a sibling. The natural response would be anger, confrontation, consequences. But Yaqub (AS) demonstrates extraordinary wisdom - he recognizes that his sons have reached a point where yelling, arguing, or punishing will only make things worse. They’re adults. They’ve committed an unspeakable crime. His only option now is صَبْرٌ جَمِيلٌ - beautiful patience.

But understand the weight of what he’s enduring. He’s not just lost a child - he’s lost a child without knowing what happened to him. At least when someone dies, a believing parent knows their child is with Allah ﷻ. But Yaqub (AS) doesn’t know if Yusuf (AS) is dead or alive. He doesn’t know whose hands he’s in. He knows his son is beautiful, and people can have all kinds of evil intentions. This is the agony of a parent dealing with a kidnapping - one of the most psychologically devastating crimes imaginable. Parents lose their sanity over this. The uncertainty, the not knowing, the horrific possibilities that flood the mind.

Yet despite this inconceivable trauma, he says: وَٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُسْتَعَانُ عَلَىٰ مَا تَصِفُونَ - “And Allah is the One sought for help against that which you describe.”

المُسْتَعَان - the only One whose aid can be sought. His sons came with elaborate descriptions - the wolf was gray, it had sharp teeth, somehow it managed to take the shirt off politely without even tearing it with its claws. They’re describing and describing, piling detail upon detail to make their lie sound convincing.

Yaqub’s (AS) response is essentially: “Enough. I don’t need to hear your descriptions anymore. Allah is the One I turn to for help against these fabrications you’re presenting.”

This is perhaps the most profound lesson of these ayahs: how to maintain faith through inconceivable tragedy. Most people lose their faith because of trauma. Something terrible happens and they stop believing, asking “How could Allah let this happen to me? What did I do to deserve this?” If you saw this story on the news - a child kidnapped, apparently sold into slavery in some distant land - wouldn’t you change the channel? Wouldn’t you question how such evil could be allowed to happen?

Allah ﷻ shows us this scenario because we need to learn. The greatest prophets went through the most horrible tragedies. Losing a child. Losing your young, gifted child the day after having a beautiful dream about his future. And yet what we’ll discover as this surah continues is that both father and son emerge from this tragedy with increased faith, not diminished faith.

This is more than any other surah teaches us how to cope with trauma, how to find صَبْرٌ جَمِيلٌ - beautiful patience - even when the situation is indescribably ugly, even when every circumstance screams injustice. The answer isn’t in the circumstances. The answer is in turning to Allah ﷻ - وَٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُسْتَعَانُ - He is the One sought for help.

Ayah 19–23 · linked video: 05. Yusuf (Ayah 19-23)

Surah Yusuf: Ayahs 19-23

Ayah 19

وَجَآءَتْ سَيَّارَةٌ فَأَرْسَلُوا۟ وَارِدَهُمْ فَأَدْلَىٰ دَلْوَهُۥ ۖ قَالَ يَـٰبُشْرَىٰ هَـٰذَا غُلَـٰمٌ ۚ وَأَسَرُّوهُ بِضَـٰعَةً ۚ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌۢ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ

The scene shifts now from the house of Yaqub (AS) back to the well where Yusuf (AS) had been left. A caravan arrived at this location, traveling with their camels and horses. Like any traveling group crossing through the wilderness, they needed to find water. They sent their scout ahead - the وَارِد, the one who descends from elevated ground to search for water sources, or to check for potential dangers like bandits.

When this scout discovered the well, he lowered his bucket down. Notice the precision of the Quranic language here: فَأَدْلَىٰ دَلْوَهُۥ - “he lowered his bucket.” The ayah doesn’t simply say “he lowered a bucket,” but specifically “his bucket.” This detail reveals something significant: the well didn’t have its own bucket and rope system. The brothers had simply thrown Yusuf (AS) down into an empty well. The scout had to use his own equipment to draw water.

The word أَدْلَىٰ means to lower something down gradually. We’ve encountered this root before in the Quran - in the phrase شَيْءٍ قَلِيلًا (gradually, little by little), describing how revelation came down in stages. Here it describes the physical act of lowering the bucket into the well’s depths.

When the scout pulled up his bucket, he discovered Yusuf (AS). His immediate reaction was يَـٰبُشْرَىٰ - but this isn’t the formal “glad tidings” that our English translations often provide. This is a natural human exclamation of joy and surprise: “Oh, what good news! What great fortune!” It’s the excitement of someone who has just stumbled upon something valuable. He calls out: “This is a boy!”

The scout looked around carefully before announcing his discovery. Why? Because child slavery was common in that era, and there were established practices around finding abandoned or lost children. If he could claim this child as found property, there would be profit in it.

The verb وَأَسَرُّوهُ - “they hid him” - is particularly interesting. The ayah only mentioned one scout going to check the well, yet it says “they” hid him. This indicates that when the scout brought Yusuf (AS) back to the caravan, the entire group immediately conspired together: “Quick, hide him! Before his parents show up, before his guardians come looking, before anyone else sees him - hide him!” If others witnessed their find, someone might come forward to claim the child.

They concealed him بِضَـٰعَة - as merchandise, as cargo. Think of a caravan like a truck or train: there’s the passenger section where people travel, and there’s the cargo area where goods for trade are stored. The بِضَـٰعَة is specifically the merchandise you’ve packed to sell at your destination, not the supplies you need for the journey itself. They placed this young boy among the trade goods, treating him as an item to be sold.

The ayah concludes with a powerful reminder: وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌۢ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ - “And Allah ﷻ is Knowing of what they do.” Allah ﷻ witnesses this crime. This isn’t just about the one scout who found Yusuf (AS) - it’s about the entire caravan that participated in this conspiracy to kidnap and hide a child. They will all be held accountable for this action on the Day of Judgment.

Ayah 20

وَشَرَوْهُ بِثَمَنٍۭ بَخْسٍ دَرَٰهِمَ مَعْدُودَةٍ وَكَانُوا۟ فِيهِ مِنَ ٱلزَّٰهِدِينَ

Because they possessed stolen goods - a kidnapped child - the caravan faced a dangerous situation. People might be searching for this boy. Anyone selling stolen merchandise lives in fear of being caught, which is why such items are called “hot merchandise.” Hold onto them too long, and you risk getting burned. The solution? Get rid of them quickly.

This explains why they وَشَرَوْهُ بِثَمَنٍۭ بَخْسٍ - “sold him for a price that was offensively cheap.” The word بَخْس doesn’t just mean inexpensive - it means obscenely low, almost insultingly cheap. دَرَٰهِمَ مَعْدُودَةٍ - just a few dirhams, a handful of coins you could count on your fingers.

Imagine the haggling: “A hundred?” “No, I’ll give you ten.” “Fifty?” “No, five.” “Please, just take fifty.” “Five, final offer.” The buyer keeps lowering his price, and the sellers keep accepting because they’re desperate to unload this dangerous cargo. They just want to be rid of him before they get caught.

Then comes the profound irony in Allah’s ﷻ words: وَكَانُوا۟ فِيهِ مِنَ ٱلزَّٰهِدِينَ - “and they were of those who had no interest in him.” The word زَاهِد typically describes someone spiritually detached from worldly matters, someone who doesn’t care about material gain. But here, Allah ﷻ uses it with divine sarcasm. They appeared so disinterested, so willing to let him go for almost nothing - but not because they were generous or spiritual. They were terrified of getting arrested.

Think of a child who doesn’t care whether you buy him expensive brand-name shoes or whatever is on sale - he’s content with anything. That’s a zahid regarding clothing. But these merchants were “zahids” regarding Yusuf (AS) for entirely different reasons: fear masked as indifference, desperation disguised as disinterest.

Ayah 21

وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِى ٱشْتَرَىٰهُ مِن مِّصْرَ لِٱمْرَأَتِهِۦٓ أَكْرِمِى مَثْوَىٰهُ عَسَىٰٓ أَن يَنفَعَنَآ أَوْ نَتَّخِذَهُۥ وَلَدًا ۚ وَكَذَٰلِكَ مَكَّنَّا لِيُوسُفَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلِنُعَلِّمَهُۥ مِن تَأْوِيلِ ٱلْأَحَادِيثِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ غَالِبٌ عَلَىٰٓ أَمْرِهِۦ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ

The man who purchased Yusuf (AS) in Egypt was a minister, a high-ranking political figure. The ayah tells us something revealing about his household dynamics: this politician was extremely busy, spending little time at home. His wife felt neglected or distant from him. So when he saw this child being sold, he recognized an opportunity - something to occupy his wife’s time and attention, perhaps to ease her loneliness or keep her from other troubles. They had no children of their own, and he thought this might fill that void.

Notice what happens when he brings Yusuf (AS) home. He tells his wife: أَكْرِمِى مَثْوَىٰهُ - “Honor his place of stay.” He doesn’t say “honor him” directly; he says honor his dwelling, his residence. The word مَثْوَىٰ comes from the root meaning to stay for a very long time - so long that you might die there. It implies permanent residence. The Prophet ﷺ used this word when he said the people of Madinah weren’t just visitors; they had lived their entire lives there until death.

The minister instructs his wife to give Yusuf (AS) a noble room, not some servant’s quarters in the back or a shed like where you’d keep animals. When you honor a guest, you provide them with your best accommodations. He continues: عَسَىٰٓ أَن يَنفَعَنَآ - “Perhaps he will benefit us” - and then adds أَوْ نَتَّخِذَهُۥ وَلَدًا - “or we may adopt him as our son.” The child was attractive enough, well-mannered enough, that they considered making him part of their family.

Then Allah ﷻ interjects with divine commentary: وَكَذَٰلِكَ مَكَّنَّا لِيُوسُفَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ - “And thus We established Yusuf in the land.” This is the answer to a question that runs throughout the story: How did the Israelites come to have a presence in Egypt? How did they eventually gain influence and position there? It all began here, with Yusuf (AS) being brought into this household, given status and opportunity.

The wisdom continues: وَلِنُعَلِّمَهُۥ مِن تَأْوِيلِ ٱلْأَحَادِيثِ - “And so that We might teach him the interpretation of narratives.” Remember that تَأْوِيل means to interpret confusing or complex matters, and أَحَادِيث means all kinds of speech and communication - not just dreams, though that’s included.

Living in the minister’s household provided Yusuf (AS) with an extraordinary education. We’ve already seen that this minister knows how to negotiate - he got Yusuf (AS) for an incredibly low price. The minister’s house hosted other politicians, high-ranking officials, people engaged in serious economic, political and social discussions. As Yusuf (AS) went about his duties in the house - perhaps cleaning, serving, performing his responsibilities - he was privy to all these elite conversations.

His father had already recognized his exceptional intelligence. Now, exposed to these high-level discussions, Yusuf (AS) gained deep insight into how the world works, how politics operates, how power functions. It was like receiving an internship at the highest levels of government - learning the inner workings of state affairs while having his room and board provided, all while being honored and well-treated.

But there’s another dimension to this education. The minister’s wife would later reveal her corruption, but this didn’t appear suddenly one day. She had been corrupt all along, entertaining corrupt friends, engaging in corrupt conversations. Yusuf (AS) witnessed this as well. He learned not just about politics and governance, but about human corruption, about how to recognize and deal with morally compromised people. He became street-smart, worldly-wise, gaining social intelligence alongside his spiritual gifts and ability to interpret dreams.

Then comes one of the most powerful statements in the entire Surah: وَٱللَّهُ غَالِبٌ عَلَىٰٓ أَمْرِهِۦ - “And Allah ﷻ is predominant over His affair.” The word غَالِب means to oversee, overpower, dominate. Whether Yusuf (AS) was thrown into a well, pulled out, sold as a slave, or elevated to a position in an elite household - in every single situation, Allah ﷻ remained completely in control of his affairs. Allah ﷻ never abandoned him.

This is crucial for anyone going through difficulty: when tragedy strikes, we might feel as if Allah ﷻ has left us, as if He’s no longer managing our situation. But Allah ﷻ declares that He was dominant over Yusuf’s (AS) affair through every trial and transition. وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ - “But most people do not know.” Most people, when facing problems, don’t recognize that Allah ﷻ maintains complete control. They ask, “Why has Allah ﷻ left me? Why isn’t Allah ﷻ handling this?” They lack this essential knowledge.

Ayah 22

وَلَمَّا بَلَغَ أَشُدَّهُۥٓ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ حُكْمًا وَعِلْمًا ۚ وَكَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ

وَلَمَّا بَلَغَ أَشُدَّهُۥٓ - “And when he reached his mature age” - the word أَشُدّ indicates full physical and mental maturity, the prime of young adulthood. The exact numerical age isn’t specified in the Quran - was he twenty? Thirty-three? Forty? This ambiguity is intentional and meaningful. The precise number doesn’t change anything about the narrative or its lessons. What matters is that he reached the age of maturity and responsibility.

Many scholars suggest this refers to the late teens or mid-twenties, the time when a young man reaches his full strength and capability. The moment this occurred, ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ حُكْمًا وَعِلْمًا - “We gave him wisdom and knowledge.”

Notice something profound about the order here. Allah ﷻ mentions حُكْم before عِلْم - wisdom before knowledge. Normally, we would expect the opposite sequence: first you gain knowledge, then that knowledge enables you to make wise decisions. But with Yusuf (AS), Allah ﷻ gave him the ability to make sound, firm decisions even before extensive knowledge.

This teaches us something essential about surviving youth, particularly for young men: more important than accumulating information is developing حُكْم - the ability to control yourself, to make wise choices, to not be impulsive. حُكْم is defined as making the right decision based on right judgment, exercising sound discretion in the moment.

Allah ﷻ gave Yusuf (AS) this quality first: firmness, self-control, the capacity to not let emotions and impulses dictate his actions. Many people possess tremendous knowledge but make terrible decisions. They understand theories perfectly but act foolishly in practice. Knowledge alone doesn’t guarantee wisdom.

For young people especially - and young men in particular - this is critical: learn to control yourself. Don’t let your hormones run your life. Don’t be impulsive. Develop the ability to pause, reflect and choose wisely. Once you establish that foundation of self-control, then pursue knowledge, and Allah ﷻ will open doors of excellence for you.

This is exactly what happened with Yusuf (AS): he had that control, that حُكْم, and then Allah ﷻ reinforced it with علم, with more and more knowledge.

وَكَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ - “And thus do We reward those who do good.” This isn’t limited to Yusuf (AS). The same pattern is available to anyone: develop self-control and pursue excellence, and Allah ﷻ will reward you with wisdom and knowledge. This is the path of the محسنين - those who excel, those who pursue ihsan in their character and conduct.

Ayah 23

وَرَٰوَدَتْهُ ٱلَّتِى هُوَ فِى بَيْتِهَا عَن نَّفْسِهِۦ وَغَلَّقَتِ ٱلْأَبْوَٰبَ وَقَالَتْ هَيْتَ لَكَ ۚ قَالَ مَعَاذَ ٱللَّهِ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ رَبِّىٓ أَحْسَنَ مَثْوَاىَ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُفْلِحُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ

The word رَٰوَدَتْ is extraordinary in its precision. The root رود means to go back and forth looking for something, wavering in your mind. When this becomes مُرَاوَدَة, it means trying to make someone else adopt your intention, trying to coax someone into thinking like you, especially when they’re resistant to your idea. It’s the attempt to make someone slip away from their own resolve and embrace yours instead.

In the previous ayah, we learned that Yusuf (AS) had حُكْم - firmness in his decisions. Now we see someone actively trying to break that firmness, to make him slip from his conviction.

Notice how Allah ﷻ describes her: ٱلَّتِى هُوَ فِى بَيْتِهَا - “the one in whose house he was.” Allah ﷻ could have simply said “the wife of Al-Aziz” or “the minister’s wife” - that would have been much shorter and simpler. But by using this longer phrase, Allah ﷻ emphasizes something critical about Yusuf’s (AS) situation: he was under her roof, in her domain.

Even more precisely, Allah ﷻ doesn’t say “in the house of Al-Aziz” because while the minister was the head of household, he was rarely home. She managed all the household affairs. She was the one constantly present, the one who gave Yusuf (AS) instructions throughout each day. To ignore her command, to refuse her request, was not simple for him. The power dynamic was entirely in her favor.

She attempted to seduce him, to pull him away عَن نَّفْسِهِۦ - from his own self, from his inner resolve. Deep within himself, he had determined not to be drawn into this sin, but she worked to break down that determination, to make him think as she did.

وَغَلَّقَتِ ٱلْأَبْوَٰبَ - “And she locked the doors.” The form غَلَّقَتْ is intensive - it doesn’t mean she locked one door with one lock. She secured multiple doors with multiple locks: latches, hooks, keys, bolts. Even if he tried to run, he would get stuck at the first door, fumbling with the locks while she closed in on him. She had trapped him completely.

Picture the scene: he tries to flee, and there’s a physical struggle as he attempts to unlatch the doors one by one, and she’s chasing him, grabbing at him. His shirt tears in the struggle. He’s younger, faster, but the locked doors slow him down. As he finally manages to open the doors and escape, she’s running after him, frustrated that she can’t catch him.

وَقَالَتْ هَيْتَ لَكَ - “And she said, ‘Come here!’” According to Ibn Abbas (RA), هَيْتَ indicates yelling, calling out with urgency and authority: “Come here! Get back here!” This wasn’t a gentle invitation - it was a command. Remember, Yusuf (AS) had obeyed her instructions his entire time in that household. This was the authoritative voice he had been conditioned to respond to.

قَالَ مَعَاذَ ٱللَّهِ - “He said, ‘I seek refuge in Allah ﷻ!’” The word مَعَاذ includes both seeking refuge and seeking a place of protection - “Give me a place with You where I can hide, take me away from here!” He’s not just seeking abstract protection; he’s begging to be physically removed from this situation.

إِنَّهُۥ رَبِّىٓ أَحْسَنَ مَثْوَاىَ - “Indeed, he is my master; he has made good my residence.” This is interpreted in two ways. Some scholars say he’s referring to Allah ﷻ as his Rabb. Others say he’s referring to the minister, her husband, as his master - and at that time in history, there was nothing wrong with calling one’s master رَبّ. It wasn’t yet prohibited. The Prophet ﷺ later specifically instructed that slaves should not be called عَبْد (slave) and masters should not be called رَبّ (lord), as preventative measures against associations that could lead to shirk. But in Yusuf’s (AS) time, these terms were still in use.

The second interpretation seems more immediate to the context: “Your husband - my master - went out of his way to honor me, to give me good accommodations.” Remember the very first day Yusuf (AS) arrived, when the minister told his wife: أَكْرِمِى مَثْوَىٰهُ - “Honor his place of stay.” The minister didn’t repeat these instructions every day, but Yusuf (AS) never forgot them. Years later, in this moment of crisis, he remembers that kindness, that initial generosity.

But he’s not just reminding himself - he’s trying to reach her conscience: “Look, I am so grateful to him for giving me a room. He’s given you this entire house, this life of luxury and status. How can you be so disloyal to him?”

إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُفْلِحُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ - “Indeed, wrongdoers will not succeed.” The word ظَالِمُون - wrongdoers - is significant here. Obviously, the act being proposed is فَاحِشَة, an outrageous shameless sin, زِنَا (zina). But Yusuf (AS) describes it as ظُلْم - wrong, injustice, betrayal.

Later in the surah, when Yusuf (AS) is in prison, he will describe the people he left behind using this same word: their ظُلْم. This reveals something profound: people who immerse themselves in such acts aren’t just being disloyal to a spouse. They’re being disloyal to Allah ﷻ. This is an act of profound disregard for Allah ﷻ, a betrayal of trust at the highest level. The ظُلْم isn’t just against the minister - it’s against Allah ﷻ Himself, against the sacred boundaries He has established.

Ayah 23–29 · linked video: 06. Yusuf (Ayah 23-29)

Surah Yusuf: Ayahs 23-29

Ayah 23

وَرَٰوَدَتْهُ ٱلَّتِى هُوَ فِى بَيْتِهَا عَن نَّفْسِهِۦ وَغَلَّقَتِ ٱلْأَبْوَٰبَ وَقَالَتْ هَيْتَ لَكَ ۚ قَالَ مَعَاذَ ٱللَّهِ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ رَبِّىٓ أَحْسَنَ مَثْوَاىَ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُفْلِحُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ

Before we explore this ayah, we need to understand the reality of the situation Yusuf (AS) faced. Here was a young, unmarried man in the prime of his life. He was extraordinarily handsome. Both parties were willing. Every opportunity was present, every motive aligned. He was, after all, human—a man with natural inclinations toward marriage and attraction to the opposite gender, as we’ll learn in the next ayah. This is part of human nature that no man lacks.

The perfect storm had assembled itself. She had every reason to keep this secret because protecting her reputation meant he would likely never tell. All the circumstances pointed in one direction, yet despite all of this, he fought against it and ran. What does this teach us?

Fast forward to our time. Consider a young man away from home, on campus, living independently. His parents are away. Nobody will know if he goes to that party. He knows how to delete text messages, clear his computer’s cache every hour. Nobody will find out. What’s the only thing that can stop such a person? The ability to make a firm decision and remember that you’re not just wronging your parents or yourself or the other person—you’re wronging Allah ﷻ. That consciousness alone can stop you. Without that inner decency, without that awareness, these emotions and desires become too strong, too overwhelming for a young person to resist on their own.

But let’s examine the other side of this situation. Why was she so attracted to Yusuf (AS)? Was his good looks enough? No. He was kind, courteous, respectful, and intelligent. He was always present when needed. Her husband, by contrast, was never there. She was desperate, lonely, dealing with emotional struggles. She had convinced herself that her husband didn’t love her. And Yusuf (AS)—whenever she asked him to sit and listen, he would come and listen attentively. He understood her in ways her husband never did.

An emotional void needed to be filled. This conversation is particularly important for those of us who have daughters. As girls grow up, they develop emotional voids that need attention. They need to feel like they belong. This is equally true of our wives and mothers—they all need that sense of belonging. When we don’t provide that, twisted things happen. What we see in this ayah is one manifestation of such neglect, but it’s not the only one.

Angry outbursts can occur. Women can become emotionally volatile. All kinds of destructive patterns emerge when we ignore the women in our families. The time and attention that needs to be given to wives, daughters, and mothers—these are critical necessities. When we fail in this, we can really damage them. You wonder why your mother becomes upset every time you call? She’s not getting any time from you, any attention. When someone doesn’t receive attention, they first become emotionally scarred and don’t know how to process it. But given enough opportunity, that emotional wound can translate into harmful actions.

This is actually the same dynamic we saw with Yusuf’s brothers, isn’t it? They felt they weren’t getting attention. Look how dangerous feeling ignored can be. Consider how many young people in America have taken up weapons and attacked their schools—and when we investigate, we discover their parents never paid them any attention. They just played video games all day at home. This is lethal. This is serious.

Giving proper time and attention to our family lives is essential. I don’t claim to have the complete solution, but I can offer one piece of it: turn your phone off when you’re home. No smartphone, nothing. Just human communication. No screen-mediated interaction—real, face-to-face connection.

Ayah 24

وَلَقَدْ هَمَّتْ بِهِۦ ۖ وَهَمَّ بِهَا لَوْلَآ أَن رَّءَا بُرْهَـٰنَ رَبِّهِۦ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ لِنَصْرِفَ عَنْهُ ٱلسُّوٓءَ وَٱلْفَحْشَآءَ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ مِنْ عِبَادِنَا ٱلْمُخْلَصِينَ

Perhaps she truly desired him. The question becomes: did he desire her as well? Some interpret the phrase وَهَمَّ بِهَا لَوْلَآ to mean “and he would have desired her had he not seen the proof of his master.” They read it as: “She desired him, and he would have desired her except that he saw the proof of his master.”

The linguistic challenge with this interpretation is that when you use لَوْلَا, the response typically requires a specific grammatical structure. If this were the intended meaning, we would expect different wording. Some classical scholars like Imam As-Suyuti interpreted it this way out of deep respect for Yusuf (AS), but the actual structure of the language suggests a different understanding.

First, let’s understand the word هَمَّ. Hamma means desire or inclination, and linguistically it has been understood at multiple levels. It can indicate a want that you’re determined to fulfill, or it can represent a desire that you haven’t yet acted upon.

She had a desire and she acted on it—she fulfilled that hamma. Imam Ash-Shafi’i describes the hamma of Yusuf (AS) as the natural desire any man would experience when seeing an attractive woman, but he controlled himself and lowered his gaze. Simply finding someone attractive is not itself a sin. Having that hamma is not the crime. The crime is acting upon it. That’s where the line is crossed.

He compared it to fasting: feeling thirsty while fasting is not a sin—that’s the natural appetite Allah ﷻ placed within human beings. Yusuf (AS) experienced that natural human desire, but he knew how to control it because Allah ﷻ had given him what? حُكْم—firmness, wisdom, the ability to judge and restrain himself.

What we learn here is profound: no matter how spiritually elevated you become, you don’t kill your hormones. You’re not suddenly above temptation. You remain human. The ayah uses لَوْلَآ أَن رَّءَا بُرْهَـٰنَ رَبِّهِۦ—“had he not seen the clear proof of his master.” The structure itself honors Yusuf (AS) because Allah ﷻ doesn’t even complete the thought of what would have happened. The consequence—that he might have failed—is understood but left unsaid. That’s the honor Allah ﷻ grants Yusuf (AS) in this ayah.

What was this برهان, this clear proof? Scholars have offered various explanations. Some say he saw the face of Ya’qub (AS). Others say revelation came to him, or that the strong character instilled in him manifested at that moment. One beautiful interpretation suggests that as he ran toward the door, all the locks opened on their own, as if Allah ﷻ was showing him: “I want you to run.”

Imam Al-Qurtubi, after collecting all these opinions, wisely concluded that we don’t need to go beyond what the Quran states. We can simply say that Allah ﷻ showed him something that absolutely convinced him, and we should be content with that understanding. Allah ﷻ showed him something or placed something in his heart that left no doubt in his mind that this could never be a good idea.

Now observe the next phrase: كَذَٰلِكَ لِنَصْرِفَ عَنْهُ ٱلسُّوٓءَ وَٱلْفَحْشَآءَ. “That is how We removed from him evil and shamelessness.”

This ayah presents something unexpected. We would anticipate that in this context, الْفَحْشَآءَ (shamelessness, lewdness) would be mentioned first, as that’s what the situation directly involves. Yet Allah ﷻ mentions السُّوٓءَ (evil) first. Why?

Before addressing that, notice the beautiful precision in Allah’s words. He says He removed evil from him—not that He removed him from evil. This is significant. The phrasing suggests that evil was trying to cling to him like a parasite, and Allah ﷻ removed that parasite. If Allah ﷻ had said “We removed him from evil,” it would imply Yusuf (AS) was somehow holding onto evil, which would discredit his character. By reversing it, Allah ﷻ makes clear that the criminal here is not Yusuf (AS)—it’s the evil itself.

Now, why does Allah ﷻ mention السُّوٓءَ first? السُّوٓءَ encompasses all forms of evil—every type of wrongdoing, every bad deed. What we’re learning is that Yusuf (AS) had been a man of exemplary character throughout his entire life. Allah ﷻ had kept evil away from him consistently. When the ugliest, most powerful, most tempting form of evil appeared before him, he had already been exercising the muscle of resisting evil for so long that he was able to withstand even this.
