He had built that spiritual strength because he guarded himself against all other sins. He never fell into those other transgressions. Allah ﷻ had been removing sins from him throughout his life. What this teaches us is profound: some sins open the door to other sins, while protecting yourself from certain sins closes the door to others. If backbiting runs rampant in your life, there’s no guarantee you won’t fall into lying and other crimes. Allah ﷻ first protected Yusuf (AS) from all kinds of evil, and then when the ultimate test arrived, Allah ﷻ protected him even from الْفَحْشَآءَ.

The ayah concludes: إِنَّهُۥ مِنْ عِبَادِنَا ٱلْمُخْلَصِينَ—“Indeed, he was from Our slaves who have been made sincere.”

Notice the word: not مُخْلِص (one who is sincere), but مُخْلَص (one who has been granted sincerity). This is passive, indicating that Allah ﷻ bestowed this quality upon him. Throughout this surah, Allah ﷻ repeatedly takes credit for Yusuf’s character, acknowledging how his ability to hold firm earned him the divine gift of consistent sincerity.

Ayah 25

وَٱسْتَبَقَا ٱلْبَابَ وَقَدَّتْ قَمِيصَهُۥ مِن دُبُرٍ وَأَلْفَيَا سَيِّدَهَا لَدَا ٱلْبَابِ ۚ قَالَتْ مَا جَزَآءُ مَنْ أَرَادَ بِأَهْلِكَ سُوٓءًا إِلَّآ أَن يُسْجَنَ أَوْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ

وَٱسْتَبَقَا ٱلْبَابَ—“They both raced toward the door.” The last time there was a race in Yusuf’s life, he got into serious trouble with his brothers. Now another race unfolds, but this time with entirely different stakes.

Before we proceed, let’s revisit something important from the previous ayah. When describing her desire for him, Allah ﷻ could have combined the two verbs and said هَمَّا بِهَا as a single construction. Instead, He separated them: هَمَّتْ بِهِۦ ۖ وَهَمَّ بِهَا. Why this separation?

Because you cannot equate their emotional states. Her desire had fully turned toward evil—it was overwhelming, all-consuming. His response, by contrast, was merely the natural human reaction: she’s a woman, she’s beautiful, I need to run, I won’t look. These are not comparable emotional conditions, so Allah ﷻ doesn’t even place them in the same grammatical structure. He separates the two completely.

Yet when it comes to racing toward the door, He joins them: وَٱسْتَبَقَا (they raced together). They’re running in opposite emotional states but toward the same door.

وَقَدَّتْ قَمِيصَهُۥ مِن دُبُرٍ—“And she ripped his shirt from behind.”

وَأَلْفَيَا سَيِّدَهَا لَدَا ٱلْبَابِ—“And they stumbled upon her master right at the door.”

The word أَلْفَيَا is significant. When you find something you’re looking for, you use وَجَدَ . But أَلْفَيَا means to stumble upon something unexpectedly, to find something you weren’t even looking for. They had no idea he would be standing right there. Yusuf (AS) finally manages to unlock all the latches and fling open the door, and she has just finished ripping his shirt. Who does the husband see first? Yusuf (AS).

Imagine the scene: the husband sees Yusuf (AS) first, and he already understands that Yusuf (AS) is trying to escape. But this creates confusion—perhaps Yusuf (AS) did something wrong and is now fleeing. The situation remains unclear.

قَالَتْ—“She spoke.” She speaks immediately. Notice the cunning intelligence of this woman, as morally corrupt as she is.

مَا جَزَآءُ مَنْ أَرَادَ بِأَهْلِكَ سُوٓءًا—“What should be the reward of one who intended evil for your family?”

إِلَّآ أَن يُسْجَنَ أَوْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ—“Except that he be imprisoned or given painful punishment?”

She’s offering alternatives like multiple choice: prison or painful torture. What do you think he deserves? She’s turned the entire situation on its head before anyone else can speak.

Had she not opened her mouth, Yusuf (AS) likely would not have said anything to humiliate her. Out of respect and maintaining proper boundaries, he probably would have remained silent. But now that she’s coming after him directly, accusing him publicly, he has no choice but to defend himself.

Ayah 26

قَالَ هِىَ رَٰوَدَتْنِى عَن نَّفْسِى ۚ وَشَهِدَ شَاهِدٌ مِّنْ أَهْلِهَآ إِن كَانَ قَمِيصُهُۥ قُدَّ مِن قُبُلٍ فَصَدَقَتْ وَهُوَ مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰذِبِينَ

قَالَ هِىَ رَٰوَدَتْنِى عَن نَّفْسِى—“He said: She is the one who tried to seduce me.”

Simple, direct, truthful. No elaboration, no drama—just the facts.

وَشَهِدَ شَاهِدٌ مِّنْ أَهْلِهَآ—“And a witness from her family testified.”

Who was this witness? There are numerous scholarly opinions. Some narrations suggest—and this is quite remarkable—that it was actually a baby in a crib who spoke. If this is true, imagine the scene: everyone present would have been absolutely stunned. Did that baby just speak? What just happened?

Others suggest it was a servant or another family member. The identity isn’t definitively established in the Quran, so we have various possibilities. What matters is what this witness said:

إِن كَانَ قَمِيصُهُۥ قُدَّ مِن قُبُلٍ فَصَدَقَتْ وَهُوَ مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰذِبِينَ—“If his shirt is torn from the front, then she has spoken the truth and he is from the liars.”

The logic is simple and brilliant: if the shirt is torn from the front, it means he was approaching her and she tore it defending herself. In that case, she’s telling the truth and he’s lying.

Ayah 27

وَإِن كَانَ قَمِيصُهُۥ قُدَّ مِن دُبُرٍ فَكَذَبَتْ وَهُوَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ

وَإِن كَانَ قَمِيصُهُۥ قُدَّ مِن دُبُرٍ فَكَذَبَتْ وَهُوَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ—“And if his shirt is torn from the back, then she has lied and he is from the truthful.”

The counter-logic: if the shirt is torn from behind, it means he was running away and she grabbed him from behind. Therefore, she’s the liar and he’s telling the truth.

Ayah 28

فَلَمَّا رَءَا قَمِيصَهُۥ قُدَّ مِن دُبُرٍ قَالَ إِنَّهُۥ مِن كَيْدِكُنَّ ۖ إِنَّ كَيْدَكُنَّ عَظِيمٌ

فَلَمَّا رَءَا قَمِيصَهُۥ قُدَّ مِن دُبُرٍ—“So when he saw his shirt torn from the back…”

Mystery solved immediately. The evidence is clear and indisputable.

قَالَ إِنَّهُۥ مِن كَيْدِكُنَّ ۖ إِنَّ كَيْدَكُنَّ عَظِيمٌ —“He said: Indeed, this is from the plotting of you women. Indeed, your plotting is tremendous.”

This response is fascinating. He doesn’t directly blame his wife. Instead, he generalizes: “You women.” The old English translation captures this perfectly, though it sounds almost comical: “This is from you women. You women really know how to scheme.”

What’s he doing? He’s deflecting direct blame away from her. He shifts it to all women: “This is just how women are.” Any other husband would have exploded in rage. He’s not particularly disturbed. Why not?

Because he’s a politician, and politicians usually have questionable character themselves. He likely has far too many skeletons in his own closet. He can’t really come after his wife with full moral authority—and who knows more about his secrets than anyone else? His wife does. He’s not going to attack her directly. So instead: “Yeah, you women. What you people do is really messed up.”

This reveals something disturbing about the worst kind of family dysfunction—promiscuity and infidelity between spouses. The most severe instances of this occur in the highest political circles, and often both parties are fine with it. They don’t mind. They possess such compromised character that they overlook each other’s transgressions.

The only thing he’s worried about is a scandal. He’s not concerned about his wife’s morality or Yusuf’s wellbeing or the sanctity of his home. All he cares about is whether a scandal might emerge. If Yusuf (AS) is arrested right now and thrown in prison, he might talk. He might say something. And that wouldn’t be good for anyone—meaning it wouldn’t be good for the minister’s career.

Ayah 29

يُوسُفُ أَعْرِضْ عَنْ هَـٰذَا ۚ وَٱسْتَغْفِرِى لِذَنۢبِكِ ۖ إِنَّكِ كُنتِ مِنَ ٱلْخَاطِـِٔينَ

يُوسُفُ أَعْرِضْ عَنْ هَـٰذَا—“Yusuf, turn away from this.”

Just forget this ever happened. Pretend it never occurred. You’re a good kid. I like you. He’s being the ultimate politician here—this man would have made an excellent presidential candidate if he were alive today. His priority is burying the scandal.

وَٱسْتَغْفِرِى لِذَنۢبِكِ ۖ إِنَّكِ كُنتِ مِنَ ٱلْخَاطِـِٔينَ—“And you, ask forgiveness for your sin. Indeed, you have been of the wrongdoers.”

Just seek forgiveness and you’ll be fine. That’s his solution. Make istighfar and we’re good.

But here’s the critical question: does this scandal stay buried? No. And at whose hands does it get out?

وَشَهِدَ شَاهِدٌ مِّنْ أَهْلِهَآ—remember, a witness from among her family saw what happened. If this witness was among the family, were there other servants who probably witnessed this too?

Imagine the conversations among the household staff: "Hey, you know what the boss was saying today about women? He said إِنَّ كَيْدَكُنَّ عَظِيمٌ.

“Why would he say that?”

“You don’t know what happened? The lady went crazy. She ripped Yusuf’s shirt.”

“She what? His shirt? No way!”

“Hold on, let me call my friend…”

Next thing you know, all of Egypt knows. This is the second story in the Quran where a scandal is supposed to remain hidden but spreads anyway. The first was Musa (AS) and the humiliation Fir’awn suffered at his hands within his own palace—that was supposed to stay secret, but word got out.

This scandal won’t just damage her reputation—it will destroy the minister’s reputation as well. And here’s something else we need to understand about elite society: elite women often despise each other. There’s fierce competition among them. They want to see their rivals crash and burn. First ladies can’t stand each other.

So the best way to see another elite political woman destroyed is to watch her husband’s career implode, taking her rival down with it. Their interest in spreading this scandal is deeply political. If his career goes down in flames, their rival who’s currently on top will fall as well.

That’s why when these women later spread the word throughout Egypt, Allah ﷻ describes it as كَيْد (plotting, scheming). It has profound political ramifications. These promiscuity scandals—when they happen today involving governors, politicians, high officials—always carry further political consequences. And notice how they always seem to emerge right before an election cycle. These behaviors are as common among political elites as breathing, but they only become morally deplorable and publicly unacceptable when there are political ramifications nearby. Then suddenly they become scandalous.

So the minister tells Yusuf (AS): just let it go, don’t worry about it. And to his wife: make istighfar and you’ll be fine. Case closed—or so he thinks.

Ayah 29–36 · linked video: 07. Yusuf (Ayah 29-36)

Surah Yusuf: Ayahs 29-36

Ayah 29

يُوسُفُ أَعْرِضْ عَنْ هَـٰذَا ۚ وَٱسْتَغْفِرِى لِذَنۢبِكِ ۖ إِنَّكِ كُنتِ مِنَ ٱلْخَاطِـِٔينَ

The minister’s response reveals the careful navigation required in this politically charged situation. He addresses Yusuf (AS) first: “Yusuf, turn away from this.” His instruction to ignore the incident entirely—to pretend it never happened—reflects the political reality they’re facing. Then he turns to his wife with measured words: “Ask forgiveness for your sin.”

The choice of words here is significant. The term ذنب refers to something embarrassing, something one should be ashamed of. The minister continues with إِنَّكِ كُنتِ مِنَ ٱلْخَاطِـِٔينَ—“you are from those who made mistakes.” The word خَاطِئ is the opposite of doing something intentionally. It describes an error, not a calculated evil.

Even at this moment, the minister cannot afford to offend his wife too severely. He doesn’t call her inherently corrupt or evil. He frames her behavior as a mistake, something that happens. This is the nature of politics—even marriage becomes political. He must play this delicate game because she holds power to damage his career and reputation through her testimony. His words are chosen to resolve the crisis without creating a greater one.

Ayah 30

۞ وَقَالَ نِسْوَةٌ فِى ٱلْمَدِينَةِ ٱمْرَأَتُ ٱلْعَزِيزِ تُرَٰوِدُ فَتَىٰهَا عَن نَّفْسِهِۦ ۖ قَدْ شَغَفَهَا حُبًّا ۖ إِنَّا لَنَرَىٰهَا فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ

The scandal spreads through the city, but notice the careful choice of vocabulary. Allah ﷻ uses قَالَ—the masculine form of “said”—rather than the expected feminine قَالَتْ, even though نِسْوَةٌ refers to women. This serves two rhetorical purposes. First, grammatically, it’s permitted because niswah is a broken plural, which allows either masculine or feminine conjugation. Second, the choice carries meaning: the masculine form suggests a smaller, more specific group. Some scholars note it’s also a form of belittling—these women weren’t behaving in a ladylike manner, so they’re not granted the feminine verb.

The word نِسْوَة itself indicates a special group of women who share something in common—in this case, their social status and their united agenda to bring down the minister’s wife. These are the wives of other political figures, women of influence who now have ammunition.

“The wife of the minister is trying to seduce her young slave,” they whisper. The term تُرَاوِدُ means she’s attempting to seduce him, to get him to slip from his character. Then comes a remarkable word: شَغَفَهَا—“it has pierced her heart.” The root شَغَاف refers to the inner lining of the heart, and شَغَف describes a love so intense it hurts deep inside. This isn’t ordinary affection; this is love that causes pain, a heart aching with desire.

The women say this with mock sympathy: “Oh, poor woman, her heart is aching for him. She’s so crazy about him.” But their words drip with sarcasm. إِنَّا لَنَرَىٰهَا فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ—“We clearly see her in manifest error.” The chatter spreads through the city’s elite circles, these conversations happening everywhere, making a mockery of the minister’s household.

Ayah 31

فَلَمَّا سَمِعَتْ بِمَكْرِهِنَّ أَرْسَلَتْ إِلَيْهِنَّ وَأَعْتَدَتْ لَهُنَّ مُتَّكَـًٔا وَءَاتَتْ كُلَّ وَٰحِدَةٍ مِّنْهُنَّ سِكِّينًا وَقَالَتِ ٱخْرُجْ عَلَيْهِنَّ ۖ فَلَمَّا رَأَيْنَهُۥٓ أَكْبَرْنَهُۥ وَقَطَّعْنَ أَيْدِيَهُنَّ وَقُلْنَ حَـٰشَ لِلَّهِ مَا هَـٰذَا بَشَرًا إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا مَلَكٌ كَرِيمٌ

When the minister’s wife heard about their مَكْر—their plotting—she understood what was happening. The word makr is significant: it refers to a covert plan designed to harm someone. It necessarily involves secrecy and the intent to damage an enemy. Allah ﷻ is revealing that beneath the surface gossip lies a deeper agenda. These women aren’t just chatting; they’re working to undermine her family’s political standing, to take down her husband’s position.

Her response is calculated and brilliant. أَرْسَلَتْ إِلَيْهِنَّ—she sent for them, inviting them to her home. وَأَعْتَدَتْ لَهُنَّ مُتَّكَـًٔا—she prepared for them a place to recline. The word مُتَّكَأ comes from the root meaning to lean back comfortably. She didn’t just clear out a room; she completely rearranged the space, moved furniture, created a setup where each woman would have a perfect, unobstructed view. She’s staging something.

وَءَاتَتْ كُلَّ وَٰحِدَةٍ مِّنْهُنَّ سِكِّينًا—and she gave each one of them a knife. The ayah emphasizes كُلَّ وَٰحِدَةٍ—every single one. She personally handed out the knives, going to each woman: “Here’s one for you, and one for you, and one for you.” In her mind, perhaps she’s imagining slitting their gossiping throats. She despises them. But she maintains her composure, placing fruit and other items before them that would require cutting.

Then, as if she suddenly remembered something, she steps away and calls: “Yusuf, come here for a moment.” The moment hasn’t even emerged yet, but the women hear his name. Their eyes immediately fix on the door. They’re not watching what they’re cutting anymore; they’re watching for him.

فَلَمَّا رَأَيْنَهُۥٓ أَكْبَرْنَهُۥ—when they saw him, they magnified him. The word أَكْبَرْنَ means to declare something great, but it can be done with the tongue or with overwhelming emotion. They didn’t need to say anything; their faces showed everything. They were stunned, overwhelmed by his appearance.

وَقَطَّعْنَ أَيْدِيَهُنَّ—and they cut their hands. The verb قَطَّعْنَ is in a form that suggests continuous action. Some scholars paint the scene this way: one woman is cutting fruit, glances at Yusuf (AS), and in her distraction cuts her hand. He notices and moves to help her, perhaps bringing a cloth to stop the bleeding. Another woman sees this interaction and thinks, “If I cut my hand, he’ll come help me too.” So she deliberately cuts herself. Then another does the same, and another. The scene becomes surreal—Yusuf (AS) moving from woman to woman, tending to their self-inflicted wounds, while they continue cutting themselves to gain his attention.

حَـٰشَ لِلَّهِ—they begin exclaiming, an expression of astonishment, something like “Good Lord!” or “Oh my goodness!” مَا هَـٰذَا بَشَرًا—“This is not a human being!” إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا مَلَكٌ كَرِيمٌ—“This is nothing but a noble angel!” They’re completely losing their composure, making these declarations, perhaps reaching out toward him. The minister’s wife watches this entire performance unfold exactly as she planned.

Ayah 32

قَالَتْ فَذَٰلِكُنَّ ٱلَّذِى لُمْتُنَّنِى فِيهِ ۖ وَلَقَدْ رَٰوَدتُّهُۥ عَن نَّفْسِهِۦ فَٱسْتَعْصَمَ ۖ وَلَئِن لَّمْ يَفْعَلْ مَآ ءَامُرُهُۥ لَيُسْجَنَنَّ وَلَيَكُونًا مِّنَ ٱلصَّـٰغِرِينَ

Now she speaks, her voice cutting through their dazed exclamations. فَذَٰلِكُنَّ ٱلَّذِى لُمْتُنَّنِى فِيهِ—“So that, my dear ladies, is the one you blamed me about.” The feminine emphasis in ذَٰلِكُنَّ and لُمْتُنَّنِى underscores her point: you women were gossiping about me, judging me, questioning how I could have feelings for a servant. Well, look at yourselves now.

The demonstration has worked perfectly. She’s vindicated, and now she becomes bold, shameless even. وَلَقَدْ رَٰوَدتُّهُۥ عَن نَّفْسِهِۦ—“And I certainly did try to seduce him.” She admits it openly, no longer hiding behind pretense. فَٱسْتَعْصَمَ—“But he held firm, he held on for dear life.” The word اسْتَعْصَمَ comes from عِصْمَة, meaning to hold onto something tightly, to struggle against falling. She could see him fighting against her advances, desperately trying to maintain his integrity.

Then comes the threat, and it’s chilling in its directness: وَلَئِن لَّمْ يَفْعَلْ مَآ ءَامُرُهُۥ—“And if he doesn’t do what I command him”—notice she’s not even pretending anymore that this is about mutual affection or that she needs to think about whether it’s right or wrong. She’s stripped away all pretense: “Just do what I say without thinking about it”—لَيُسْجَنَنَّ—“he will definitely be imprisoned.”

She had already suggested prison to her husband as punishment. Now she’s doubling down, and she adds: وَلَيَكُونًا مِّنَ ٱلصَّـٰغِرِينَ—“and he will be among the humiliated.” The word الصَّـٰغِرِينَ is particularly pointed because she herself has been humiliated by his rejection and by the gossip. Now she promises to transfer that humiliation onto him. It’s a threat made publicly, in front of witnesses, with all her authority as the minister’s wife behind it.

Ayah 33

قَالَ رَبِّ ٱلسِّجْنُ أَحَبُّ إِلَىَّ مِمَّا يَدْعُونَنِىٓ إِلَيْهِ ۖ وَإِلَّا تَصْرِفْ عَنِّى كَيْدَهُنَّ أَصْبُ إِلَيْهِنَّ وَأَكُن مِّنَ ٱلْجَـٰهِلِينَ

Yusuf’s (AS) response reveals the depth of his connection with Allah ﷻ. قَالَ رَبِّ—“He said, ‘My Master.’” He doesn’t talk about Allah ﷻ; he talks directly to Him, as though Allah ﷻ is present in the room, witnessing everything. This is the essence of إحسان—to worship Allah ﷻ as though you see Him. In the middle of this den of temptation, surrounded by powerful women who desire him, with threats of imprisonment hanging over him, Yusuf (AS) turns in conversation to his Lord.

ٱلسِّجْنُ أَحَبُّ إِلَىَّ مِمَّا يَدْعُونَنِىٓ إِلَيْهِ—“Prison is more beloved to me than what they call me toward.” He uses the plural يَدْعُونَنِى—“they call me”—because it’s not just the minister’s wife anymore. Now all these women, emboldened by their gathering, are encouraging him, asking what’s wrong with him, why he won’t comply when she clearly wants it. The pressure is coming from every direction.

His choice is stark and clear: he prefers the physical confinement of prison over the spiritual imprisonment of sin. But then he makes a profound acknowledgment of his own human weakness: وَإِلَّا تَصْرِفْ عَنِّى كَيْدَهُنَّ—“And if You do not turn away from me their plot.” Notice the phrasing: he doesn’t ask Allah ﷻ to remove the women or change them. He asks Allah ﷻ to remove him from their plotting, recognizing that the solution isn’t to change external circumstances but to protect his own heart.

أَصْبُ إِلَيْهِنَّ—“I might incline toward them.” The word أَصْبُ comes from صَبَا, meaning to incline, and it’s related to صَبِيّ—a child. He’s saying, “I will become childish, immature in my inclination toward them.” What defines childish behavior? It’s acting without thinking of consequences. A child touches a hot stove, sticks a hand in a closing door, puts something dangerous in their mouth because they don’t consider what will happen next. They see something and react impulsively.

Yusuf (AS) is making a stunning admission: “Without Your protection, I will stop thinking about consequences. I’ll become completely impulsive and just give in.” This isn’t false humility; this is profound self-awareness. Despite his righteousness, despite his strong character demonstrated throughout this ordeal, he recognizes that he is still human, still vulnerable, still in need of Allah’s divine protection.

وَأَكُن مِّنَ ٱلْجَـٰهِلِينَ—“And I will be among the ignorant.” الْجَـٰهِل refers to someone ruled by their emotions rather than their intellect, someone who cannot control their impulses. The opposite is العَاقِل—the one who uses their intellect to govern their emotions. Yusuf (AS) is saying that without Allah’s help, his emotions will overcome his reason, and he’ll be counted among those who are emotionally unstable, who act on feeling rather than wisdom.

Ayah 34

فَٱسْتَجَابَ لَهُۥ رَبُّهُۥ فَصَرَفَ عَنْهُ كَيْدَهُنَّ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ

فَٱسْتَجَابَ لَهُۥ رَبُّهُۥ—His Master responded to him. The word اسْتَجَابَ is important to understand. Arabic distinguishes between إِجَابَة—an immediate response—and اسْتِجَابَة—a response that takes time. Allah ﷻ did answer Yusuf’s (AS) prayer, but not instantaneously. He wasn’t immediately transported out of that room or magically shielded. The resolution would unfold over time.

Some scholars note that we must be careful in how we phrase our prayers, because Allah ﷻ is always listening. Yusuf (AS) said, “Prison is more beloved to me,” and Allah ﷻ responded to that prayer—which meant he would indeed go to prison. Had he only asked to be removed from their plot without mentioning prison, perhaps the outcome would have been different. This teaches us the weight of our words when speaking to Allah ﷻ.

فَصَرَفَ عَنْهُ كَيْدَهُنَّ—He turned away from him their plotting. Allah ﷻ protected Yusuf (AS) from falling into sin, gave him the strength to navigate that impossible social situation, and provided him a way to escape their pressure. إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ—Indeed, He is the All-Hearing, the All-Knowing. He heard every word of Yusuf’s prayer in that moment. He knew the sincerity of his heart, the depth of his struggle, and the genuine nature of his fear of falling.

Ayah 35

ثُمَّ بَدَا لَهُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ مَا رَأَوُا۟ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لَيَسْجُنُنَّهُۥ حَتَّىٰ حِينٍ

Now the scene shifts, and the pronoun changes. Throughout the previous passages, we’ve been discussing هُنَّ—the women, feminine plural pronouns. Suddenly we have لَهُم—masculine plural. Who are “they”? The political leaders, the husbands of these women.

These men return home to find their wives with bandaged hands. They hear what happened. Word spreads among the political elite. Now these politicians—whose careers are all at stake because of their wives’ behavior—must gather to manage the crisis. They need damage control, and they need it quickly.

ثُمَّ بَدَا لَهُم—“Then it appeared to them,” it seemed the right course of action. مِّنۢ بَعْدِ مَا رَأَوُا۟ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ—“after they had seen all the signs.” The signs include the physical evidence on their wives, the implications of what happened, the potential for scandal if this story spreads further. They consider every angle, every political ramification.

Their conclusion: لَيَسْجُنُنَّهُۥ حَتَّىٰ حِينٍ—“They will definitely imprison him until a given time.” This isn’t just the minister acting alone anymore. It’s not just his wife’s influence. This is a collective decision by the political establishment. They’ve calculated that the only way to suppress the scandal is to make Yusuf (AS) unavailable for testimony, unable to speak to anyone. If he talks, the story gets out. If he’s visible, people will ask questions.

So they decide on imprisonment without trial, an indefinite detention to keep him silent حَتَّىٰ حِينٍ—until the situation cools down, until people forget, until enough time passes. They tell themselves it’s temporary, just until حِين—a particular time. They’re not completely heartless; they feel some guilt about imprisoning an innocent man. They rationalize it: “It’s just for a while. Once things settle, we’ll release him.”

But here’s the reality of political power: once something is off their desk, once it’s out of sight, they forget. They move on to the next crisis, the next issue demanding their attention. What significance does one imprisoned slave have when you’re managing an empire? They forget their promise of حَتَّىٰ حِينٍ. Life continues, and Yusuf (AS) remains behind bars, abandoned by those who imprisoned him, just as he was abandoned by his brothers who threw him in the well and told themselves a caravan would find him. The pattern repeats.

Ayah 36

وَدَخَلَ مَعَهُ ٱلسِّجْنَ فَتَيَانِ ۖ قَالَ أَحَدُهُمَآ إِنِّىٓ أَرَىٰنِىٓ أَعْصِرُ خَمْرًا ۖ وَقَالَ ٱلْـَٔاخَرُ إِنِّىٓ أَرَىٰنِىٓ أَحْمِلُ فَوْقَ رَأْسِى خُبْزًا تَأْكُلُ ٱلطَّيْرُ مِنْهُ ۖ نَبِّئْنَا بِتَأْوِيلِهِۦٓ ۖ إِنَّا نَرَىٰكَ مِنَ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ

The scene now shifts to prison itself. وَدَخَلَ مَعَهُ ٱلسِّجْنَ فَتَيَانِ—Two young men entered prison with him. The phrase مَعَهُ—“with him”—is significant. They’re not inmates who were already there when Yusuf (AS) arrived. They’re new prisoners, entering at the same time or shortly after. This means they had opportunity to observe him during the journey to prison or in the early days of imprisonment.

Prison is filled with all kinds of criminals, people with hardened hearts and corrupt character. These two young men are criminals themselves—that’s why they’re being imprisoned. They know what criminal behavior looks like; they recognize the personality types that populate prisons. And immediately, they can tell Yusuf (AS) doesn’t fit. Everything about him—his demeanor, his behavior, his character—stands in stark contrast to everyone else there.

So they approach him, but notice the progression. They don’t immediately ask him about dreams. First, they must have observed him, gotten to know him somewhat, recognized his unusual intelligence and wisdom. Only then does the conversation move to their dreams.

قَالَ أَحَدُهُمَآ إِنِّىٓ أَرَىٰنِىٓ أَعْصِرُ خَمْرًا—One of them said, “Indeed, I see myself pressing wine.” The verb أَرَىٰنِىٓ—“I see myself”—is in a form that suggests repetition. It’s not that he saw this dream once; he keeps seeing it. “I can’t get rid of this dream. It keeps coming back to me, that I’m squeezing grapes, pressing wine.”

وَقَالَ ٱلْـَٔاخَرُ إِنِّىٓ أَرَىٰنِىٓ أَحْمِلُ فَوْقَ رَأْسِى خُبْزًا تَأْكُلُ ٱلطَّيْرُ مِنْهُ—And the other said, “Indeed, I see myself carrying bread upon my head, and birds are eating from it.” Again, the repetitive form—this dream haunts him. It’s vivid enough and recurring enough that it seems significant, like it must mean something.

نَبِّئْنَا بِتَأْوِيلِهِۦٓ—“Inform us of its interpretation,” tell us what this means. The word نَبِّئْ means to inform with important news, to give information that matters. And تَأْوِيل refers to the inner meaning, the interpretation of something confusing or symbolic.

Then comes their reason for asking: إِنَّا نَرَىٰكَ مِنَ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ—“Indeed, we see you as among the excellent ones,” among those who do things with excellence and beauty. The word مُحْسِن is profound. It refers to someone who doesn’t just do what’s right, but does it in the most beautiful way possible. These criminals, surrounded by the worst of humanity in prison, have observed Yusuf (AS) and recognized something extraordinary in him—a level of character, wisdom and goodness that sets him completely apart.

They’re not just asking a random person about dreams. They’ve identified someone special, someone who might have insight beyond the ordinary. And they’re right—but they don’t yet know just how right they are, or what role these dreams will play in the unfolding of Allah’s plan for Yusuf (AS) and for Egypt itself.

Ayah 37–43 · linked video: 08. Yusuf (Ayah 37-43)

Surah Yusuf: Ayahs 37-43

Ayah 37

قَالَ لَا يَأْتِيكُمَا طَعَامٌ تُرْزَقَانِهِۦٓ إِلَّا نَبَّأْتُكُمَا بِتَأْوِيلِهِۦ قَبْلَ أَن يَأْتِيَكُمَا ۚ ذَٰلِكُمَا مِمَّا عَلَّمَنِى رَبِّىٓ ۚ إِنِّى تَرَكْتُ مِلَّةَ قَوْمٍ لَّا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَهُم بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمْ كَـٰفِرُونَ

Yusuf (AS) recognizes this moment as an opportunity to share the message of Islam with his fellow prisoners. However, he demonstrates remarkable wisdom in his approach. When someone comes to you seeking specific information, you shouldn’t hijack the conversation to impose your own agenda. If Yusuf (AS) had simply interpreted their dreams and nothing more, the conversation would have ended immediately. Yet he also understands that you cannot force guidance upon people who haven’t come seeking it.

This balance in dawah is crucial. Sometimes people violate this principle dramatically. Imagine stopping at a masjid during a journey, and a well-meaning brother asks if he can share a brief reminder. You agree, expecting a few minutes, but he speaks for two hours about topics completely disconnected from your situation. This isn’t effective communication. It’s a violation of trust and people’s time.

Consider the Friday khutbah as another example. Many Muslims attend only for the congregational prayer. If you deliver a 45-minute sermon filled with advanced rulings and stories of the extraordinary piety of past generations, you risk alienating the very people who need guidance most. They won’t return, and you’ll have lost your opportunity to reach them. Someone who barely makes it to the masjid for Eid at 10 a.m. doesn’t need to hear about Tahajjud just yet. You must start where people are, not where you wish they were.

Yusuf (AS) models this beautifully. He sets a clear time boundary: “No food will come to you both which you are provided except that I will inform you of its interpretation before it reaches you.” Perhaps there are 30 minutes, maybe 20, before the meal arrives. He essentially says, “Lunch is coming soon. By the time it gets here, you’ll already know what your dream means. Is that acceptable?” They agree, and now they have a clear expectation. They have a deadline in mind.

This respect for people’s time is itself part of the teaching from his Master. When he says, “That is from what my Master has taught me” (ذَٰلِكُمَا مِمَّا عَلَّمَنِى رَبِّىٓ), he refers not only to dream interpretation but also to the etiquette of respectful communication. Allah ﷻ taught him to honor people’s time and attention.

We must apply this principle consistently. When visiting a masjid, ask what time the adhan is called and what time the iqamah will be given. Then respect those times. People have obligations—work, family responsibilities. It’s unfair to extend beyond reasonable boundaries, even with good intentions.

Having established this framework, Yusuf (AS) begins to introduce himself: “I have certainly left the religion of a people who do not believe in Allah” (إِنِّى تَرَكْتُ مِلَّةَ قَوْمٍ لَّا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ). The word مِلَّة refers to a way of life, a worldview that unites people emotionally and spiritually. He didn’t necessarily abandon an entire nation, but rather a particular group within that society who shared a specific ideology. This millah represents those who reject belief in Allah ﷻ and, especially, deny the afterlife.

Notice the emphasis: “and they, concerning the Hereafter, are disbelievers” (وَهُم بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمْ كَـٰفِرُونَ). The structure places extraordinary weight on their denial of the akhirah. Why this emphasis? Because the crimes committed against Yusuf (AS)—the minister’s wife’s transgression, his unjust imprisonment—all demonstrate a complete absence of fear regarding the consequences of the Hereafter.

This reveals something profound about human behavior. When it comes to temptation, corruption and wrongdoing, the strongest deterrent is belief in ultimate accountability. Those who refuse to remember the akhirah become deeply engrossed in sin. When you remind them of the Day of Judgment, they become disturbed and bothered. Yet this reminder is essential.

The message of Islam contains both hope and fear, and both are necessary. The Quran doesn’t bring only comfort; it also instills healthy fear of consequences. We cannot say people “aren’t ready” for warnings about accountability. The early Muslims were deeply engaged in various sins, yet they received both hope and fear simultaneously. Of course, we often begin with hope and transition to fear, but both messages are equally vital and cannot be separated.

Ayah 38

وَٱتَّبَعْتُ مِلَّةَ ءَابَآءِىٓ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَإِسْحَـٰقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ ۚ مَا كَانَ لَنَآ أَن نُّشْرِكَ بِٱللَّهِ مِن شَىْءٍ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ مِن فَضْلِ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْنَا وَعَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَشْكُرُونَ

Having explained what he left behind, Yusuf (AS) now describes what he embraced: “And I have followed the religion of my fathers, Ibrahim, Ishaq and Yaqub” (وَٱتَّبَعْتُ مِلَّةَ ءَابَآءِىٓ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَإِسْحَـٰقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ). He traces his spiritual lineage through three generations—his great-grandfather, grandfather and father. This isn’t merely biographical information; it’s a declaration of inherited wisdom and divine guidance.

Then comes the foundational principle: “It is not for us to associate anything with Allah” (مَا كَانَ لَنَآ أَن نُّشْرِكَ بِٱللَّهِ مِن شَىْءٍ). Notice that he begins with tawhid—the absolute oneness of Allah ﷻ—before addressing any other moral issue. Why? Because when you establish the proper relationship with Allah ﷻ, proper relationships with creation naturally follow.

These prisoners weren’t incarcerated for shirk. They committed crimes against other people—theft, murder, harm. But Yusuf (AS) identifies the root cause: once you wrong Allah ﷻ through shirk, it becomes easy to wrong His creation. Fix the foundation, and the structure can be repaired.

He describes this freedom from shirk as “a favor of Allah upon us and upon the people” (ذَٰلِكَ مِن فَضْلِ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْنَا وَعَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ). This is remarkable humility. He acknowledges, “I come from a special family, and what makes us special is that we do right by Allah ﷻ.” But immediately, he universalizes this blessing: this favor isn’t exclusive to his lineage. It’s available to all people. Allah’s guidance is open to everyone.

Yet he concludes with a sobering reality: “but most people are not grateful” (وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَشْكُرُونَ). Consider the irony: Yusuf (AS) speaks of gratitude while sitting in prison. Earlier in the surah, Yaqub (AS) described patience as something beautiful while enduring the loss of his beloved son. Now his son speaks of gratitude while unjustly imprisoned. They possess a completely different way of viewing life, a transformed perspective that transcends circumstances.

The ayah creates a deliberate contrast: it opens with shirk (associating partners with Allah ﷻ) and closes with the absence of shukr (gratitude). This is no accident. Shirk and ingratitude are intimately connected—both represent a failure to recognize Allah’s exclusive right to worship and thanks.

Ayah 39

يَـٰصَـٰحِبَىِ ٱلسِّجْنِ ءَأَرْبَابٌ مُّتَفَرِّقُونَ خَيْرٌ أَمِ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْوَٰحِدُ ٱلْقَهَّارُ

Yusuf (AS) now addresses them with warmth and camaraderie: “O my two companions of the prison” (يَـٰصَـٰحِبَىِ ٱلسِّجْنِ). He doesn’t say, “Hey, you two criminals,” or use their names with disdain. He acknowledges their shared circumstance with respect and friendship. This approach opens hearts far more effectively than condemnation.

Then he poses a profound logical question: “Are separate lords better, or Allah, the One, the Prevailing?” (ءَأَرْبَابٌ مُّتَفَرِّقُونَ خَيْرٌ أَمِ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْوَٰحِدُ ٱلْقَهَّارُ). The word أَرْبَابٌ refers to multiple lords or masters, and he uses the masculine plural form deliberately. The people of Egypt didn’t only worship stone idols; they also deified human beings. Kings claimed divinity, their families were worshipped, and entire dynasties presented themselves as living gods. The Pharaohs who would later rule Egypt exemplified this perfectly.

Yusuf (AS) asks them to consider: Do you really think scattered, competing authorities are superior to the One who is الْوَٰحِدُ (the One) and الْقَهَّارُ (the Prevailing, the Irresistible)? Al-Qahhar describes the One you cannot escape from, the One who dominates all existence, the undeniable and irresistible Power.

This question exposes the absurdity of shirk through simple logic. Multiple gods would naturally conflict with one another. They would have competing interests, contradictory commands, overlapping jurisdictions. The entire system becomes chaotic and ultimately meaningless. In contrast, Allah ﷻ is One, and His authority is absolute and inescapable.

Ayah 40

مَا تَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ إِلَّآ أَسْمَآءً سَمَّيْتُمُوهَآ أَنتُمْ وَءَابَآؤُكُم مَّآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ بِهَا مِن سُلْطَـٰنٍ ۚ إِنِ ٱلْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ ۚ أَمَرَ أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوٓا۟ إِلَّآ إِيَّاهُ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ٱلدِّينُ ٱلْقَيِّمُ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ

Yusuf (AS) now strips away the illusion entirely: “You worship not besides Him except names you have named them, you and your fathers” (مَا تَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ إِلَّآ أَسْمَآءً سَمَّيْتُمُوهَآ أَنتُمْ وَءَابَآؤُكُم). Perhaps as he spoke about Allah ﷻ, they reflexively reached for their amulets or protective bracelets bearing the names of their false gods. Maybe they protested: “No, no, we do believe in God! We believe in this god and that goddess, and this whole pantheon…”

He cuts through this rationalization: all of this is invented. These are merely names—labels you and your ancestors created. They have no reality, no power, no authority. “Allah has not sent down any authority for them” (مَّآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ بِهَا مِن سُلْطَـٰنٍ). The word سُلْطَـٰن means authority, power or proof. Allah ﷻ never authorized these objects of worship. They exist only in human imagination, transmitted through generations of inherited mythology.

“Legislation belongs to none but Allah” (إِنِ ٱلْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ). True authority, true governance, true wisdom—all of this belongs exclusively to Allah ﷻ. He alone has the right to command and forbid, to establish what is right and wrong.

“He has commanded that you worship none but Him” (أَمَرَ أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوٓا۟ إِلَّآ إِيَّاهُ). This is the divine command that supersedes all human invention: worship Allah ﷻ alone, without partners, without intermediaries, without invented deities.

“That is the correct religion” (ذَٰلِكَ ٱلدِّينُ ٱلْقَيِّمُ). The word ٱلْقَيِّمُ means upright, straight, established, valuable. This is the religion that stands firm, the path that doesn’t deviate, the way of life that has true worth.

“But most people do not know” (وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ). In the previous ayah, he said most people don’t show gratitude. Now he says most people don’t know. Ignorance and ingratitude are connected. When people don’t know the truth about Allah ﷻ, they cannot properly thank Him. And when they refuse to be grateful, they remain in ignorance.

Throughout this message, Yusuf (AS) maintains his tone of friendship: “My companions, I’m not insulting you. I care about you. I’m telling you this for your own benefit.”

Ayah 41

يَـٰصَـٰحِبَىِ ٱلسِّجْنِ أَمَّآ أَحَدُكُمَا فَيَسْقِى رَبَّهُۥ خَمْرًا ۖ وَأَمَّا ٱلْـَٔاخَرُ فَيُصْلَبُ فَتَأْكُلُ ٱلطَّيْرُ مِن رَّأْسِهِۦ ۚ قُضِىَ ٱلْأَمْرُ ٱلَّذِى فِيهِ تَسْتَفْتِيَانِ

Once again, he addresses them with affection: “O my two companions of the prison” (يَـٰصَـٰحِبَىِ ٱلسِّجْنِ). By this time, the meal cart is probably making its way toward their cell, and he promised to deliver the interpretation before the food arrives.

He faces a delicate situation: one interpretation is favorable, the other devastating. How does he present this? He begins with the good news: “As for one of you, he will give drink to his master of wine” (أَمَّآ أَحَدُكُمَا فَيَسْقِى رَبَّهُۥ خَمْرًا). This man will be released from prison and restored to his position, serving wine in the royal court.

Then comes the harsh reality: “But as for the other, he will be crucified, and the birds will eat from his head” (وَأَمَّا ٱلْـَٔاخَرُ فَيُصْلَبُ فَتَأْكُلُ ٱلطَّيْرُ مِن رَّأْسِهِۦ). Imagine being that second prisoner, hearing your imminent execution described so plainly. The natural human response would be denial: “No! You’re wrong! That can’t be true! Get away from me!”

But Yusuf (AS) doesn’t soften or retract his words. He concludes firmly: “The matter has been decreed about which you both inquire” (قُضِىَ ٱلْأَمْرُ ٱلَّذِى فِيهِ تَسْتَفْتِيَانِ). The decision is made. This is what you asked me to interpret, and I’ve given you an honest answer. I cannot change the interpretation to make you feel better. The dream reveals what will happen, and my responsibility is to convey the truth.

This demonstrates a crucial principle: when you’re asked for guidance or interpretation, you must speak the truth even when it’s difficult to hear. Yusuf (AS) doesn’t alter his message to please his audience. He delivers what Allah ﷻ has shown him with clarity and conviction.

Naturally, the prisoner facing execution no longer wishes to continue the conversation. But the one who received good news remains engaged. He’s intrigued, hopeful, perhaps even convinced by Yusuf’s evident sincerity and wisdom.

Ayah 42

وَقَالَ لِلَّذِى ظَنَّ أَنَّهُۥ نَاجٍ مِّنْهُمَا ٱذْكُرْنِى عِندَ رَبِّكَ فَأَنسَىٰهُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ ذِكْرَ رَبِّهِۦ فَلَبِثَ فِى ٱلسِّجْنِ بِضْعَ سِنِينَ

To the one who became optimistic about his release—“And he said to the one he knew would be saved of the two” (وَقَالَ لِلَّذِى ظَنَّ أَنَّهُۥ نَاجٍ مِّنْهُمَا)—Yusuf (AS) makes a simple request: “Mention me before your master” (ٱذْكُرْنِى عِندَ رَبِّكَ). When you return to the court, when you resume your position serving wine to the king or his officials, just mention me. Don’t make a big case. Just drop a note, like a lobbyist putting in a word. “There’s someone named Yusuf in the prison. You might want to look into his situation.”

This is a reasonable, human request. Yusuf (AS) isn’t demanding immediate release or making elaborate plans. He simply asks to be remembered, for his case to be brought to someone’s attention. The man will likely serve in the households of the very people connected to his imprisonment—perhaps even the ministers or nobles who know the women from the banquet scene.

But then something goes wrong: “But Satan made him forget the mention of his Master” (فَأَنسَىٰهُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ ذِكْرَ رَبِّهِۦ). This phrase carries two layers of meaning. On the surface, Shaytan made the freed prisoner forget to mention Yusuf to his earthly master. He got caught up in his new life, his restored position, his relief at escaping execution. The promise he made in prison faded from his mind.

But there’s a deeper significance: this man had just been introduced to the truth about Allah ﷻ. The dream’s fulfillment should have confirmed everything Yusuf (AS) taught him about tawhid, about the One God, about ultimate accountability. When the interpretation came true exactly as predicted, it should have convinced him that Yusuf (AS) was truthful—not just about dreams, but about the message of Islam itself.

However, he didn’t maintain remembrance of Allah ﷻ. He forgot his Master in the ultimate sense. And when you stop remembering Allah ﷻ, Shaytan gains a particular power over you: the power to make you forget even more. Forgetfulness breeds forgetfulness. Distance from Allah ﷻ creates further distance.

The consequence: “So he remained in prison for several years” (فَلَبِثَ فِى ٱلسِّجْنِ بِضْعَ سِنِينَ). The word بِضْعَ means several, typically understood as less than ten, with some scholars suggesting specifically seven years. Yusuf (AS) continues his unjust imprisonment, waiting for Allah’s plan to unfold in His perfect timing.

Ayah 43

وَقَالَ ٱلْمَلِكُ إِنِّىٓ أَرَىٰ سَبْعَ بَقَرَٰتٍ سِمَانٍ يَأْكُلُهُنَّ سَبْعٌ عِجَافٌ وَسَبْعَ سُنۢبُلَـٰتٍ خُضْرٍ وَأُخَرَ يَابِسَـٰتٍ ۖ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْمَلَأُ أَفْتُونِى فِى رُءْيَـٰىَ إِن كُنتُمْ لِلرُّءْيَا تَعْبُرُونَ

The scene shifts dramatically. We move from the prison to the royal court. After seeing the minister and his household, we now enter the presence of the king himself, surrounded by his courtiers and advisors—the الْمَلَأُ, the assembly of nobles and officials.

This marks the fourth dream in Surah Yusuf: first, Yusuf’s own childhood vision; second and third, the dreams of his two prison companions; and now, the king’s troubling dream.

“The king said: Indeed, I keep seeing seven fat cows being eaten by seven thin cows” (وَقَالَ ٱلْمَلِكُ إِنِّىٓ أَرَىٰ سَبْعَ بَقَرَٰتٍ سِمَانٍ يَأْكُلُهُنَّ سَبْعٌ عِجَافٌ). The word سِمَانٍ means fat, well-fed, healthy. These cows represent abundance and prosperity. But they’re being devoured by عِجَافٌ—lean, emaciated cows.

The word عِجَافٌ comes from a root meaning to refrain from eating or to stop eating before being satisfied. These aren’t just thin cows; they’re desperately hungry, never satisfied, consuming but never full.

The king continues: “And seven green spikes of grain and others dry” (وَسَبْعَ سُنۢبُلَـٰتٍ خُضْرٍ وَأُخَرَ يَابِسَـٰتٍ). The imagery shifts from livestock to agriculture—seven fresh, green ears of grain contrasted with seven dried, withered ones. Both symbols point toward the same reality: cycles of abundance and scarcity, prosperity and famine.

The king turns to his advisors: “O eminent ones, explain to me my vision, if you should interpret visions” (يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْمَلَأُ أَفْتُونِى فِى رُءْيَـٰىَ إِن كُنتُمْ لِلرُّءْيَا تَعْبُرُونَ). Notice the conditional at the end: “if you are able to interpret dreams.” This reveals something important.

These courtiers are professional yes-men. That’s how they secured their positions. When the king expresses interest in something, they immediately claim expertise. “Can you interpret dreams?” “Oh yes, Your Majesty, absolutely!” Just like a restaurant server who, when asked if they have an obscure dish, confidently replies, “Don’t worry, we’ll get it for you”—even if they’ve never heard of it.

The king took them at their word previously when they boasted about their interpretive abilities. Now he presents them with his actual dream and essentially says, “You told me you were specialists in dream interpretation. This is what you claimed. So explain this to me.”

We’ll see in the coming ayahs how these supposed experts respond—and how Allah ﷻ orchestrates events to finally bring Yusuf (AS) out of prison and into his destined role. The king’s dream will prove to be the key that unlocks the door to Yusuf’s vindication and elevation, all according to Allah’s perfect wisdom and timing.

Ayah 44–57 · linked video: 09. Yusuf (Ayah 44-57)

Surah Yusuf: Ayahs 44-57

Ayah 44

قَالُوٓا۟ أَضْغَـٰثُ أَحْلَـٰمٍ ۖ وَمَا نَحْنُ بِتَأْوِيلِ ٱلْأَحْلَـٰمِ بِعَـٰلِمِينَ

The courtiers dismissed the king’s dream with arrogant indifference: أَضْغَـٰثُ أَحْلَـٰمٍ- confused, tangled dreams. The word أَضْغَـٰث carries the connotation of something jumbled and meaningless, like you had a heavy dinner and now you’re having pointless, baseless dreams with no fruit or purpose to them. The term أَحْلَـٰمٍ is typically used in the Quran for bad dreams, dreams that disturb rather than enlighten.

They continued: وَمَا نَحْنُ بِتَأْوِيلِ ٱلْأَحْلَـٰمِ بِعَـٰلِمِينَ - “And we are not knowledgeable in the interpretation of dreams.” Their dismissal wasn’t just about lacking expertise. It was about not even considering dream interpretation worth knowing. “We don’t know anything about that. That’s not even worth knowing. Don’t worry about it.”

Ayah 45

وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِى نَجَا مِنْهُمَا وَٱدَّكَرَ بَعْدَ أُمَّةٍ أَنَا۠ أُنَبِّئُكُم بِتَأْوِيلِهِۦ فَأَرْسِلُونِ

After the entire court proved useless, the cupbearer who had been released from prison suddenly saw an opportunity. The phrase وَٱدَّكَرَ بَعْدَ أُمَّةٍ- “and he remembered after a period of time” - is significant. How much time had passed? Seven years. Even after this royal meeting where the king desperately sought help and everyone said no, the cupbearer went home that day thinking, “Dream… dream… wait a second.”

After some time, the realization hit him. But notice what drove him back to the king. It wasn’t loyalty to Yusuf (AS) or gratitude for the interpretation that saved his life. It was pure self-interest. Is it easy for a servant to speak directly to the king? No. But he mustered the courage because he knew something was in it for him. This was his chance for a promotion.

أَنَا۠ أُنَبِّئُكُم بِتَأْوِيلِهِۦ - “I will inform you of its interpretation.” Notice he doesn’t mention Yusuf (AS). He’s protecting his source, keeping this classified information to himself. Why? Because this is valuable capital. He might go from serving drinks to having drinks served to him.

فَأَرْسِلُونِ - “So send me.” He asks for time off, a couple of days perhaps. The king grants it, and the cupbearer heads to the prison.

Ayah 46

يُوسُفُ أَيُّهَا ٱلصِّدِّيقُ أَفْتِنَا فِى سَبْعِ بَقَرَٰتٍ سِمَانٍ يَأْكُلُهُنَّ سَبْعٌ عِجَافٌ وَسَبْعِ سُنۢبُلَـٰتٍ خُضْرٍ وَأُخَرَ يَابِسَـٰتٍ لَّعَلِّىٓ أَرْجِعُ إِلَى ٱلنَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ

The scene shifts to the prison. Yusuf (AS) is behind bars. Seven years or more have passed, and the cupbearer has completely forgotten him. Now he suddenly appears: يُوسُفُ أَيُّهَا ٱلصِّدِّيقُ - “Yusuf! O truthful one! My buddy! How you doing?” What shameless audacity. This is criminal psychology on display, the workings of a con artist. He completely forgot about Yusuf (AS), but now that he needs him, it’s all false familiarity and manufactured urgency.

أَفْتِنَا - “Give us your ruling” - help us interpret this dream. He rushes through pleasantries: “Sorry I forgot about you and all that, but could you help me out real quick?” Then he describes the king’s vision: سَبْعِ بَقَرَٰتٍ سِمَانٍ يَأْكُلُهُنَّ سَبْعٌ عِجَافٌ - “Seven fat cows being eaten by seven lean ones” - وَسَبْعِ سُنۢبُلَـٰتٍ خُضْرٍ وَأُخَرَ يَابِسَـٰتٍ - “and seven green ears of grain and others dried.”

Then comes the manipulation: لَّعَلِّىٓ أَرْجِعُ إِلَى ٱلنَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ - “So that I may return to the people, so that they may know.” Who is it actually for? The king. But the cupbearer frames it as serving the greater good, helping the nation. This is classic con artistry: make yourself look altruistic while pursuing purely selfish ends.

Ayahs 47-49

قَالَ تَزْرَعُونَ سَبْعَ سِنِينَ دَأَبًا فَمَا حَصَدتُّمْ فَذَرُوهُ فِى سُنۢبُلِهِۦٓ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا مِّمَّا تَأْكُلُونَ

ثُمَّ يَأْتِى مِنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ سَبْعٌ شِدَادٌ يَأْكُلْنَ مَا قَدَّمْتُمْ لَهُنَّ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا مِّمَّا تُحْصِنُونَ

ثُمَّ يَأْتِى مِنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ عَامٌ فِيهِ يُغَاثُ ٱلنَّاسُ وَفِيهِ يَعْصِرُونَ

Yusuf (AS) doesn’t waste time with the cupbearer’s games. He delivers the interpretation directly and completely: قَالَ تَزْرَعُونَ سَبْعَ سِنِينَ دَأَبًا - “You will cultivate for seven years consecutively.” The word دَأَبًا means continuously, with consistency and diligence.

Then comes the crucial agricultural guidance: فَمَا حَصَدتُّمْ فَذَرُوهُ فِى سُنۢبُلِهِۦٓ - “And whatever you harvest, leave it in its ear” - don’t peel it, don’t process it fully. Keep it stored in its natural protective covering. إِلَّا قَلِيلًا مِّمَّا تَأْكُلُونَ - “Except a little from which you will eat.” This is precise economic planning: store the maximum, consume the minimum.

ثُمَّ يَأْتِى مِنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ سَبْعٌ شِدَادٌ - “Then will come after that seven difficult years.” The word شِدَادٌ emphasizes severity and hardship. These years will يَأْكُلْنَ مَا قَدَّمْتُمْ لَهُنَّ - “devour what you advanced for them” - they will consume whatever you stored, إِلَّا قَلِيلًا مِّمَّا تُحْصِنُونَ - “except a little which you will preserve.”

But the interpretation doesn’t end with despair. ثُمَّ يَأْتِى مِنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ عَامٌ - “Then will come after that a year” - فِيهِ يُغَاثُ ٱلنَّاسُ - “in which people will be given abundant rain.” The verb يُغَاثُ literally means they will be drenched, overwhelmed with rain, blessed with abundance. وَفِيهِ يَعْصِرُونَ - “And in it they will press” - they will squeeze fruits, make juices, extract oils. Times will be prosperous again.

This is the fifteenth year. The dream interpretation spans a decade and a half - longer than four presidential terms. This requires serious, sustained, long-term economic planning.

Ayah 50

وَقَالَ ٱلْمَلِكُ ٱئْتُونِى بِهِۦ ۖ فَلَمَّا جَآءَهُ ٱلرَّسُولُ قَالَ ٱرْجِعْ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ فَسْـَٔلْهُ مَا بَالُ ٱلنِّسْوَةِ ٱلَّـٰتِى قَطَّعْنَ أَيْدِيَهُنَّ ۚ إِنَّ رَبِّى بِكَيْدِهِنَّ عَلِيمٌ

The Quran omits the cupbearer’s return to the king. We can imagine the scene: “Where did you get this interpretation? Who told you?” The cupbearer clearly isn’t intelligent enough to devise such a comprehensive economic plan. Eventually he reveals his source: there’s a man in prison.

وَقَالَ ٱلْمَلِكُ ٱئْتُونِى بِهِۦ - “And the king said, ‘Bring him to me.’” But when the royal messenger arrived at the prison, Yusuf (AS) demonstrated extraordinary wisdom and self-respect. قَالَ ٱرْجِعْ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ - “He said, ‘Return to your master.’”

What follows is remarkable. After seven years of unjust imprisonment, most people would rush out the moment the door opened. But Yusuf (AS) says: فَسْـَٔلْهُ مَا بَالُ ٱلنِّسْوَةِ ٱلَّـٰتِى قَطَّعْنَ أَيْدِيَهُنَّ - “And ask him what was the case of the women who cut their hands?” I’m not coming out of prison until my name is cleared. I need my reputation restored first.

إِنَّ رَبِّى بِكَيْدِهِنَّ عَلِيمٌ - “Indeed, my Lord is Knowing of their plan.” The word رَبِّى - “my Lord” - could refer to Allah ﷻ, which is most likely. But it could also refer to the minister, meaning: that master you work for knows all about this plot. If you want to start the investigation, ask him.

This decision reveals Yusuf’s character. He refuses to be freed as a guilty man receiving royal pardon. He insists on being freed as an innocent man whose name has been publicly cleared.

Ayah 51

قَالَ مَا خَطْبُكُنَّ إِذْ رَٰوَدتُّنَّ يُوسُفَ عَن نَّفْسِهِۦ ۚ قُلْنَ حَـٰشَ لِلَّهِ مَا عَلِمْنَا عَلَيْهِ مِن سُوٓءٍ ۚ قَالَتِ ٱمْرَأَتُ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْـَٔـٰنَ حَصْحَصَ ٱلْحَقُّ أَنَا۠ رَٰوَدتُّهُۥ عَن نَّفْسِهِۦ وَإِنَّهُۥ لَمِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ

Between the lines, we see the messenger returning to report that Yusuf (AS) refuses to leave prison. The king is puzzled: “He doesn’t want to come out? That’s strange. Why wouldn’t anyone want to leave prison? How long has he been there?” Seven years. “And he still doesn’t want to come out? What’s his question?”

The investigation begins. Now everyone is trying to save themselves. The cupbearer wanted a promotion, so he pointed to Yusuf (AS). Yusuf (AS) pointed to the women. The women will point to the minister’s wife. The minister says, “Ask the ladies.”

قَالَ مَا خَطْبُكُنَّ إِذْ رَٰوَدتُّنَّ يُوسُفَ عَن نَّفْسِهِۦ - “He said, ‘What was your condition when you sought to seduce Yusuf?’” The scandal has come out. The king now knows something improper happened.

قُلْنَ حَـٰشَ لِلَّهِ مَا عَلِمْنَا عَلَيْهِ مِن سُوٓءٍ - “They said, ‘Perfect is Allah! We know of no evil from him.’” The elite women immediately testified to Yusuf’s innocence, and all eyes turned to the minister’s wife.

قَالَتِ ٱمْرَأَتُ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْـَٔـٰنَ حَصْحَصَ ٱلْحَقُّ - “The wife of al-‘Aziz said, ’Now the truth has become evident.’” The verb حَصْحَصَ beautifully captures how truth surfaces and becomes manifest, like something climbing up from beneath until it can no longer be hidden.

Finally exposed with no options left, she confesses: أَنَا۠ رَٰوَدتُّهُۥ عَن نَّفْسِهِۦ - “I sought to seduce him.” She admits her guilt. وَإِنَّهُۥ لَمِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ - “And indeed, he is from the truthful ones.” She vindicates Yusuf (AS) publicly.

This isn’t genuine decency. If she were truly righteous, she would have spoken up seven years ago. She only confessed because she was cornered, because fingers kept pointing until they finally landed on her.

Ayah 52

ذَٰلِكَ لِيَعْلَمَ أَنِّى لَمْ أَخُنْهُ بِٱلْغَيْبِ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِى كَيْدَ ٱلْخَآئِنِينَ

Now comes a psychologically fascinating moment. As she’s being led away, perhaps in custody, she makes one final statement - and it reveals the depth of her delusion: ذَٰلِكَ لِيَعْلَمَ أَنِّى لَمْ أَخُنْهُ بِٱلْغَيْبِ - “That is so he will know that I did not betray him in his absence.”

This is the statement of someone deeply disturbed. She’s essentially saying: “Let him know I didn’t cheat on him. I proved his innocence. Maybe he still loves me.” She never grasped that he never loved her. This is how criminals sometimes behave when being taken away - they try to highlight one supposedly good thing they did, grasping for redemption or recognition.

وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِى كَيْدَ ٱلْخَآئِنِينَ - “And that Allah does not guide the plan of betrayers.” Allah ﷻ does not allow the plots of those who cheat and betray to succeed.

Ayah 53

وَمَآ أُبَرِّئُ نَفْسِىٓ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلنَّفْسَ لَأَمَّارَةٌۢ بِٱلسُّوٓءِ إِلَّا مَا رَحِمَ رَبِّىٓ ۚ إِنَّ رَبِّى غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

Her confession continues, and she appears to have a spiritual awakening - though we cannot judge its sincerity: وَمَآ أُبَرِّئُ نَفْسِىٓ - “And I do not acquit myself.” I don’t claim innocence anymore. I admit my guilt.

إِنَّ ٱلنَّفْسَ لَأَمَّارَةٌۢ بِٱلسُّوٓءِ - “Indeed, the soul is a persistent enjoiner of evil.” The word أَمَّارَةٌ is an intensive form, meaning the nafs doesn’t just command evil once - it keeps commanding over and over, compelling you repeatedly to do wrong. It doesn’t stop.

إِلَّا مَا رَحِمَ رَبِّىٓ - “Except what my Lord has mercy upon.” In other words, if not for Allah’s mercy, I would keep doing evil more and more. Without divine intervention and mercy, the soul spirals downward.

إِنَّ رَبِّى غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ - “Indeed, my Lord is Forgiving and Merciful.” She ends with an acknowledgment of Allah’s attributes - His constant forgiveness and encompassing mercy.

These are her words. We don’t know if they’re sincere or simply the desperate statements of someone caught. The Quran records what she said without validating the depth of her repentance.

Ayah 54

وَقَالَ ٱلْمَلِكُ ٱئْتُونِى بِهِۦٓ أَسْتَخْلِصْهُ لِنَفْسِى ۖ فَلَمَّا كَلَّمَهُۥ قَالَ إِنَّكَ ٱلْيَوْمَ لَدَيْنَا مَكِينٌ أَمِينٌ

Now cleared of all charges, وَقَالَ ٱلْمَلِكُ ٱئْتُونِى بِهِۦٓ - “The king said, ‘Bring him to me.’” But this time it’s different: أَسْتَخْلِصْهُ لِنَفْسِى - “I will select him exclusively for myself.” The king recognizes something special here. This man was wrongly imprisoned, yet he’s helped tremendously with the dream interpretation. The king wants to keep Yusuf (AS) as a special adviser with no intermediary, answering to no corrupt bureaucracy or ministers - reporting directly to him.

فَلَمَّا كَلَّمَهُۥ - “And when he spoke to him” - when the king engaged Yusuf (AS) in conversation during the interview, he realized this wasn’t just someone to keep as a servant. This man was extraordinarily sharp.

قَالَ إِنَّكَ ٱلْيَوْمَ لَدَيْنَا مَكِينٌ أَمِينٌ - “He said, ‘Indeed, you are today established in position and trusted.’” The king offers two things: مَكِينٌ - a firmly established position of authority, and أَمِينٌ - complete trust and security. Politically, Yusuf (AS) has enemies because of the scandal, so the king grants not just job security but physical security as well.

Ayah 55

قَالَ ٱجْعَلْنِى عَلَىٰ خَزَآئِنِ ٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ إِنِّى حَفِيظٌ عَلِيمٌ

What Yusuf (AS) does next is extraordinary and requires careful understanding. He knows he has a stable position. He knows he’s in a position to speak. And he realizes something critical: if the dream interpretation isn’t executed properly, people will die.

The dream requires immense fiscal responsibility, and it’s already become clear that a huge number of ministers and political elite in this society are corrupt. Fiscal corruption will prevent proper execution of the economic plan, and people will starve.

This is not the time to be humble. This is the time for confidence and decisive action.

قَالَ ٱجْعَلْنِى عَلَىٰ خَزَآئِنِ ٱلْأَرْضِ - “He said, ‘Appoint me over the storehouses of the land.’” He doesn’t accept the secretary position. He looks around the room and essentially says, “Nobody else’s resume is good enough. Make me the Treasury Secretary. Make me that. I’m telling you, nobody else knows what they’re doing and nobody else is trustworthy.”

Then he provides his qualifications: إِنِّى حَفِيظٌ عَلِيمٌ - “Indeed, I am a knowing guardian.” First, حَفِيظٌ - I will watch over that trust carefully. None of these people look like they know how to guard it. Second, عَلِيمٌ - I am knowledgeable, I have the proper credentials and understanding to manage this responsibility.

This moment represents a beautiful transition in Yusuf’s character. He was once a shy boy in his father’s company, reluctant to fully describe his dream about the sun and moon prostrating. He couldn’t even finish that sentence. But when the time comes, you must step up to the plate with confidence.

When Yusuf (AS) realizes that showing false humility - “No, no, I cannot fit those shoes” - would result in people dying, that’s not the time to be humble. Be humble on your own time. Right now the nation needs you. Take action.

We learn here a beautiful balance between humility and confidence. Too much humility becomes complacency. Too much confidence becomes arrogance. True taqwa places you right in the middle, as embodied in Yusuf’s character. Now is the time for confidence.

Ayah 56

وَكَذَٰلِكَ مَكَّنَّا لِيُوسُفَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ يَتَبَوَّأُ مِنْهَا حَيْثُ يَشَآءُ ۚ نُصِيبُ بِرَحْمَتِنَا مَن نَّشَآءُ ۖ وَلَا نُضِيعُ أَجْرَ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ

وَكَذَٰلِكَ مَكَّنَّا لِيُوسُفَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ - “And thus We established Yusuf in the land.” This is the second time Allah ﷻ uses this phrase about Yusuf (AS). The first was when he initially arrived in Egypt and found a comfortable room in the minister’s house. But now he’s truly established - not just with a nice room, but as the Treasury Secretary with real authority.

يَتَبَوَّأُ مِنْهَا حَيْثُ يَشَآءُ - “He could settle therein wherever he willed.” The verb يَتَبَوَّأُ means to seek out and settle in a property. When you get a significant promotion, the first thing you do is look for a house in a nice neighborhood, find the perfect property, and say, “I want to move here.”

This is profoundly significant because Yusuf (AS) never got to pick his own home before. His first “home” was the well. His next was the caravan. Then the minister’s house. Then prison. He never chose any of them. Now Allah ﷻ says: you get to pick your house. Go choose whatever you like, wherever you want.

There’s nothing wrong with seeking a beautiful home. This is halal and natural.

نُصِيبُ بِرَحْمَتِنَا مَن نَّشَآءُ - “We bestow Our mercy upon whom We will.” The verb نُصِيبُ comes from مُصِيبَة - calamity. But مُصِيبَة actually means “something that targets you.” All his life, Yusuf (AS) was targeted with difficulty - one calamity after another struck him with precision. Now Allah ﷻ says: We target with Our mercy whomever We want. The targeting was always there; what changed was whether it was difficulty or mercy being aimed at him. And all of it - every single moment - was part of the divine plan, exactly according to schedule.

وَلَا نُضِيعُ أَجْرَ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ - “And We do not allow to be lost the reward of those who do good.” This is a remarkable observation. When Yusuf (AS) tells the king, “I am the right person for this job,” you might think he’s highlighting himself with arrogance. But Allah ﷻ calls him here مُحْسِن - one who excels in doing good.

What is the definition of ihsan? To be cognizant of Allah’s presence, to worship Allah as though you see Him. It’s Yusuf’s awareness of Allah’s presence that compels him to say, “You need to put me in charge.” Why? Because Allah ﷻ will ask him about the ability He gave, the talent He bestowed, the knowledge He granted, the character He shaped - all of which could save lives. If Yusuf (AS) doesn’t take action, he will be accountable.

He can feel the presence of Allah ﷻ so intensely, it’s almost as if he has no choice but to ask for this job. It’s his taqwa, his humility before Allah ﷻ, that makes him confident - not arrogant.

We sometimes think being cognizant of Allah means shrinking back, saying “I’m nobody, I have nothing.” But that’s not always taqwa. Sometimes taqwa demands you step forward. There are communities with only a handful of Muslims who desperately need someone to lead prayer, and the most knowledgeable person - even if relatively limited in knowledge - must step up. That’s not arrogance; that’s responsibility.

Sometimes, for the sake of Allah ﷻ, you have to take charge.

Ayah 57

وَلَأَجْرُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ خَيْرٌ لِّلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَكَانُوا۟ يَتَّقُونَ

Even as Yusuf (AS) enjoys this tremendous blessing - choosing his own home, holding one of the most dignified positions in the land, with everyone looking up to him and all his political enemies defeated - Allah ﷻ reminds us what truly matters:

وَلَأَجْرُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ خَيْرٌ لِّلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَكَانُوا۟ يَتَّقُونَ - “But the reward of the Hereafter is better for those who believed and were fearing Allah.”

All of this worldly success - the position, the respect, the authority, the beautiful home - is nothing compared to the reward awaiting in the akhirah for those who truly believed and truly had taqwa.

This ayah brings resolution to the latest challenge in Yusuf’s story. He was imprisoned unjustly, and now he’s been freed, vindicated, and elevated to a position where he can serve and save lives. The next set of ayahs will begin when his brothers arrive in Egypt, setting the stage for a reunion none of them could have imagined.

Ayah 58–76 · linked video: 10. Yusuf (Ayah 58-76)

Ayah 58

وَجَآءَ إِخْوَةُ يُوسُفَ فَدَخَلُوا۟ عَلَيْهِ فَعَرَفَهُمْ وَهُمْ لَهُۥ مُنكِرُونَ

The brothers of Yusuf (AS) came and entered upon him. He recognized them, but they were completely unaware of who he was.

To understand this moment, we need some background. Seven years of abundance had passed in Egypt, followed by the beginning of seven years of severe famine. The only source of food was what the ministry had carefully stored in the royal treasury during the prosperous years. Towns throughout the region suffering from drought were traveling to Egypt to exchange whatever little they had for subsidized goods from the king’s stores. The policy was simple: for each person from your family, you receive one camel’s load of grain.

The brothers of Yusuf (AS) were also suffering from the famine in Canaan. They traveled to Egypt to trade what little they had for provisions, but they did not bring Binyamin with them. Their father Ya’qub (AS) refused to let him go—the last time he entrusted them with a son, it ended in tragedy.

However, hoping to receive extra provisions, the brothers mentioned to the Egyptian officials that they had another brother at home who couldn’t make the journey. Could they perhaps take his portion as well?

When Allah ﷻ says فَعَرَفَهُمْ (“he recognized them”), this was a moment of overwhelming emotion for Yusuf (AS). Years had passed, yet he immediately knew who they were.

But وَهُمْ لَهُۥ مُنكِرُونَ - they were in a state of complete non-recognition. The word مُنكِر implies total alienation; they didn’t have even the slightest suspicion of his identity. There are several reasons for this. While the brothers had been adults when they threw Yusuf (AS) into the well and hadn’t changed much physically, Yusuf (AS) had been a young boy. His face had matured completely. Beyond the physical transformation, his entire demeanor had changed. The shy, meek child they remembered was now a confident ruler carrying himself with authority. This was the last person they would expect to encounter.

It must have required tremendous self-control for Yusuf (AS) to maintain his composure when he saw all of them standing before him.

Ayah 59

وَلَمَّا جَهَّزَهُم بِجَهَازِهِمْ قَالَ ٱئْتُونِى بِأَخٍ لَّكُم مِّنْ أَبِيكُمْ ۚ أَلَا تَرَوْنَ أَنِّىٓ أُوفِى ٱلْكَيْلَ وَأَنَا۠ خَيْرُ ٱلْمُنزِلِينَ

When he had furnished them with their provisions, he said, “Bring me a brother of yours from your father. Do you not see that I give full measure and that I am the best of hosts?”

The phrase وَلَمَّا جَهَّزَهُم بِجَهَازِهِمْ indicates that Yusuf (AS) loaded them up with provisions—but he gave them extra, more than the standard ration. During their transaction, the brothers had mentioned their absent sibling. Yusuf (AS) engaged them in conversation: Why couldn’t he come? Who is he? Tell me about him.

They explained that this was their stepbrother, their father’s other son. After losing Yusuf (AS) years ago, their father was extremely protective and wouldn’t let this one travel far from home.

Yusuf (AS) responded strategically: “Alright, bring him next year.”

Notice how Allah ﷻ records his words: ٱئْتُونِى بِأَخٍ لَّكُم مِّنْ أَبِيكُمْ - “Bring me a brother of yours from your father.” He doesn’t simply say “your brother.” The phrasing emphasizes that this person is connected to them only through their common father—he is their stepbrother. This detail matters because it reveals the family dynamics and creates the setup for what’s to come.

Then Yusuf (AS) appeals to their reason: أَلَا تَرَوْنَ أَنِّىٓ أُوفِى ٱلْكَيْلَ - “Don’t you see that I give full measure?” He’s pointing to his generosity and fairness. He gave them extra this time, demonstrating his trustworthiness.

وَأَنَا۠ خَيْرُ ٱلْمُنزِلِينَ - “And I am the best of hosts.” The word مُنزِل doesn’t just mean someone who gives things, but specifically refers to someone who gives gifts of hospitality. Yusuf (AS) is establishing himself as exceptionally generous, the best gift-giver they’re ever going to find.

Now comes the condition—and it’s brilliant because it serves dual purposes. On the surface, it’s completely logical. Beneath the surface, it’s driven by Yusuf’s desperate desire to see Binyamin.

Ayah 60

فَإِن لَّمْ تَأْتُونِى بِهِۦ فَلَا كَيْلَ لَكُمْ عِندِى وَلَا تَقْرَبُونِ

“But if you do not bring him to me, then there will be no measure for you from me, nor will you approach me.”

This is the consequence: فَإِن لَّمْ تَأْتُونِى بِهِۦ فَلَا كَيْلَ لَكُمْ عِندِى - “If you don’t bring him to me, none of you will receive any provisions.” The logic is airtight: if you claimed you have another brother to get extra rations, and then you don’t bring him, that means you were lying to scam the system for an extra camel’s worth of supplies.

وَلَا تَقْرَبُونِ - “And don’t even come near me.” You’ll be banned from this land entirely. Don’t even come close.

Yusuf (AS) has created a perfect cover for getting Binyamin to Egypt. His stated reason is completely legitimate—he’s protecting against fraud. But his real motivation is the overwhelming desire to see his full brother, the only other son of his beloved mother.

Ayah 61

قَالُوا۟ سَنُرَٰوِدُ عَنْهُ أَبَاهُ وَإِنَّا لَفَـٰعِلُونَ

They said, “We will attempt to dissuade his father from keeping him, and we will certainly do it.”

The brothers respond: قَالُوا۟ سَنُرَٰوِدُ عَنْهُ أَبَاهُ. The verb رَاوَدَ means to try to change someone’s mind, to persuade someone to think differently, to make someone slip away from their position. We’ve encountered this word before in the surah when the wife of the Aziz tried to seduce Yusuf (AS).

Here, who are they going to try to make “slip”? Their father. They’re going to work on Ya’qub (AS) to convince him to let go of Binyamin, to loosen his protective grip.

وَإِنَّا لَفَـٰعِلُونَ - “And we will certainly do it.” They promise. They’re really going to accomplish this. Thank you for the opportunity, they’re essentially saying. We’ll make it happen.

Ayah 62

وَقَالَ لِفِتْيَـٰنِهِ ٱجْعَلُوا۟ بِضَـٰعَتَهُمْ فِى رِحَالِهِمْ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَعْرِفُونَهَآ إِذَا ٱنقَلَبُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهِمْ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ

And he said to his servants, “Put their merchandise into their saddlebags so they might recognize it when they return to their people, so that perhaps they will come back.”

After the brothers left, Yusuf (AS) gave an instruction to his young attendants. وَقَالَ لِفِتْيَـٰنِهِ - he said to his servants, the young men who worked for him.

ٱجْعَلُوا۟ بِضَـٰعَتَهُمْ فِى رِحَالِهِمْ - “Put their merchandise into their saddlebags.” The بِضَاعَة refers to the goods they had brought to trade for provisions. The رِحَال are their packed travel bags. Yusuf (AS) instructed his servants to secretly slip the goods they had traded back into their bags.

Why would he do this? On the surface, it appears to be a gesture of goodwill, an act of generosity. But there’s more strategic thinking at work here.

لَعَلَّهُمْ يَعْرِفُونَهَآ إِذَا ٱنقَلَبُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهِمْ - “So that they might recognize it when they return to their people.” When they arrive home and unpack, they’ll discover their money has been returned.

لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ - “So that perhaps they will come back.”

Here’s the deeper wisdom: Yusuf (AS) was intelligent enough to recognize that what they brought was pathetically little. The economic situation was dire. They barely had anything to trade this year. What would they bring next year? If times continue to worsen, they might not have anything at all to trade. And if they have nothing to trade, they won’t come back—because they’d be too ashamed or unable to make the journey.

By returning their merchandise, Yusuf (AS) ensures they’ll have something to bring next year. This isn’t just generosity—it’s a calculated move to guarantee their return. And their return means Binyamin’s arrival. If the goods they brought had been substantial, suggesting they could bring more next year, he wouldn’t have returned them. But from the small amount they traded, it was clear they were struggling. He needed to give this back to them, or he might never see them—or Binyamin—again.

Ayah 63

فَلَمَّا رَجَعُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰٓ أَبِيهِمْ قَالُوا۟ يَـٰٓأَبَانَا مُنِعَ مِنَّا ٱلْكَيْلُ فَأَرْسِلْ مَعَنَآ أَخَانَا نَكْتَلْ وَإِنَّا لَهُۥ لَحَـٰفِظُونَ

So when they returned to their father, they said, “O our father, measure has been denied to us, so send our brother with us that we may obtain measure, and we will surely be his guardians.”

فَلَمَّا رَجَعُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰٓ أَبِيهِمْ - when they returned to their father, they immediately began their persuasion campaign.

قَالُوا۟ يَـٰٓأَبَانَا مُنِعَ مِنَّا ٱلْكَيْلُ - “O our father, measure has been denied to us.” They’re explaining that next year’s provisions have been forbidden to them. The الْكَيْل refers to the measured portion of grain they would receive.

فَأَرْسِلْ مَعَنَآ أَخَانَا نَكْتَلْ - “So send our brother with us that we may obtain measure.” The verb نَكْتَلْ comes from the same root, meaning to receive the full measured portion. Notice they don’t say “so that he can get his share”—they say “so that we may obtain measure,” making it about the collective family benefit.

وَإِنَّا لَهُۥ لَحَـٰفِظُونَ - “And we will surely be his guardians.”

These exact words. Have they used these exact words before? Yes—when they asked to take Yusuf (AS). Their father, patient as he is, hasn’t forgotten.

Ayah 64

قَالَ هَلْ ءَامَنُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ إِلَّا كَمَآ أَمِنتُكُمْ عَلَىٰٓ أَخِيهِ مِن قَبْلُ ۖ فَٱللَّهُ خَيْرٌ حَـٰفِظًا ۖ وَهُوَ أَرْحَمُ ٱلرَّٰحِمِينَ

He said, “Should I trust you with him except as I trusted you with his brother before? But Allah ﷻ is the best guardian, and He is the most merciful of the merciful.”

Ya’qub (AS) responds, and his pain breaks through: قَالَ هَلْ ءَامَنُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ إِلَّا كَمَآ أَمِنتُكُمْ عَلَىٰٓ أَخِيهِ مِن قَبْلُ - “Should I trust you with him except as I trusted you with his brother before?”

He lost his composure for a moment. The wound is still fresh. You think I forgot? You’re asking me to trust you with him just like I trusted you with Yusuf (AS)?

But immediately, Ya’qub (AS) catches himself and turns back to Allah ﷻ. This is the remarkable character of a prophet—even when emotions overwhelm him, he redirects himself to his Lord.

فَٱللَّهُ خَيْرٌ حَـٰفِظًا - “But Allah ﷻ is the best guardian.” Wait, I shouldn’t have lost my temper like that. The reality is that Allah ﷻ is the better protector. Just because Binyamin stays with me doesn’t mean he’s truly safe. Safety doesn’t come from physical proximity—it comes from Allah ﷻ.

وَهُوَ أَرْحَمُ ٱلرَّٰحِمِينَ - “And He is the most merciful of the merciful.” Forgive me for my outburst. You are the Most Merciful. I place my trust in You.

This is the nature of Ya’qub (AS). Even when he loses his cool, he immediately returns to Allah ﷻ. It’s a beautiful example of how believers handle their emotions.

Ayah 65

وَلَمَّا فَتَحُوا۟ مَتَـٰعَهُمْ وَجَدُوا۟ بِضَـٰعَتَهُمْ رُدَّتْ إِلَيْهِمْ ۖ قَالُوا۟ يَـٰٓأَبَانَا مَا نَبْغِى ۖ هَـٰذِهِۦ بِضَـٰعَتُنَا رُدَّتْ إِلَيْنَا ۖ وَنَمِيرُ أَهْلَنَا وَنَحْفَظُ أَخَانَا وَنَزْدَادُ كَيْلَ بَعِيرٍ ۖ ذَٰلِكَ كَيْلٌ يَسِيرٌ

And when they opened their baggage, they found their merchandise returned to them. They said, “O our father, what more could we desire? This is our merchandise returned to us. We will supply our family and protect our brother and obtain an increase of a camel’s load. That is an easy measurement.”

وَلَمَّا فَتَحُوا۟ مَتَـٰعَهُمْ - when they opened their baggage back home, وَجَدُوا۟ بِضَـٰعَتَهُمْ رُدَّتْ إِلَيْهِمْ - they found their merchandise had been returned to them.

قَالُوا۟ يَـٰٓأَبَانَا مَا نَبْغِى - “O our father, what more could we desire?” This is an exclamation of amazement. مَا نَبْغِى literally means “what are we seeking?” or “what more could we possibly ask for?” It’s like saying, “Dad, come on! What more could we want?”

هَـٰذِهِۦ بِضَـٰعَتُنَا رُدَّتْ إِلَيْنَا - “This is our merchandise returned to us!” Not only did we get the grain, but we got our money back too!

Now watch how they structure their argument. They’re trying to convince their skeptical father, so every phrase is calculated:

وَنَمِيرُ أَهْلَنَا - “We will supply our family.” The verb نَمِيرُ specifically means to feed or provide for your family. Grammatically, you don’t need to add أَهْلَنَا (“our family”) because it’s already implied in the verb. But they add it anyway for emphasis. Why? Because they know their father doesn’t trust them.
