So they’re saying: we’ll feed our family—really, our family, not just ourselves. We’re thinking about everyone at home.

وَنَحْفَظُ أَخَانَا - “And we will protect our brother.” There’s that word حَفِظ again—guardian, protector.

وَنَزْدَادُ كَيْلَ بَعِيرٍ - “And we will obtain an increase of a camel’s load.” We’ll all get more—an entire extra camel’s worth of provisions just for bringing him.

ذَٰلِكَ كَيْلٌ يَسِيرٌ - “That is an easy measurement.” This is easy money, Dad! It’s a no-brainer. Why would you turn this down? We don’t have to do anything except bring our brother along.

They’re building their case: Look at this incredible deal we got, look how trustworthy these people are, look at the extra provisions we’ll receive, look how easy this is. Every argument is designed to break down their father’s resistance.

Ayah 66

قَالَ لَنْ أُرْسِلَهُۥ مَعَكُمْ حَتَّىٰ تُؤْتُونِ مَوْثِقًا مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ لَتَأْتُنَّنِى بِهِۦٓ إِلَّآ أَن يُحَاطَ بِكُمْ ۖ فَلَمَّآ ءَاتَوْهُ مَوْثِقَهُمْ قَالَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ مَا نَقُولُ وَكِيلٌ

He said, “I will never send him with you until you give me a promise by Allah ﷻ that you will bring him back to me, unless you are overwhelmed.” And when they had given him their promise, he said, “Allah ﷻ is Witness over what we say.”

Ya’qub (AS) finally responds: قَالَ لَنْ أُرْسِلَهُۥ مَعَكُمْ - “I will never send him with you.” The لَنْ indicates emphatic future negation—absolutely not, I will not do this.

حَتَّىٰ تُؤْتُونِ مَوْثِقًا مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ - “Until you give me a covenant from Allah ﷻ.” The word مَوْثِق is a very strong term for a binding agreement, even stronger than مِيثَاق. It’s derived from وَثِقَ, meaning to be firm, reliable, trustworthy. A مَوْثِق is the most serious kind of promise, one that invokes Allah ﷻ as witness.

لَتَأْتُنَّنِى بِهِۦٓ - “That you will surely bring him back to me.” The emphasis in the Arabic construction (with the doubled نّ) shows the intensity of the oath required.

إِلَّآ أَن يُحَاطَ بِكُمْ - “Unless you are overwhelmed.” The phrase يُحَاطَ بِكُمْ literally means “you are surrounded” or “you are overcome.” Ya’qub (AS) understands the reality of the times. Economic hardship breeds crime. When times are tough, caravans get looted. Desperate people do desperate things. He’s worried that when they travel, they might be robbed or attacked, and something could happen beyond their control. Outside of such a catastrophic situation, they must bring Binyamin back.

فَلَمَّآ ءَاتَوْهُ مَوْثِقَهُمْ - “And when they had given him their promise.” They agreed. They took the oath. They gave him the strongest possible covenant before Allah ﷻ.

قَالَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ مَا نَقُولُ وَكِيلٌ - “He said, ‘Allah ﷻ is Witness over what we say.’” The word وَكِيل (wakil) means the one who takes care of affairs, the disposer, the executor, the trustee. Ya’qub (AS) is saying: Whatever we have agreed upon here today, Allah ﷻ is the One who will take care of it. He is the One who will execute it. He is watching over this agreement.

This is Ya’qub (AS) placing the matter entirely in Allah’s hands, even as he secures the strongest human guarantee possible from his sons.

Ayah 67

وَقَالَ يَـٰبَنِىَّ لَا تَدْخُلُوا۟ مِنۢ بَابٍ وَٰحِدٍ وَٱدْخُلُوا۟ مِنْ أَبْوَٰبٍ مُّتَفَرِّقَةٍ ۖ وَمَآ أُغْنِى عَنكُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ مِن شَىْءٍ ۖ إِنِ ٱلْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ ۖ عَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْتُ ۖ وَعَلَيْهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ ٱلْمُتَوَكِّلُونَ

And he said, “O my sons, do not enter from one gate but enter from different gates. And I cannot avail you against Allah ﷻ at all. The decision is only for Allah ﷻ; upon Him I have relied, and upon Him let those who would rely place their trust.”

Before they departed, Ya’qub (AS) gave them practical advice, now speaking as a loving, concerned father: وَقَالَ يَـٰبَنِىَّ - “And he said, ‘O my sons.’” The tenderness in يَـٰبَنِىَّ is palpable—“my dear sons.”

لَا تَدْخُلُوا۟ مِنۢ بَابٍ وَٰحِدٍ وَٱدْخُلُوا۟ مِنْ أَبْوَٰبٍ مُّتَفَرِّقَةٍ - “Do not enter from one gate, but enter from different gates that are far apart.”

Why this instruction? If people see ten or eleven men traveling together, riding ten or eleven camels loaded with goods, they’ll immediately identify them as a wealthy caravan. In times of economic hardship, such a group becomes a target for criminals. Scattered across different entry points, they won’t attract the same attention. They won’t catch the eyes of those looking for someone to rob.

Some commentators suggest another wisdom: protection from the evil eye. Ten strong sons from one family—that’s remarkable, and jealousy could bring harm. But the former explanation fits better with what Ya’qub (AS) has already said: إِلَّآ أَن يُحَاطَ بِكُمْ - “unless you are overwhelmed.” He’s concerned about their physical safety.

The key phrase is أَبْوَٰبٍ مُّتَفَرِّقَةٍ - gates that are متفرقة, meaning separated, far apart. Not gates that are ten feet from each other where people could still tell they’re together—gates far enough apart that no one connects them as one group.

But immediately after giving this worldly advice, Ya’qub (AS) makes a critical spiritual point: وَمَآ أُغْنِى عَنكُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ مِن شَىْءٍ - “And I cannot avail you against Allah ﷻ at all.”

I’m not saying that entering from different gates will save you if Allah ﷻ has decreed something else. I’m not claiming that this precaution makes you independent of Allah’s protection. If Allah ﷻ has planned something, my advice won’t prevent it.

إِنِ ٱلْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ - “The decision is only for Allah ﷻ.” The final verdict, the ultimate decree, belongs to no one except Allah ﷻ.

This is the perfect balance between taking precautions and relying on Allah ﷻ—the concept often expressed as “tie your camel and trust in Allah ﷻ.” Yes, take practical steps. Use your intelligence. Make smart decisions. But never imagine that those steps are what truly protect you. Protection comes from Allah ﷻ alone.

عَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْتُ - “Upon Him I have relied.” Ya’qub (AS) personalizes it first: I have placed my trust in Him.

وَعَلَيْهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ ٱلْمُتَوَكِّلُونَ - “And upon Him let those who would rely place their trust.” Then he extends it universally: and everyone who wants to place their trust in someone should place it in Him alone.

Ayah 68

وَلَمَّا دَخَلُوا۟ مِنْ حَيْثُ أَمَرَهُمْ أَبُوهُم مَّا كَانَ يُغْنِى عَنْهُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ مِن شَىْءٍ إِلَّا حَاجَةً فِى نَفْسِ يَعْقُوبَ قَضَىٰهَا ۚ وَإِنَّهُۥ لَذُو عِلْمٍ لِّمَا عَلَّمْنَـٰهُ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ

And when they entered from where their father had ordered them, it did not avail them against Allah ﷻ at all, except for a need within the soul of Ya’qub (AS), which he satisfied. And indeed, he was a possessor of knowledge because of what We had taught him, but most people do not know.

وَلَمَّا دَخَلُوا۟ مِنْ حَيْثُ أَمَرَهُمْ أَبُوهُم- “And when they entered from where their father had ordered them.” They obeyed. They entered Egypt from different gates as instructed.

مَّا كَانَ يُغْنِى عَنْهُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ مِن شَىْءٍ - “It did not avail them against Allah ﷻ at all.”

Allah ﷻ confirms what Ya’qub (AS) had just said. If Allah ﷻ had decreed something else, entering from different gates wouldn’t have made any difference whatsoever. This precaution had no power to change Allah’s decree.

So why did Ya’qub (AS) tell them to do it? إِلَّا حَاجَةً فِى نَفْسِ يَعْقُوبَ قَضَىٰهَا - “Except for a need within the soul of Ya’qub, which he satisfied.”

This was a حَاجَة - a need, a desire, a concern deep in Ya’qub’s heart. As a father, as someone who understood the dangers of travel in difficult times, he felt they should take extra precautions. So he fulfilled that inner need by giving them this advice.

Now, Allah ﷻ doesn’t dismiss this as foolishness or empty worry. Watch what comes next: وَإِنَّهُۥ لَذُو عِلْمٍ لِّمَا عَلَّمْنَـٰهُ - “And indeed, he was a possessor of knowledge because of what We had taught him.”

Allah ﷻ validates Ya’qub’s concern. This wasn’t just paranoia or baseless fear. Ya’qub (AS) had knowledge—knowledge that Allah ﷻ had given him. His advice was grounded in wisdom and understanding of how the world works. Taking reasonable precautions is part of the knowledge Allah ﷻ teaches His prophets and His believing servants.

So there are two truths held in balance: (1) No precaution can override Allah’s decree, and (2) Taking wise precautions is part of knowledge and doesn’t contradict reliance on Allah ﷻ.

وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ - “But most people do not know.” Most people don’t understand this balance. They don’t know when to take precautions and when to recognize that ultimately everything is in Allah’s hands. They don’t possess the knowledge that comes from divine teaching.

Ayah 69

وَلَمَّا دَخَلُوا۟ عَلَىٰ يُوسُفَ ءَاوَىٰٓ إِلَيْهِ أَخَاهُ ۖ قَالَ إِنِّىٓ أَنَا۠ أَخُوكَ فَلَا تَبْتَئِسْ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ

And when they entered upon Yusuf (AS), he took his brother to himself, saying, “Indeed, I am your brother, so do not despair over what they used to do.”

وَلَمَّا دَخَلُوا۟ عَلَىٰ يُوسُفَ - “And when they entered upon Yusuf.” They’ve arrived in Egypt again, and this time Binyamin is with them.

ءَاوَىٰٓ إِلَيْهِ أَخَاهُ - “He took his brother to himself.” The verb آوَىٰ means to give refuge, to pull someone close to protect them from danger.

Imagine this moment. Yusuf (AS) sees his full brother—the only other son of his mother—standing among the very brothers who hurt him years ago. There’s an immediate, overwhelming urge to protect Binyamin. Yusuf (AS) feels that as long as Binyamin is standing with them, he’s in danger. So he pulls him away, gives him refuge close to himself.

He must have found a private moment, away from the others. قَالَ إِنِّىٓ أَنَا۠ أَخُوكَ - “He said, ‘Indeed, I am your brother.’”

Notice the emphasis: إِنِّىٓ أَنَا۠ - “Indeed, I…” The pronoun أَنَا is technically unnecessary in Arabic because the verb already indicates “I,” but it’s added for intense emphasis. “It’s really me. I am your brother. It’s actually me—Yusuf.”

Can you imagine Binyamin’s shock? The powerful minister of Egypt, the man everyone fears and respects, pulls you aside and tells you he’s your long-lost brother?

They must have embraced. Tears must have flowed. All the years of grief, all the pain, all the loss—it comes flooding out.

فَلَا تَبْتَئِسْ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ - “So do not despair over what they used to do.”

The verb تَبْتَئِسْ comes from بُؤْس, meaning misery, grief, distress. لَا تَبْتَئِسْ means “don’t be grieved,” “don’t be disturbed,” “don’t despair.”

This tells us something: they had been doing things to Binyamin too. The mistreatment didn’t stop with Yusuf (AS). When you meet someone you can finally trust after years of difficulty, everything comes pouring out. Binyamin must have started sharing his pain, and Yusuf (AS) comforts him: “It’s okay. It’s okay now. I’m here. I’ve got you, little brother. Don’t grieve anymore over what they’ve been doing.”

This is a moment of profound reunion and protection. The older brother, who once couldn’t protect himself, now has the power to protect his younger brother. And he’s determined to do exactly that.

Ayah 70

فَلَمَّا جَهَّزَهُم بِجَهَازِهِمْ جَعَلَ ٱلسِّقَايَةَ فِى رَحْلِ أَخِيهِ ثُمَّ أَذَّنَ مُؤَذِّنٌ أَيَّتُهَا ٱلْعِيرُ إِنَّكُمْ لَسَـٰرِقُونَ

So when he had furnished them with their supplies, he put the drinking cup into his brother’s bag. Then an announcer called out, “O caravan, indeed you are thieves.”

فَلَمَّا جَهَّزَهُم بِجَهَازِهِمْ - “So when he had furnished them with their supplies.” The same phrase used before—Yusuf (AS) loaded them up with provisions for their journey home.

But then: جَعَلَ ٱلسِّقَايَةَ فِى رَحْلِ أَخِيهِ- “He put the drinking cup into his brother’s bag.”

The word السِّقَايَة comes from سَقَى, to give water, to provide drink. A سِقَايَة is a special drinking vessel, specifically a goblet or cup used by someone of elite status. It’s not just any cup—it’s the kind you hold while someone else pours for you. This was a valuable item, likely made of precious metal.

Yusuf (AS) placed this royal cup into the رَحْل —the travel bag, the packed luggage—of his own brother Binyamin.

ثُمَّ أَذَّنَ مُؤَذِّنٌ - “Then an announcer called out.”

Who is this مُؤَذِّن? In those days, they didn’t have electronic security systems. When someone stole something, you needed a person whose job was to raise the alarm. This was his entire function—he was the security system, the alarm. And he’d been waiting for this moment. Now he finally gets to do his job.

أَيَّتُهَا ٱلْعِيرُ إِنَّكُمْ لَسَـٰرِقُونَ - “O caravan, indeed you are thieves!”

The عِير is a caravan, the traveling party with their loaded camels. لَسَـٰرِقُونَ - the لَ adds emphasis: you definitely are thieves.

Now remember, these brothers—what do they call themselves? They’re tough guys, right? They have a reputation. So they don’t just slink away or cower. No—watch their response.

Ayah 71

قَالُوا۟ وَأَقْبَلُوا۟ عَلَيْهِم مَّاذَا تَفْقِدُونَ

They said, while approaching them, “What is it you are missing?”

قَالُوا۟ وَأَقْبَلُوا۟ عَلَيْهِم - “They said, while approaching them.”

They didn’t just respond verbally. أَقْبَلُوا عَلَيْهِم - they turned toward them, they approached, they came at them. It’s confrontational. They stuck their chests out. Who are you calling thieves?

مَّاذَا تَفْقِدُونَ - “What is it you are missing?” What are you talking about? What do you think we took?

Their body language and tone convey: We know who we are. We don’t steal. You better explain yourself.

Ayah 72

قَالُوا۟ نَفْقِدُ صُوَاعَ ٱلْمَلِكِ وَلِمَن جَآءَ بِهِۦ حِمْلُ بَعِيرٍ وَأَنَا۠ بِهِۦ زَعِيمٌ

They said, “We are missing the measure of the king. And for he who produces it is the reward of a camel’s load, and I am responsible for it.”

قَالُوا۟ نَفْقِدُ صُوَاعَ ٱلْمَلِكِ - “They said, ‘We are missing the king’s measuring cup.’”

Now the word changes from سِقَايَة to صُوَاع. What’s the difference? صُوَاع is more specific—it’s a vessel made of gold or silver, something extremely valuable. And it’s not just described as “a cup”—it’s صُوَاعَ ٱلْمَلِكِ, the king’s cup.

This detail reveals how serious the theft is. Yusuf (AS) didn’t just take one of his own cups and plant it. He took the king’s personal cup. This is a major crime.

وَلِمَن جَآءَ بِهِۦ حِمْلُ بَعِيرٍ - “And for he who produces it is the reward of a camel’s load.”

If anyone rats out the thief or returns the cup, they’ll receive an extra camel’s worth of provisions. It’s a reward for information.

وَأَنَا۠ بِهِۦ زَعِيمٌ - “And I am responsible for it.” The announcer himself guarantees the reward. I’m personally vouching for this. The word زَعِيم means someone who takes responsibility, a guarantor. You’ll definitely get your reward—I stake my reputation on it.

But there’s another layer to زَعِيم. It can also mean: I’m completely confident. I know you’re the thief. I’m certain of it.

Ayah 73

قَالُوا۟ تَٱللَّهِ لَقَدْ عَلِمْتُم مَّا جِئْنَا لِنُفْسِدَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا كُنَّا سَـٰرِقِينَ

They said, “By Allah ﷻ, you have certainly known that we did not come to cause corruption in the land, and we have not been thieves.”

The brothers respond with a powerful oath: قَالُوا۟ تَٱللَّهِ - “They said, ‘By Allah ﷻ!’”

This isn’t the normal oath form وَاللَّهِ. When you’re shocked, when you’re emphatic, you use تَٱللَّهِ. It’s stronger, more intense. “I swear by Allah ﷻ!”

لَقَدْ عَلِمْتُم - “You have certainly known.” The لَقَدْ adds emphasis—you already know this, you know it well.

مَّا جِئْنَا لِنُفْسِدَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ - “We did not come to cause corruption in the land, to make trouble.”

وَمَا كُنَّا سَـٰرِقِينَ - “And we have not been thieves.” كُنَّا refers to our continuous state, our character. We haven’t been thieves—not now, not before, not ever. This isn’t who we are.

Their argument is logical: We came here last year too. Did we cause any trouble? Did we steal anything? Why would we come back and do something like this? If we were going to steal, would we really come back to the same place? Think about it!

Ayah 74

قَالُوا۟ فَمَا جَزَٰٓؤُهُۥٓ إِن كُنتُمْ كَـٰذِبِينَ

They said, “Then what would be its recompense if you should be liars?”

The Egyptian officials respond: قَالُوا۟ فَمَا جَزَٰٓؤُهُۥٓ إِن كُنتُمْ كَـٰذِبِينَ - “Then what should be the recompense if you are found to be liars?”

Alright, you’re very confident. You’re swearing by God that you’re innocent. So what should happen if it turns out you’re lying? What should be the punishment?

This is a brilliant legal move. They’re getting the accused to set their own punishment.

Ayah 75

قَالُوا۟ جَزَٰٓؤُهُۥ مَن وُجِدَ فِى رَحْلِهِۦ فَهُوَ جَزَٰٓؤُهُۥ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

They said, “Its recompense is that he in whose bag it is found—he himself will be its recompense. Thus do we recompense the wrongdoers.”

The brothers answer: قَالُوا۟ جَزَٰٓؤُهُۥ مَن وُجِدَ فِى رَحْلِهِۦ فَهُوَ جَزَٰٓؤُهُۥ - “Its recompense is that whoever it’s found in his bag—he himself will be the recompense.”

This is the law they grew up with, the teaching passed down in the household of Ya’qub (AS): if you’re caught stealing, you become the servant of the person you stole from. You yourself are the compensation. Your freedom is forfeited.

كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ - “That is how we deal with wrongdoers back where we come from.” That’s the justice system in our land, among our people—the Bedouin way, the way of our father Ya’qub (AS).

They’ve just sealed the arrangement. They’ve set the terms themselves. Whatever happens next, they agreed to it.

Ayah 76

فَبَدَأَ بِأَوْعِيَتِهِمْ قَبْلَ وِعَآءِ أَخِيهِ ثُمَّ ٱسْتَخْرَجَهَا مِن وِعَآءِ أَخِيهِ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ كِدْنَا لِيُوسُفَ ۖ مَا كَانَ لِيَأْخُذَ أَخَاهُ فِى دِينِ ٱلْمَلِكِ إِلَّآ أَن يَشَآءَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ نَرْفَعُ دَرَجَـٰتٍ مَّن نَّشَآءُ ۗ وَفَوْقَ كُلِّ ذِى عِلْمٍ عَلِيمٌ

So he began with their bags before his brother’s bag; then he extracted it from his brother’s bag. Thus did We plan for Yusuf (AS). He could not have taken his brother within the religion of the king except that Allah ﷻ willed. We raise in degrees whom We will, but over every possessor of knowledge is one more knowing.

فَبَدَأَ بِأَوْعِيَتِهِمْ قَبْلَ وِعَآءِ أَخِيهِ - “So he began with their bags before his brother’s bag.”

The وِعَاء is a container, a vessel, a bag. The official started searching their luggage. But smartly, he doesn’t go straight to Binyamin’s bag. That would be too suspicious. How would you know which bag to check? So he goes through them one by one. He’s taking his time, looking through each bag carefully.

ثُمَّ ٱسْتَخْرَجَهَا مِن وِعَآءِ أَخِيهِ - “Then he extracted it from his brother’s bag.”

ٱسْتَخْرَجَ means to extract after searching, after digging around. It’s not أَخْرَجَ, which is just to take out. ٱسْتَخْرَجَ implies he’s searching, looking carefully. “Where is it? I don’t see it here… wait, what’s this? Let me pull this out…” He’s acting the whole scene.

Then comes Allah’s comment on this entire plan: كَذَٰلِكَ كِدْنَا لِيُوسُفَ - “Thus did We plan for Yusuf.”

This word كِدْنَا - “We planned,” “We schemed,” “We devised a strategy.” Some people are troubled by this. Yusuf (AS) lied, he planted evidence, he framed his own brother as a thief. How can this be right?

But Allah ﷻ Himself says: كِدْنَا - We planned this. This wasn’t Yusuf’s idea. This was divine instruction, divine revelation. Allah ﷻ devised this plan and revealed it to Yusuf (AS). We saw something similar with Ibrahim (AS) when he destroyed the idols and said the big one did it—that wasn’t Ibrahim’s cleverness, that was Allah’s instruction to him.

Why was this necessary? مَا كَانَ لِيَأْخُذَ أَخَاهُ فِى دِينِ ٱلْمَلِكِ - “He could not have taken his brother within the constitution of the king.”

The دِين here means the law, the system, the constitution. Notice: Yusuf (AS) served under the دِينِ ٱلْمَلِكِ - the king’s legal system—even though his personal دِين as a Muslim was different. This teaches us that دِين has two dimensions: personal religion and collective legal system. He was a Muslim individually, but he operated within the constitutional framework of Egypt.

Under Egyptian law, there was no way to keep Binyamin. Immigration was restricted because of the famine. But under the law of Ya’qub’s people—the law the brothers themselves just invoked—a thief becomes the slave of the victim. This was the only legal way.

إِلَّآ أَن يَشَآءَ ٱللَّهُ - “Except that Allah ﷻ willed.” Had Allah ﷻ not devised this plan for him, there would have been no way.

نَرْفَعُ دَرَجَـٰتٍ مَّن نَّشَآءُ - “We raise in degrees whom We will.”

Think about it: false accusations are normally humiliating. Yusuf (AS) has already experienced the humiliation of false accusation when he was thrown in prison. But Allah ﷻ can take the same situation—an accusation of theft—and use it to raise someone’s rank, to elevate them, to accomplish their goals. Allah ﷻ raises the ranks of whomever He wills, through whatever means He chooses.

وَفَوْقَ كُلِّ ذِى عِلْمٍ عَلِيمٌ - “But over every possessor of knowledge is one more knowing.”

At this point in the story, Yusuf (AS) is recognized as the most knowledgeable person in Egypt. He interpreted dreams no one else could interpret. He devised an economic plan that saved the nation. Everyone acknowledges his brilliance.

But even Yusuf (AS)—how would he have figured out how to keep his brother? He didn’t know. Allah ﷻ revealed the plan to him: كِدْنَا لِيُوسُفَ - We planned it out for Yusuf.

So no matter how much knowledge you possess, there is always someone who knows more. And above all who possess knowledge is Allah ﷻ, the All-Knowing.

This is a beautiful principle for all of us. You’ll learn things in life. You’ll specialize. You’ll become an expert in your field. And just when you think you know something, you’ll meet someone who makes your knowledge look like nothing. This happens constantly. And it should keep us humble. Above every person of knowledge is someone with greater knowledge, and above all of them is Allah ﷻ, the source of all knowledge.

Ayah 77–90 · linked video: 11. Yusuf (Ayah 77-90)

Surah Yusuf: Ayahs 77-90

Ayah 77

قَالُوٓا۟ إِن يَسْرِقْ فَقَدْ سَرَقَ أَخٌ لَّهُۥ مِن قَبْلُ ۚ فَأَسَرَّهَا يُوسُفُ فِى نَفْسِهِۦ وَلَمْ يُبْدِهَا لَهُمْ ۚ قَالَ أَنتُمْ شَرٌّ مَّكَانًا ۖ وَٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا تَصِفُونَ

When the brothers were confronted with evidence that Benjamin had the king’s cup in his possession, their response reveals a striking grammatical choice that exposes their lingering hostility. They said, “If he stole, then a brother of his stole before.” Notice the structure here: إِن يَسْرِقْ فَقَدْ سَرَقَ أَخٌ لَّهُۥ.

The word أَخٌ is kept indefinite—“a brother”—rather than making it proper by saying أَخُوهُ, “his brother.” This subtle grammatical choice reflects their continued reluctance to acknowledge Yusuf (AS) with any dignity or specificity. They won’t even name him. It’s as if they’re saying, “Some brother he had,” keeping the reference vague and dismissive even after all these years.

Some narrations suggest this refers to an incident from Yusuf’s childhood. Perhaps his caretaker, deeply attached to him, falsely accused him of stealing something so she could keep him with her longer until she passed away, after which he returned to Yaqub (AS). Whether this incident actually occurred or the brothers simply fabricated it in the moment, the accusation stands as a direct insult to both Yusuf and Benjamin.

Consider the dramatic irony of this moment. Just moments earlier, these same brothers had declared their policy: whoever is found with stolen goods shall be detained. They made this declaration with absolute confidence, never imagining it would apply to their own brother. Now, when their own words trap Benjamin, they resort to character assassination—not just of Benjamin, but of Yusuf (AS) as well.

Yusuf (AS) is standing right there, hearing them slander him and his full brother to his face. This could have been the tipping point, the moment when everything came apart. The rage, the frustration, the years of injustice—all of it could have erupted right then. But notice what Allah ﷻ tells us: فَأَسَرَّهَا يُوسُفُ فِى نَفْسِهِۦ وَلَمْ يُبْدِهَا لَهُمْ —“Yusuf kept it to himself and did not reveal it to them.”

The verb أَسَرَّ means to hide something secret, and يُبْدِ means to make apparent. Allah ﷻ mentions both the hiding and the not revealing, emphasizing that Yusuf (AS) went out of his way to control himself. He didn’t just suppress his reaction—he actively concealed his emotions and deliberately chose not to respond. This required tremendous self-restraint at a moment when he had every worldly reason to expose them.

Instead, he responded only within himself: أَنتُمْ شَرٌّ مَّكَانًا —“You are worse in position.” The irony cuts deep. You accuse us of stealing? You, who stole a child? You, who destroyed a family? You, who threw an innocent boy into a well? You think you have the moral standing to judge anyone?

وَٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا تَصِفُونَ —“And Allah knows best what you describe.” The word تَصِفُونَ is plural and ongoing, indicating they didn’t just make one accusation. They kept going, describing and embellishing, perhaps adding details about how troubled Yusuf was as a child, how problematic that family was. And through it all, Yusuf (AS) held his composure, knowing that Allah ﷻ knew the truth of what they were saying.

Ayah 78

قَالُوا۟ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْعَزِيزُ إِنَّ لَهُۥٓ أَبًا شَيْخًا كَبِيرًا فَخُذْ أَحَدَنَا مَكَانَهُۥٓ ۖ إِنَّا نَرَىٰكَ مِنَ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ

Having failed to defend Benjamin through character assassination, the brothers shift tactics entirely. Now they appeal to Yusuf’s apparent compassion: يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْعَزِيزُ—“O Minister”—conveniently dropping all mention of their “worthless brother” to focus on what they think will work: sympathy for their father.

إِنَّ لَهُۥٓ أَبًا شَيْخًا كَبِيرًا —“Indeed, he has a father, aged and elderly.” Notice the redundancy: شَيْخًا already means old, but they add كَبِيرًا —very old, very senior. They’re emphasizing their father’s advanced age and frailty, trying to create an image of a helpless old man who will be devastated by this loss.

The irony here is staggering. These are the same men who, years ago, dismissed their father’s grief over Yusuf with cold indifference. Now they suddenly care deeply about their father’s emotional wellbeing? They’re essentially saying, “He’s really attached to this one. Our poor, elderly father won’t survive this loss.”

فَخُذْ أَحَدَنَا مَكَانَهُۥٓ —“Take one of us in his place.” They’re offering to substitute themselves, which sounds noble until you remember they’re the ones who created this situation in the first place. And notice: أَحَدَنَا —“one of us.” They’re not saying which one. They’re certainly not volunteering individually. It’s a vague, non-committal offer.

إِنَّا نَرَىٰكَ مِنَ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ —“Indeed, we see you as one of those who do good.” They’re flattering him, appealing to his reputation for excellence and generosity. Surely a man of your character and benevolence would do this favor for us?

Ayah 79

قَالَ مَعَاذَ ٱللَّهِ أَن نَّأْخُذَ إِلَّا مَن وَجَدْنَا مَتَـٰعَنَا عِندَهُۥٓ إِنَّآ إِذًا لَّظَـٰلِمُونَ

Yusuf’s response is swift and firm: مَعَاذَ ٱللَّهِ —“God forbid!” or more literally, “We seek Allah’s protection.” This is a strong expression of refusal, invoking Allah’s name to emphasize the impropriety of what they’re suggesting.

أَن نَّأْخُذَ إِلَّا مَن وَجَدْنَا مَتَـٰعَنَا عِندَهُۥٓ —“That we should take anyone except him with whom we found our goods.” He’s holding them to their own standard, their own declaration of policy. You were the ones who announced that whoever has the stolen goods should be detained. Now you want to change the rules?

إِنَّآ إِذًا لَّظَـٰلِمُونَ —“Indeed, we would then be wrongdoers.” This phrase carries particular weight. He’s essentially asking: Are you standing in the minister’s court, in front of all these witnesses, asking me to commit injustice? Are you suggesting I violate the very principle of justice that you yourselves declared?

The brothers realize they cannot make their case with him. He’s been kind and generous to them throughout this ordeal, but on this matter of justice, he will not bend. There’s a lesson here about true excellence (إحسان): it includes both generosity and justice, kindness and firmness. Excellence doesn’t mean being soft in every situation—sometimes it means upholding what is right even when it’s difficult.

Ayah 80

فَلَمَّا ٱسْتَيْـَٔسُوا۟ مِنْهُ خَلَصُوا۟ نَجِيًّا ۖ قَالَ كَبِيرُهُمْ أَلَمْ تَعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ أَبَاكُمْ قَدْ أَخَذَ عَلَيْكُم مَّوْثِقًا مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَمِن قَبْلُ مَا فَرَّطتُمْ فِى يُوسُفَ ۖ فَلَنْ أَبْرَحَ ٱلْأَرْضَ حَتَّىٰ يَأْذَنَ لِىٓ أَبِىٓ أَوْ يَحْكُمَ ٱللَّهُ لِى ۖ وَهُوَ خَيْرُ ٱلْحَـٰكِمِينَ

فَلَمَّا ٱسْتَيْـَٔسُوا۟ مِنْهُ —“When they had completely lost hope in him.” The verb ٱسْتَيْـَٔسُوا suggests reaching the absolute end of hope. They realize Yusuf will not change his mind, and they’re watching him as he himself seems to be losing his composure under the weight of what’s happening.

خَلَصُوا۟ نَجِيًّا —“They withdrew in private conference.” The verb خَلَصُوا comes from a root meaning “to be pure” or “free from mixture”—they separated themselves from the crowd, from the court, from the marketplace, and found a quiet corner where they could speak without being overheard.

قَالَ كَبِيرُهُمْ —“The eldest among them said.” This is likely the same brother who had tried to save Yusuf years ago by suggesting they throw him in the well instead of killing him. Now he speaks up again, but notice the accusatory tone:

أَلَمْ تَعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ أَبَاكُمْ قَدْ أَخَذَ عَلَيْكُم مَّوْثِقًا مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ —“Don’t you realize that your father already took from you a solemn pledge with Allah as witness?” He says “your father,” distancing himself slightly, and he’s reminding them: we made a covenant, we swore an oath with Allah’s name attached to it.

وَمِن قَبْلُ مَا فَرَّطتُمْ فِى يُوسُفَ —“And before this, how you failed in the case of Yusuf.” Here’s where it gets fascinating. He says فَرَّطتُمْ —“you failed”—not “we failed.” He’s pointing the finger at them, as if to say: you guys were the ones bent on killing him. I was trying to save him. This was your fault.

The word فَرَّطَ is typically used when you exceed proper limits, you transgress. But it can also mean to fall short of expectations. From Yusuf’s perspective, what they did was clearly transgression—they went beyond all bounds. But from their father’s perspective, they fell short—they failed to live up to what was expected of them as sons and as brothers. The word choice here reflects that they’re still thinking primarily about their father, about his expectations, about disappointing him.

فَلَنْ أَبْرَحَ ٱلْأَرْضَ —“I will never leave this land.” The verb أَبْرَحَ means to leave or depart from a place. Its opposite, بَرَزَ (baraza), means to come out into an open field. So أَبْرَحَ in the negative (لَنْ أَبْرَحَ) means “I will not leave”—but more than that, it means “I will hold my ground, I will remain stationed here.”

حَتَّىٰ يَأْذَنَ لِىٓ أَبِىٓ —“Until my father gives me permission.” He’s saying: I was the spokesperson before. I was the one who negotiated Yusuf’s fate. I was the one who convinced father to let Benjamin come. I’m not leaving until my father himself comes here and gives me permission to leave.

أَوْ يَحْكُمَ ٱللَّهُ لِى —“Or Allah gives judgment for me.” This is profound. He’s saying: When my father gives me permission, since he’s a prophet, he’ll make istighfar for me, he’ll pray for me, and through his dua, Allah will make things easy for me. Allah will give a verdict in my case.

وَهُوَ خَيْرُ ٱلْحَـٰكِمِينَ —“And He is the best of judges.” In the midst of this human drama, this reminder brings us back to the ultimate reality: Allah ﷻ is the one who truly judges, who truly decides, who brings all matters to their rightful conclusion.

Ayah 81

ٱرْجِعُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰٓ أَبِيكُمْ فَقُولُوا۟ يَـٰٓأَبَانَآ إِنَّ ٱبْنَكَ سَرَقَ وَمَا شَهِدْنَآ إِلَّا بِمَا عَلِمْنَا وَمَا كُنَّا لِلْغَيْبِ حَـٰفِظِينَ

ٱرْجِعُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰٓ أَبِيكُمْ —“Return to your father.” The eldest brother, having decided to stay, now instructs the others to go back and deliver the news.

فَقُولُوا۟ يَـٰٓأَبَانَآ إِنَّ ٱبْنَكَ سَرَقَ —“And say: O our father, indeed your son has stolen.” The phrasing is stark and direct: ٱبْنَكَ —“your son.” Not “our brother Benjamin,” not even “Benjamin.” Just “your son”—that one you care so much about. He stole.

وَمَا شَهِدْنَآ إِلَّا بِمَا عَلِمْنَا —“And we did not testify except to what we knew.” They’re trying to establish their credibility: we’re only reporting what we witnessed with our own eyes.

وَمَا كُنَّا لِلْغَيْبِ حَـٰفِظِينَ —“And we could not be guardians of the unseen.” This phrase does double work. On one level, they’re saying: we had no way of knowing he would steal. If we had known the unseen—that he had stolen—we never would have announced our policy that whoever steals stays behind. We didn’t guard against that unknown possibility.

But there’s another layer: we told you we’d guard him, but we couldn’t guard against what’s hidden, what’s in people’s hearts, what they might do when we’re not watching. We couldn’t protect against the unseen.

Ayah 82

وَسْـَٔلِ ٱلْقَرْيَةَ ٱلَّتِى كُنَّا فِيهَا وَٱلْعِيرَ ٱلَّتِىٓ أَقْبَلْنَا فِيهَا ۖ وَإِنَّا لَصَـٰدِقُونَ

وَسْـَٔلِ ٱلْقَرْيَةَ ٱلَّتِى كُنَّا فِيهَا —“And ask the town in which we were.” They’re suggesting Yaqub (AS) can verify their story by asking the townspeople—meaning the people of the town, the witnesses who saw the whole incident unfold.

وَٱلْعِيرَ ٱلَّتِىٓ أَقْبَلْنَا فِيهَا —“And the caravan in which we returned.” The caravan wasn’t just them—other people from their village had made the journey to Egypt and back. All these witnesses can confirm what happened.

وَإِنَّا لَصَـٰدِقُونَ —“And indeed, we are truthful.” Notice they don’t say this time, “You probably don’t believe us,” or “We know you think we’re lying.” They’re not even addressing his doubt directly anymore. They already know he doubts them. After what happened with Yusuf, how could he not? But they assert their truthfulness anyway, knowing full well it won’t be readily believed.

Ayah 83

قَالَ بَلْ سَوَّلَتْ لَكُمْ أَنفُسُكُمْ أَمْرًا ۖ فَصَبْرٌ جَمِيلٌ ۖ عَسَى ٱللَّهُ أَن يَأْتِيَنِى بِهِمْ جَمِيعًا ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلْعَلِيمُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ

قَالَ —Yaqub (AS) responds, and this time he doesn’t want to hear their explanations or investigate further. He doesn’t ask to speak to witnesses. He simply states his conclusion:

بَلْ سَوَّلَتْ لَكُمْ أَنفُسُكُمْ أَمْرًا —“Rather, your souls have enticed you to something.” We’ve heard these exact words before, haven’t we? When they first came back claiming Yusuf was eaten by a wolf, he said the same thing. Now, years later, here we go again. You’ve made something easy on your consciences. You’ve convinced yourselves of a convenient story.

فَصَبْرٌ جَمِيلٌ —“Then beautiful patience.” This isn’t even a complete sentence in Arabic. It’s just two words: ṣabrun jamīl. Patience—beautiful. He’s so hurt he can’t even form a full sentence. He’s not saying, “I will have beautiful patience” or “I should practice beautiful patience.” He’s just grasping at the concept itself: Beautiful patience. That’s all I have. That’s all I can hold onto.

عَسَى ٱللَّهُ أَن يَأْتِيَنِى بِهِمْ جَمِيعًا —“Perhaps Allah will bring them to me, all together.” The word عَسَى expresses hope tinged with uncertainty: perhaps, maybe, hopefully. And notice: بِهِمْ جَمِيعًا —all of them. Not just Benjamin. Not just his two youngest sons. All of them—including Yusuf, including the eldest who stayed behind. He’s hoping for complete reunion, complete restoration.

إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلْعَلِيمُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ —“Indeed, He is the All-Knowing, the All-Wise.” In his deepest grief, Yaqub (AS) anchors himself in Allah’s attributes. Allah knows what I don’t know. Allah’s wisdom operates in ways I cannot see. This is how a prophet deals with overwhelming loss—not by understanding everything, but by trusting the One who does.

Ayah 84

وَتَوَلَّىٰ عَنْهُمْ وَقَالَ يَـٰٓأَسَفَىٰ عَلَىٰ يُوسُفَ وَٱبْيَضَّتْ عَيْنَاهُ مِنَ ٱلْحُزْنِ فَهُوَ كَظِيمٌ

وَتَوَلَّىٰ عَنْهُمْ —“And he turned away from them.” Yaqub (AS) physically removes himself from his sons’ presence. He can’t bear to look at them, can’t continue the conversation. He needs to be alone with his grief.

وَقَالَ يَـٰٓأَسَفَىٰ عَلَىٰ يُوسُفَ —“And he said: O my grief over Yusuf!” Wait—they just told him about Benjamin being held back, and his eldest son refusing to return. Why is he crying about Yusuf?

The word أَسَف is crucial here. It’s not just sadness—it’s a specific type of grief mixed with anger and frustration over a situation you cannot change. If you could change it, you’d use غَضَب for anger or حُزْن for simple grief. But أَسَف is when you’re overpowered by circumstances, when grief and anger swirl together in helplessness.

This new loss—Benjamin being detained, his eldest son staying behind—has ripped open the old wound. The first loss comes flooding back. All these years, he’s been living with this grief, and now it’s multiplied. His emotional state has reached a breaking point, and the name that comes to his lips is Yusuf.

وَٱبْيَضَّتْ عَيْنَاهُ مِنَ ٱلْحُزْنِ —“And his eyes became white from grief.” This is a figure of speech for losing one’s sight. The overwhelming sorrow has taken a physical toll—he can no longer see.

فَهُوَ كَظِيمٌ—“And he was suppressing.” This word is remarkable. كَظِيم comes from كَظْم, which refers to the nasal passage. When you fill a container to the brim and cover it, the contents are كَظِيم—completely full, pressed down, held in.

We say in English, “I’ve had it up to here,” gesturing to our throat or forehead. That’s exactly what this word captures. Yaqub’s grief, anger, frustration, and longing have filled him completely—all the way up to his nostrils. He’s drowning in these emotions, and he’s trying to swallow them, hold them back, contain them.

Modern medicine tells us what the Quran knew all along: when people are emotionally stressed, depressed, carrying unresolved grief, it affects them physically. Blood pressure rises. Heart rate changes. The body breaks down. Everything physical is connected to everything emotional. The more you hold in, the more grief you live with, the sicker you become. This grief literally ate away at Yaqub (AS), manifesting in the loss of his sight and the deterioration of his physical health.

Ayah 85

قَالُوا۟ تَٱللَّهِ تَفْتَؤُا۟ تَذْكُرُ يُوسُفَ حَتَّىٰ تَكُونَ حَرَضًا أَوْ تَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْهَـٰلِكِينَ

قَالُوا۟—The sons respond, and their shock is palpable. They’re stunned by what they’re witnessing. It’s been years—many years—since Yusuf disappeared. They expect their father to have moved on by now.

تَٱللَّهِ—“By Allah!” They swear, which itself indicates how extraordinary this situation seems to them. Notice the grammatical choices here are unusual. The word تَفْتَأُ, which means to cease or stop, is rarely used in Arabic. The structure حَرَضًا, meaning so sick you’re close to death, is also uncommon. Even the oath form تَٱللَّهِ instead of the more typical وَاللَّهِ is rare.

Why all these unusual words? Because everything about this situation is strange to them, shocking, incomprehensible. The vocabulary itself reflects their bewilderment. It’s as if the language has to reach for rare expressions to capture how bizarre they find their father’s continued grief.

تَفْتَؤُا۟ تَذْكُرُ يُوسُفَ —“You will not cease remembering Yusuf.” You won’t stop. You will stop at nothing to remember him. It’s been years and you’re still talking about him?

حَتَّىٰ تَكُونَ حَرَضًا —“Until you become so consumed you’re at death’s door.” حَرَض means to be so sick you’re close to death, wasting away.

أَوْ تَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْهَـٰلِكِينَ —“Or you’ll be among those who perish,” meaning you’ll just tip over and die suddenly, a violent or sudden death.

They’re essentially saying: Get over it, old man. We’re standing here talking to you about your living sons who need you now, and you’re obsessing over someone who’s been gone for years. This is going to kill you.

Ayah 86

قَالَ إِنَّمَآ أَشْكُوا۟ بَثِّى وَحُزْنِىٓ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ وَأَعْلَمُ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ

Remember, Yaqub (AS) had already turned away from them. He went to be alone with his grief. But they followed him, invaded his privacy, and started criticizing how he’s handling his pain. So he responds, but he’s not really talking to them—he’s talking at them, explaining himself because they’ve forced the issue.

قَالَ إِنَّمَآ أَشْكُوا۟ بَثِّى وَحُزْنِىٓ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ—“He said: I only complain of my بَثّ and my حُزْن to Allah.”

What’s the difference between بَثّ and حُزْن? Both can be translated as grief or sorrow, but بَثّ is prolonged, deep sorrow that’s been with you a long time. حُزْن is more recent, fresher pain. He’s saying: I complain to Allah about my old grief—for Yusuf—and my new one—for Benjamin. بَثّ also carries the meaning of a grief that eats away at your insides, that tears you apart from within.

This statement is so beautiful it’s hard to fully translate. He’s saying: You think I’m just dwelling on the past? You think I’m not dealing with reality? No. I take my problems—all of them, old and new—to Allah ﷻ. That’s where I lodge my complaints. Not with you. Not with other people. With Allah.

وَأَعْلَمُ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ —“And I know from Allah what you do not know.” This is profound. He’s saying: I know how to have hope in Allah. I know how to take my pain to Him. I know things about relying on Allah that you haven’t learned yet. Perhaps I know something about Allah’s plan that you can’t see. I have knowledge from Allah that gives me a different perspective on all of this.

Ayah 87

يَـٰبَنِىَّ ٱذْهَبُوا۟ فَتَحَسَّسُوا۟ مِن يُوسُفَ وَأَخِيهِ وَلَا تَا۟يْـَٔسُوا۟ مِن رَّوْحِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ لَا يَا۟يْـَٔسُ مِن رَّوْحِ ٱللَّهِ إِلَّا ٱلْقَوْمُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ

Something has shifted. Eventually, Yaqub (AS) comes out of his private grief and speaks to his sons with a surprising instruction:

يَـٰبَنِىَّ —“O my sons.” He addresses them with affection despite everything.

ٱذْهَبُوا۟ فَتَحَسَّسُوا۟ مِن يُوسُفَ وَأَخِيهِ —“Go and search for Yusuf and his brother.” Wait. The first time they came back claiming Yusuf was dead, he didn’t tell them to go look for him. He knew they were lying, but he didn’t send them searching. Why now? What’s different?

Here’s what’s remarkable: he notices something different in them now. They have guilt. They’re changed. Before, there was no remorse, no conscience at work—there was no point trying to reason with them. But now, after years of living with what they did, after this new crisis with Benjamin, he sees guilt in them. He sees they’re not the same people they were. So now he can ask them to do something good, and they might actually do it.

تَحَسَّسُوا۟ means to search carefully, to be sensitive in your looking, to seek out with careful attention. Go look for Yusuf and his brother.

Notice who’s missing from this instruction: وَلَا تَا۟يْـَٔسُوا۟ مِن رَّوْحِ ٱللَّهِ —“And do not despair of Allah’s mercy.” The word رَوْح is related to رِيح (wind/breeze) and refers to a mercy that comes like a cool breeze, that brings relief, that takes away grief and sadness. It’s a refreshing mercy that revives you.

He tells them not to despair of this mercy, which means what? That they have despaired. They’ve lost hope. Particularly regarding the situation with the minister who won’t release Benjamin—they see no way out. But Yaqub (AS) says: Don’t lose hope in Allah’s mercy.

Then comes one of the most powerful statements in the entire Quran:

إِنَّهُۥ لَا يَا۟يْـَٔسُ مِن رَّوْحِ ٱللَّهِ إِلَّا ٱلْقَوْمُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ —“Indeed, no one despairs of Allah’s mercy except the disbelieving people.”

This statement resonates even in modern philosophy and theology. When you talk to someone who denies God’s existence, what’s the last thing on their mind? Divine mercy. They look at all the suffering in the world and conclude: What mercy? If there’s a God, He’s not merciful. Look at all the terrible things happening.

Yaqub (AS) identifies this as the root of disbelief: losing hope in Allah’s mercy. The believer, no matter how much pain they endure, no matter how impossible the situation seems, maintains hope in Allah’s mercy. That hope is what separates faith from disbelief.

Ayah 88

فَلَمَّا دَخَلُوا۟ عَلَيْهِ قَالُوا۟ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْعَزِيزُ مَسَّنَا وَأَهْلَنَا ٱلضُّرُّ وَجِئْنَا بِبِضَـٰعَةٍ مُّزْجَىٰةٍ فَأَوْفِ لَنَا ٱلْكَيْلَ وَتَصَدَّقْ عَلَيْنَآ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَجْزِى ٱلْمُتَصَدِّقِينَ

Now we’re back in Egypt. The brothers have returned, and they’re in a desperate state.

فَلَمَّا دَخَلُوا۟ عَلَيْهِ —“When they entered upon him.” They’re back in Yusuf’s presence, back in the minister’s court.

قَالُوا۟ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْعَزِيزُ —“They said: O Minister.”

مَسَّنَا وَأَهْلَنَا ٱلضُّرُّ —“Hardship has afflicted us and our family.” مَسَّ means to touch, but here it means to strike, to afflict. الضُّرُّ is need, difficulty, hardship. They’re saying: We’re suffering. Our families are suffering. The famine is taking its toll.

وَجِئْنَا بِبِضَـٰعَةٍ مُّزْجَىٰةٍ —“And we’ve come with goods that are مُزْجَى .” This word مُزْجَى means insignificant, of little value, almost worthless. They brought what they could—maybe some scraps, some minimal items—and they know it’s not enough. They’re essentially saying: We know what we brought isn’t worth much. We know it’s pathetic.

فَأَوْفِ لَنَا ٱلْكَيْلَ —“But please, give us full measure anyway.” Could you still fill our bags even though we didn’t bring equivalent payment?

وَتَصَدَّقْ عَلَيْنَآ —“And give us charity.” They’re asking for handouts now. They’ve been humbled completely. These proud brothers who once conspired to kill their sibling are now begging for charity.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَجْزِى ٱلْمُتَصَدِّقِينَ —“Indeed, Allah rewards those who give charity.” They’re trying to appeal to his faith, his sense of righteousness. You’re a good man, a righteous minister. Allah will reward you for helping us.

Ayah 89

قَالَ هَلْ عَلِمْتُم مَّا فَعَلْتُم بِيُوسُفَ وَأَخِيهِ إِذْ أَنتُمْ جَـٰهِلُونَ

What Yusuf (AS) says next changes everything:

قَالَ هَلْ عَلِمْتُم مَّا فَعَلْتُم بِيُوسُفَ وَأَخِيهِ —“He said: Do you know what you did to Yusuf and his brother?”

The question lands like thunder. How does he know about Yusuf? How does this Egyptian minister know about some missing brother from decades ago?

Notice he says “Yusuf and his brother.” We know what they did to Yusuf—they threw him in a well, sold him into slavery, destroyed his childhood. But what about “his brother”—meaning Benjamin? What did they do to him?

This is where we learn about the unspoken suffering. Benjamin lived with these brothers all these years. Yusuf (AS) has been living with his full brother now, and Benjamin has told him everything—the bullying, the beatings, the psychological torment, all the ways they made his life miserable after Yusuf disappeared.

إِذْ أَنتُمْ جَـٰهِلُونَ —“When you were ignorant.” This word جَـٰهِلُونَ doesn’t just mean lacking knowledge. It means being controlled by emotion, acting thoughtlessly, being foolish. You were so consumed by jealousy, so driven by your anger toward your father’s favoritism, that you became morons. You were completely taken over by your basest emotions.

Ayah 90

قَالُوٓا۟ أَءِنَّكَ لَأَنتَ يُوسُفُ ۖ قَالَ أَنَا۠ يُوسُفُ وَهَـٰذَآ أَخِى ۖ قَدْ مَنَّ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْنَآ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ مَن يَتَّقِ وَيَصْبِرْ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ

قَالُوٓا۟ أَءِنَّكَ لَأَنتَ يُوسُفُ —“They said: Are you indeed Yusuf?” The pieces are falling into place. The way he’s been so kind to them. The way he kept asking about their father and their brother. The way he’s known things he shouldn’t know. The question bursts out of them in shock and disbelief.

قَالَ أَنَا۠ يُوسُفُ وَهَـٰذَآ أَخِى —“He said: I am Yusuf, and this is my brother.”

Notice what he doesn’t say. He doesn’t say, “This is my brother Benjamin, whom you used to torment.” He doesn’t say, “These are my brothers” referring to them. He simply says: “I am Yusuf, and this—this one right here—is my brother.”

Remember when they used to say, “We are brothers, and these two…” separating Yusuf and Benjamin from the group? Now Yusuf (AS) returns the favor. You separated us back then? Well, guess what—this is my brother right here. The table has turned, but not in vengeance—in reality. His full brother is Benjamin, and that bond remains central.

قَدْ مَنَّ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْنَآ —“Allah has certainly favored us.” This is the moment where Yusuf (AS) could say, “Look at me now. Look at what I’ve become. Remember when you threw me in that well?” But he doesn’t do that. Instead, he immediately directs all attention and gratitude to Allah ﷻ. Allah favored us. Allah brought us here. Allah made this possible.

Then comes the key lesson, the summary of everything we’ve learned in this surah:

إِنَّهُۥ مَن يَتَّقِ وَيَصْبِرْ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ —“Indeed, whoever has تَقْوَىٰ and صَبْر, then indeed Allah does not waste the reward of the مُحْسِنِين.”

This is it. This is the formula. This is what the entire story has been teaching us:

تَقْوَىٰ —protecting yourself from Allah’s anger, being conscious of Him, conducting yourself with awareness of His presence.

صَبْر —patience, perseverance, holding firm through difficulty.

When you combine these two—when you’re conscious of Allah and you remain patient through trials—you reach إحسان, excellence. And Allah never wastes the reward of those who excel.

We’ve seen this theme again and again throughout Surah Yusuf. Every time someone practiced patience with taqwa, Allah elevated them. Yusuf (AS) in the well, in Potiphar’s house, in prison, in the palace—at every stage, taqwa and sabr led to ihsan.

فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ —“Indeed, Allah does not waste the reward of those who excel.” Allah doesn’t let it go to waste. Your patience isn’t pointless. Your taqwa isn’t in vain. Everything is seen, everything is recorded, and Allah will bring it to fruition in ways you cannot imagine.

That’s the road to excellence: combining consciousness of Allah with patience through hardship. That’s how you reach the station of those who excel. That’s the lesson of Yusuf (AS).

Ayah 91–108 · linked video: 12. Yusuf (Ayah 91-108)

Surah Yusuf: Ayahs 91-108

Ayah 91

قَالُوا۟ تَٱللَّهِ لَقَدْ ءَاثَرَكَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْنَا وَإِن كُنَّا لَخَـٰطِـِٔينَ

The brothers find themselves in yet another shocking situation. After revelation upon revelation, they finally speak: “By Allah ﷻ, He has certainly preferred you over us.” The phrase لَقَدْ ءَاثَرَكَ carries the weight of acknowledgment – Allah ﷻ has given Yusuf (AS) preference, power and honor above them.

Then comes their confession: وَإِن كُنَّا لَخَـٰطِـِٔينَ – “and indeed we were the ones who were mistaken.” The إِن here is actually the emphatic إِنَّ in shortened form. There is no doubt about it. They openly admit that all this time, they have made terrible mistakes. This isn’t a minor error they’re confessing to – these are grave sins that have shaped decades of suffering.

Ayah 92

قَالَ لَا تَثْرِيبَ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْيَوْمَ ۖ يَغْفِرُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ ۖ وَهُوَ أَرْحَمُ ٱلرَّٰحِمِينَ

Now we see the character of Yusuf (AS) shine through. He may not be the eldest brother, but in this moment, he demonstrates the spiritual maturity of true leadership. His response is immediate and powerful: لَا تَثْرِيبَ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْيَوْمَ – “No scolding over you today.”

The word تَثْرِيبَ means to scold someone, to hammer them over their mistakes, to hold their wrongdoing against them. It’s what some of us might experience when we forget our homework and face consequences. Yusuf (AS) declares there will be none of that. No punishment, no reprimand, no holding grudges. Today, no harm will come upon you.

Then he makes a supplication for them: يَغْفِرُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ – “May Allah ﷻ forgive you.” And he concludes with one of the most beautiful Names of Allah ﷻ: وَهُوَ أَرْحَمُ ٱلرَّٰحِمِينَ – “And He is the Most Merciful of those who show mercy.”

Ayah 93

ٱذْهَبُوا۟ بِقَمِيصِى هَـٰذَا فَأَلْقُوهُ عَلَىٰ وَجْهِ أَبِى يَأْتِ بَصِيرًا وَأْتُونِى بِأَهْلِكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ

There’s powerful irony in what happens next. Yusuf (AS) says: ٱذْهَبُوا۟ بِقَمِيصِى – “Take this shirt of mine.” Remember, they took a shirt to their father before, but that shirt was stained with lies, covered in false blood to deceive Ya’qub (AS). Shirts have caused considerable trouble in this family’s history.

This time, Yusuf (AS) makes sure nobody takes his shirt – he gives it. He’s in control of this narrative. “Take this shirt of mine,” he instructs, فَأَلْقُوهُ عَلَىٰ وَجْهِ أَبِى – “then throw it upon the face of my father.”

The brothers have likely already told him that their father has gone blind from grief and worry. So Yusuf (AS) sends his shirt with a promise: يَأْتِ بَصِيرًا. The word بَصِيرًا carries two beautiful meanings. First, it means Ya’qub (AS) will regain his physical sight. But it also means that when he comes to Egypt, he will have the joy of seeing his beloved son. As he travels toward Yusuf (AS), he will have that overwhelming happiness of being able to look upon him once more.

Then Yusuf (AS) extends an invitation to the entire family: وَأْتُونِى بِأَهْلِكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ – “and bring me your families, all of them.” He wants to see his nephews, his sisters-in-law, the extended family. It’s time for a reunion, a celebration. Now that everything is out in the open and forgiveness has been granted, there are no more barriers.

In immigration terms, he’s essentially sponsoring the entire family. Before, he couldn’t sponsor them because it would have revealed the secret. But now the truth is known, and everyone has their family visa to come to Egypt.

Ayah 94

وَلَمَّا فَصَلَتِ ٱلْعِيرُ قَالَ أَبُوهُمْ إِنِّى لَأَجِدُ رِيحَ يُوسُفَ ۖ لَوْلَآ أَن تُفَنِّدُونِ

One of the most beautiful passages in this Surah begins here. The word فَصَلَتِ means “to march forward, to begin a journey.” You would expect the ayah to say, “As the caravan approached Ya’qub (AS)…” But that’s not what Allah ﷻ tells us. The moment the caravan took its very first step leaving Egypt – at the start of the journey, not the end – Ya’qub’s heart felt something.

وَلَمَّا فَصَلَتِ ٱلْعِيرُ – when the caravan began its journey. قَالَ أَبُوهُمْ – their father, who is back home with his sons, far from Egypt, says something extraordinary: إِنِّى لَأَجِدُ رِيحَ يُوسُفَ – “I can certainly sense the scent of Yusuf.”

This time, the brothers are in Egypt with the most important cargo they’ve ever carried – the shirt. The previous shirts were tools of deception. This shirt carries truth. And as it begins its journey home, Ya’qub (AS) senses his son in the wind itself.

Then he adds: لَوْلَآ أَن تُفَنِّدُونِ – “if only you would not think me senile.” The word تُفَنِّدُونِ means to dismiss someone as too old, to think they’re just running their mouth without sense. Ya’qub (AS) knows what people will say, but he speaks his truth anyway.

Ayah 95

قَالُوا۟ تَٱللَّهِ إِنَّكَ لَفِى ضَلَـٰلِكَ ٱلْقَدِيمِ

And sure enough, those around him respond exactly as he anticipated. The sons who remained, along with others caring for Ya’qub (AS) in his old age, say: تَٱللَّهِ إِنَّكَ لَفِى ضَلَـٰلِكَ ٱلْقَدِيمِ – “By Allah ﷻ, you are still in that old confusion of yours.”

The word ضَلَـٰلِكَ means misguidedness, confusion, error. And قَدِيمِ means ancient, old. They’re essentially saying, “You’re still talking about Yusuf? After all these years, you haven’t moved on from that old delusion?” They think his grief has affected his mind, that his longing for Yusuf (AS) has caused him to imagine things.

Ayah 96

فَلَمَّآ أَن جَآءَ ٱلْبَشِيرُ أَلْقَىٰهُ عَلَىٰ وَجْهِهِۦ فَٱرْتَدَّ بَصِيرًا ۖ قَالَ أَلَمْ أَقُل لَّكُمْ إِنِّىٓ أَعْلَمُ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ

Notice the literary beauty here. The first time the brothers came to their father with a shirt, they came crying, weeping false tears. This time, the one who comes is called ٱلْبَشِيرُ – the bringer of good news.

And when he arrives, he doesn’t even speak. Their previous conversations with their father haven’t gone well. Words from their mouths carry no credibility anymore. So they let Yusuf’s shirt do the talking. أَلْقَىٰهُ عَلَىٰ وَجْهِهِۦ – he just threw it upon his face.

Ya’qub (AS) feels the shirt, and immediately فَٱرْتَدَّ بَصِيرًا – he returned to being able to see. The word ٱرْتَدَّ means “to return to what you were.” His sight comes flooding back.

And what does he say? Not “I told you Yusuf was alive.” He says something much more profound: أَلَمْ أَقُل لَّكُمْ إِنِّىٓ أَعْلَمُ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ – “Didn’t I tell you that I know from Allah ﷻ what you do not know?”

This was his position all along. This statement goes back to the very beginning of the Surah when he said those same words. His knowledge wasn’t based on evidence they could see or logic they could understand. It was based on his connection with Allah ﷻ, on what Allah ﷻ had revealed to his heart.

Ayah 97

قَالُوا۟ يَـٰٓأَبَانَا ٱسْتَغْفِرْ لَنَا ذُنُوبَنَآ إِنَّا كُنَّا خَـٰطِـِٔينَ

Now the brothers return and face their father directly. يَـٰٓأَبَانَا – “O our father,” ٱسْتَغْفِرْ لَنَا ذُنُوبَنَآ – “seek forgiveness for us, for our sins.”

They own it completely: إِنَّا كُنَّا خَـٰطِـِٔينَ – “Indeed, we were those who made mistakes.” We were the wrongdoers. This is their confession to their father, asking him to make istighfar for them, to seek Allah’s forgiveness on their behalf.

Ayah 98

قَالَ سَوْفَ أَسْتَغْفِرُ لَكُمْ رَبِّىٓ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلْغَفُورُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ

Ya’qub’s response is measured and wise: سَوْفَ أَسْتَغْفِرُ لَكُمْ رَبِّىٓ – “I will seek forgiveness for you from my Master.”

The word سَوْفَ indicates the future – “I will, but not right now.” He doesn’t make istighfar for them immediately. Perhaps the emotions are still too raw. Perhaps he needs time to process everything. Perhaps he wants to wait for a spiritually optimal time, like the last third of the night. Whatever the wisdom, he says, “Let me enjoy this moment of reunion first. This isn’t about you right now – it’s about my son and me thanking Allah ﷻ.”

Then he reminds them of Allah’s nature: إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلْغَفُورُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ – “Indeed, He is the Forgiving, the Merciful.” No matter how grave the sin, Allah ﷻ is ultimately the One who forgives, the One who shows mercy.

Ayah 99

فَلَمَّا دَخَلُوا۟ عَلَىٰ يُوسُفَ ءَاوَىٰٓ إِلَيْهِ أَبَوَيْهِ وَقَالَ ٱدْخُلُوا۟ مِصْرَ إِن شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ ءَامِنِينَ

The emotional reunion finally happens. The entire family – all the children, and Ya’qub’s wife – arrives in Egypt. When they enter upon Yusuf (AS), he does exactly what he did with his younger brother Binyamin years earlier. Remember how he pulled his brother close to himself? He does the same now: ءَاوَىٰٓ إِلَيْهِ أَبَوَيْهِ – “he took his two parents to himself,” embracing them.

Then he makes his official declaration: ٱدْخُلُوا۟ مِصْرَ إِن شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ ءَامِنِينَ – “Enter Egypt, if Allah ﷻ wills, in security.”

Notice the إِن شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ – even in this moment of joy and authority, Yusuf (AS) doesn’t forget to acknowledge Allah’s will. And the word ءَامِنِينَ means “in peace, in security.” You are now under this rule, citizens of Egypt, safe and protected.

Ayah 100

وَرَفَعَ أَبَوَيْهِ عَلَى ٱلْعَرْشِ وَخَرُّوا۟ لَهُۥ سُجَّدًا ۖ وَقَالَ يَـٰٓأَبَتِ هَـٰذَا تَأْوِيلُ رُءْيَـٰىَ مِن قَبْلُ قَدْ جَعَلَهَا رَبِّى حَقًّا ۖ وَقَدْ أَحْسَنَ بِىٓ إِذْ أَخْرَجَنِى مِنَ ٱلسِّجْنِ وَجَآءَ بِكُم مِّنَ ٱلْبَدْوِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ أَن نَّزَغَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ بَيْنِى وَبَيْنَ إِخْوَتِىٓ ۚ إِنَّ رَبِّى لَطِيفٌ لِّمَا يَشَآءُ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلْعَلِيمُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ

What Yusuf (AS) does next demonstrates his character beautifully. وَرَفَعَ أَبَوَيْهِ عَلَى ٱلْعَرْشِ – he elevated his two parents onto the throne. He, the minister of Egypt, steps down and places his mother and father on the seat of honor. They are the sun and moon of his childhood dream.

وَخَرُّوا۟ لَهُۥ سُجَّدًا – and they all fell before him in prostration. This was a form of honoring someone that was permissible in previous nations but would later be made forbidden in Islam. This wasn’t worship – it was a culturally accepted sign of deep respect.

Then the conversation that began at the start of the Surah comes full circle. Ya’qub (AS) and his son had a conversation about a dream in the opening passages. Now, after a lifetime of separation, that same conversation continues. Yusuf (AS) takes his father aside and embraces him: يَـٰٓأَبَتِ – “O my beloved father, my dear father.”

هَـٰذَا تَأْوِيلُ رُءْيَـٰىَ مِن قَبْلُ – “This is the interpretation of my dream from before.” قَدْ جَعَلَهَا رَبِّى حَقًّا – “My Master has made it come true.”

Now imagine: his father wants to know everything. What have you gone through? How was your life? How have you been? You must have experienced so much sadness, so much difficulty. He wants to catch up on all those lost years. The son could recount it all – being thrown off his brothers’ shoulders, waiting in the well, being sold in the marketplace, the incident with the minister’s wife, the false accusations, the politicians’ schemes, the years in prison, the cupbearer who forgot him.

But Yusuf (AS) doesn’t mention any of that. His father has already been through enough grief and pain. He needs to hear good things. So Yusuf (AS) summarizes his entire life in carefully chosen words:

وَقَدْ أَحْسَنَ بِىٓ إِذْ أَخْرَجَنِى مِنَ ٱلسِّجْنِ – “And He has been so good to me when He brought me out of prison.” He doesn’t even explain how he ended up in prison. His father doesn’t ask, and these brothers probably don’t question it either – “You were in prison? And then became minister? Interesting career path.”

وَجَآءَ بِكُم مِّنَ ٱلْبَدْوِ – “And He brought all of you from the Bedouin lands.” From the outskirts, the villages, the remote desert areas where they were living.

Then comes a statement of profound mercy and maturity: مِنۢ بَعْدِ أَن نَّزَغَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ بَيْنِى وَبَيْنَ إِخْوَتِىٓ – “After Shaytan had caused conflict between me and my brothers.”

Notice: he doesn’t say “between them and me.” He says “between me and my brothers,” claiming them as his brothers. He attributes the entire ordeal to Shaytan’s interference, not to their malice. This is the ultimate in good character and forgiveness.

إِنَّ رَبِّى لَطِيفٌ لِّمَا يَشَآءُ – “Indeed, my Master is subtle in whatever He wills.” The word لَطِيفٌ describes Allah’s planning as so subtle, so intricate, so hidden that you cannot detect it while it’s happening. You can’t see the connections, the wisdom, the way everything is being woven together. It’s covert, beyond perception. Yet when you look back, you see His perfect plan unfold.

إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلْعَلِيمُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ – “He is truly the All-Knowing, the All-Wise.”

These are the exact words Ya’qub (AS) used at the beginning of the Surah. His father told him, “Allah ﷻ is All-Knowing and All-Wise.” Now, after living through decades of difficulty and finally seeing the resolution, Yusuf (AS) says, “Dad, you were right. He really is All-Knowing and All-Wise. After looking back at my entire life, I can see that lesson you taught me in the beginning was absolutely true.”

Ayah 101

۞ رَبِّ قَدْ ءَاتَيْتَنِى مِنَ ٱلْمُلْكِ وَعَلَّمْتَنِى مِن تَأْوِيلِ ٱلْأَحَادِيثِ ۚ فَاطِرَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ أَنتَ وَلِىِّۦ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۖ تَوَفَّنِى مُسْلِمًا وَأَلْحِقْنِى بِٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ

Yusuf (AS) has just reunited with his family in a moment of great joy. But notice what he does immediately – he doesn’t credit any of this happiness to himself. In a situation of difficulty, when he was surrounded by those women trying to seduce him, he turned immediately to Allah ﷻ in conversation. Now, in a situation of great joy, he does the same: رَبِّ – “My Master.”

قَدْ ءَاتَيْتَنِى مِنَ ٱلْمُلْكِ – “You are the One who has given me authority and dominion. You granted that to me.”

وَعَلَّمْتَنِى مِن تَأْوِيلِ ٱلْأَحَادِيثِ – “And You taught me the interpretation of speech, of dreams, of narrations.” Had You not taught me, I would never have come out of prison. Had You not taught me, these people would have starved during the famine. Had You not taught me, nobody would have come to Egypt seeking help, and I would have never met my family again. It’s from what You taught me that this entire chain of events unfolded.

The word رَبِّ here – “My Master” – is the vocative form. He’s calling out to Allah ﷻ because what follows is a supplication, a deeply personal prayer.

فَاطِرَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ – “O Initiator, Creator, Molder of the heavens and the earth.” The word فَاطِرَ describes the One who brings things into existence from nothing, who shapes and fashions all of creation.

أَنتَ وَلِىِّۦ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ – “You are my Protecting Friend in this world and the next.”

This statement is profound. Yusuf (AS) doesn’t say “You were with me and then You abandoned me and now You’re with me again.” No – “You have been with me all along. You were with me in the well. You were with me when I was sold. You were with me in the house of temptation. You were with me in prison. You were with me through every moment, and You will continue to be with me in the Hereafter.”

تَوَفَّنِى مُسْلِمًا – “When You give me death, give me death as one who has submitted to You. Let me die as a Muslim.”

وَأَلْحِقْنِى بِٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ – “And join me with the righteous.”

This wording is extraordinarily beautiful. Allah ﷻ has just joined him with his family after decades of separation. But Yusuf (AS) says, “You’ve given me this reunion, and it’s more important to me, Allah ﷻ, that You join me with the righteous. Now that You’ve granted me this reunion with my family, that’s the only reunion left that matters – the reunion with the righteous in the Hereafter. Grant me that joining as well.”

Ayah 102

ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ أَنۢبَآءِ ٱلْغَيْبِ نُوحِيهِ إِلَيْكَ ۖ وَمَا كُنتَ لَدَيْهِمْ إِذْ أَجْمَعُوٓا۟ أَمْرَهُمْ وَهُمْ يَمْكُرُونَ

Now Allah ﷻ directly addresses the Prophet ﷺ. ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ أَنۢبَآءِ ٱلْغَيْبِ – “That is from the news of the unseen,” نُوحِيهِ إِلَيْكَ – “that We are revealing to you, that We are inspiring to you.”

وَمَا كُنتَ لَدَيْهِمْ – “And you were not with them,” إِذْ أَجْمَعُوٓا۟ أَمْرَهُمْ – “when they came to a consensus about their plan,” وَهُمْ يَمْكُرُونَ – “when they were plotting and scheming.”

This is remarkable. The Prophet Muhammad ﷺ had no access to these details. He wasn’t there when Yusuf’s brothers gathered to plot against him. He had no Jewish teachers feeding him this information. Yet here it is, recounted with precision and depth.

The irony is profound: they were making a bad plan, thinking harm would result. But so much good came from that plot. An entire legacy began. It wasn’t just one family affected – it was Banu Isra’il, their entire nation, their entire history, all their prophets. All of this traces back to this moment when brothers made a terrible plan. They didn’t know what Allah ﷻ was planning all along.

Ayah 103

وَمَآ أَكْثَرُ ٱلنَّاسِ وَلَوْ حَرَصْتَ بِمُؤْمِنِينَ

After hearing this powerful story, the Prophet ﷺ might think surely the Jews of Madinah will believe now. Surely this evidence is enough. But Allah ﷻ tells him: وَمَآ أَكْثَرُ ٱلنَّاسِ وَلَوْ حَرَصْتَ بِمُؤْمِنِينَ – “And most people, no matter how much you desire it, no matter how badly you want it, are not going to believe.”

This is a powerful consolation. Nothing you do will be enough for them. You could present every proof, every evidence, every miracle, and they will still find reasons to reject.

Ayah 104

وَمَا تَسْـَٔلُهُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ ۚ إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ لِّلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

وَمَا تَسْـَٔلُهُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ – “And you’re not asking them for any sort of compensation over this. You’re not saying ‘Pay me and then I’ll tell you the story.’ You’re not asking for wealth or status or recognition.”

إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ لِّلْعَـٰلَمِينَ – “It is nothing but a reminder for all nations, for all peoples of the world.” You’re giving this message freely as a gift, as a reminder for all of humanity.

Ayah 105

وَكَأَيِّن مِّنْ ءَايَةٍ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ يَمُرُّونَ عَلَيْهَا وَهُمْ عَنْهَا مُعْرِضُونَ

وَكَأَيِّن مِّنْ ءَايَةٍ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ – “And what can be said about these revelations they’re ignoring? How many signs are there in the skies and the earth?”

يَمُرُّونَ عَلَيْهَا – “They pass over them all the time.” وَهُمْ عَنْهَا مُعْرِضُونَ – “And they are completely turning away from them, ignoring them.”

The signs of Allah ﷻ are everywhere – in the rising and setting of the sun, in the growth of plants, in the birth of a child, in the vastness of space, in the intricacy of a cell. But people walk past these signs every day without reflection. This isn’t new for them. Ignoring divine guidance is a pattern as old as humanity itself.

Ayah 106

وَمَا يُؤْمِنُ أَكْثَرُهُم بِٱللَّهِ إِلَّا وَهُم مُّشْرِكُونَ

وَمَا يُؤْمِنُ أَكْثَرُهُم بِٱللَّهِ إِلَّا وَهُم مُّشْرِكُونَ – “And most of them don’t even believe in Allah ﷻ except while they are committing shirk.”

On the surface, we might think of shirk as worshiping idols or believing in the Trinity. But what form of shirk is being combated in this Surah? It’s the shirk of not trusting Allah ﷻ completely. It’s the shirk of losing hope in Him. It’s the shirk of putting all your expectations and reliance on yourself or on others instead of on Him. It’s the shirk of thinking you’re in control, that your planning supersedes His planning.

This is a subtle but devastating form of shirk – claiming to believe in Allah ﷻ while functionally relying on everything except Him.

Ayah 107

أَفَأَمِنُوٓا۟ أَن تَأْتِيَهُمْ غَـٰشِيَةٌ مِّنْ عَذَابِ ٱللَّهِ أَوْ تَأْتِيَهُمُ ٱلسَّاعَةُ بَغْتَةً وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ

أَفَأَمِنُوٓا۟ – “Are they feeling safe?” أَن تَأْتِيَهُمْ غَـٰشِيَةٌ مِّنْ عَذَابِ ٱللَّهِ – “That a covering, hovering punishment might come to them from Allah ﷻ’s punishment?”

أَوْ تَأْتِيَهُمُ ٱلسَّاعَةُ بَغْتَةً – “Or the Hour itself will come at them suddenly, out of nowhere, without warning?” وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ – “While they won’t even realize what’s happening?”

Do they really feel secure from Allah’s plan? Do they think they’re immune to consequences? This is a wake-up call, a reminder that no one should feel so safe and comfortable that they forget accountability.

Ayah 108

قُلْ هَـٰذِهِۦ سَبِيلِىٓ أَدْعُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ۚ عَلَىٰ بَصِيرَةٍ أَنَا۠ وَمَنِ ٱتَّبَعَنِى ۖ وَسُبْحَـٰنَ ٱللَّهِ وَمَآ أَنَا۠ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ

The Surah concludes with one of the most beautiful declarations in the Quran about the confidence and clarity with which the Messenger ﷺ proclaims his message.

قُلْ – “Say.” No matter what criticisms they make, no matter what objections they raise, just declare this:

هَـٰذِهِۦ سَبِيلِىٓ – “This is my path.”

أَدْعُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ – “I call to Allah ﷻ.”

عَلَىٰ بَصِيرَةٍ – “Upon clear insight, upon clear evidence.” The word بَصِيرَةٍ is powerful. It means insight, clear vision, evidence-based understanding. The Messenger ﷺ is saying, “I don’t call to this religion with my eyes closed. I call to it with my eyes wide open. When you ask for evidence, I furnish it. When you ask for intellectual proofs, I provide them. This is not blind faith. This is not ritualistic tradition based on hearsay from the past. It is based on clear proof and clear evidence.”

Our religion is unique in this regard. We ask our believers to have both spiritual and intellectual conviction. Some religions emphasize only spiritual faith – emotional connection without intellectual rigor. Others, like certain philosophical traditions, emphasize only intellectual inquiry while denying spiritual realities. Islam demands both. That’s what بَصِيرَةٍ represents – clear, comprehensive insight.

There’s also a beautiful wordplay here. Ya’qub (AS), even when he became physically blind, never lost his بَصِيرَةٍ, his spiritual insight. He could see something while blind that nobody else could see with functioning eyes. He could sense Yusuf (AS) in the wind when everyone thought he was delusional.

أَنَا۠ وَمَنِ ٱتَّبَعَنِى – “I and whoever follows me.” This path of calling to Allah ﷻ with clear insight isn’t just for the Prophet ﷺ. It’s for everyone who follows him. We are all called to understand our faith deeply, to have both heart and mind engaged, to be able to articulate and defend our beliefs with clarity and evidence.

وَسُبْحَـٰنَ ٱللَّهِ – “And glory be to Allah ﷻ, how perfect He is.”

وَمَآ أَنَا۠ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ – “And I am not from those who commit shirk.”

This final declaration brings us full circle. The entire story of Yusuf (AS) has been about trusting Allah ﷻ completely, about recognizing His plan even when we can’t see it, about avoiding the subtle shirk of putting our trust in anyone or anything besides Him.

When the Prophet ﷺ hears this legacy of Yusuf (AS), his own problems become easier to bear. His struggles feel lighter. Allah ﷻ is essentially telling him, “Just think of the Messenger Yusuf (AS). Remember what he went through and how I brought him through it all.”

This is the gift of this Surah – not just a story, but a reminder that Allah’s plan is always unfolding, even when we can’t perceive it. That He is اللَّطِيفُ, subtle and perfect in His planning. That He is الْعَلِيمُ الْحَكِيمُ, All-Knowing and All-Wise in every situation we face.

Ayah 102–111 · linked video: 13. Yusuf (Ayah 102-111)

Surah Yusuf: Ayahs 102-111

Understanding the Concluding Passage

This concluding passage of Surah Yusuf marks a significant transition. The story of Prophet Yusuf (AS) reached its completion at ayah 101, and now Allah ﷻ addresses the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ directly. These final ayahs serve as bookends to the surah, like parentheses enclosing the narrative. The beginning introduced the surah, the story unfolded in the middle, and now the ending brings us back to the present moment of the Prophet’s ﷺ mission.

Why include these passages that aren’t directly about Yusuf (AS)? The answer reveals the beautiful mercy of Allah ﷻ toward His Messenger ﷺ. When the Prophet ﷺ was experiencing devastating personal losses - the death of his beloved wife Khadijah (RA), his protective uncle Abu Talib, and mounting opposition in Makkah - he needed more than just answers to theological questions. He needed counsel, comfort and hope.

Think of an employer who notices an excellent employee going through personal difficulties. A good employer offers time off. An exceptional one provides counseling and support. How much more beautiful, then, is the relationship between Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ? While the Prophet’s ﷺ mission continued - delivering the message remained his primary task - Allah ﷻ simultaneously provided him with exactly what he needed for his personal struggles.

Ayah 102

ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ أَنۢبَآءِ ٱلْغَيْبِ نُوحِيهِ إِلَيْكَ ۖ وَمَا كُنتَ لَدَيْهِمْ إِذْ أَجْمَعُوٓا۟ أَمْرَهُمْ وَهُمْ يَمْكُرُونَ

This ayah returns to the external purpose of the surah - establishing proof of divine revelation. Allah ﷻ tells the Prophet ﷺ: “That is from the news of the unseen which We reveal to you. And you were not with them when they came to unanimous decision about their plan while they were plotting.”

The phrase أَنۢبَآءِ ٱلْغَيْبِ (news of the unseen) is decisive. The Prophet ﷺ had no access to these detailed accounts. He wasn’t present when Yusuf’s (AS) brothers conspired against him. He didn’t witness their private conversations or secret plans. The only possible source for such detailed, accurate narrative is divine revelation. This serves as powerful evidence to the People of the Book that what they’re hearing isn’t folklore or borrowed tales - it’s revelation from Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 103

وَمَآ أَكْثَرُ ٱلنَّاسِ وَلَوْ حَرَصْتَ بِمُؤْمِنِينَ

After establishing the miraculous nature of this revelation, Allah ﷻ delivers a difficult truth: “And most people, no matter how much you desire it, will not become believers.”

Notice the structure of this statement. The word حَرَصْتَ (you desire) is inserted in the middle, creating emphasis. It’s as if the Prophet ﷺ is anxiously waiting for the conclusion of this sentence: “Most people will not be…” Will not be what? “…believers, no matter how badly you want them to believe.”

This connects directly to the previous ayah. Even the most incredible proof - أَحْسَنَ ٱلْقَصَصِ (the best of narratives) - won’t be enough for some people. This isn’t a criticism of the Prophet’s ﷺ efforts or the message itself. Rather, it’s preparing him for the reality that belief requires an open heart, and many hearts remain closed despite clear evidence.

Ayah 104

وَمَا تَسْـَٔلُهُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ ۚ إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ لِّلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

“And you do not ask them for any reward for it. It is nothing but a reminder for all peoples.”

This ayah addresses a common tactic used to undermine messengers throughout history: questioning their motives. Are they seeking wealth? Power? Control? Allah ﷻ makes clear that the Prophet ﷺ asks for no compensation. This counters allegations that still persist today - that religion is merely a means of manipulating people, a scheme to extract donations and maintain institutional power.

The message is described as ذِكْرٌ لِّلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (a reminder for all peoples). It’s universal in scope and pure in purpose - a reminder of fundamental truths, not a financial transaction.

Ayah 105

وَكَأَيِّن مِّنْ ءَايَةٍ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ يَمُرُّونَ عَلَيْهَا وَهُمْ عَنْهَا مُعْرِضُونَ

“And how many signs in the heavens and the earth do they pass by, yet they turn away from them.”

The verb يَمُرُّونَ (they pass) is significant, but even more striking is the construction with عَلَيْهَا - they pass over them. When you pass by something, you might simply not notice it. But when you pass over something, you literally stumble upon it. How do you ignore what’s directly beneath your feet?

The signs of Allah’s ﷻ creation surround us constantly - in the heavens above and the earth below. The intricate design of the universe, the perfect balance of natural systems, the miracle of life itself. Yet despite encountering these signs repeatedly, people remain مُعْرِضُونَ (turning away, oblivious). They step right over the evidence and continue on their way unchanged.

Ayah 106

وَمَا يُؤْمِنُ أَكْثَرُهُم بِٱللَّهِ إِلَّا وَهُم مُّشْرِكُونَ

“And most of them believe not in Allah except while they associate others with Him.”

This ayah provides a glimmer of hope after the previous sobering statements, but with a critical caveat. Many people do believe in God - but they mix that belief with shirk (associating partners with Allah ﷻ).

This is crucial to understand. Allah ﷻ isn’t saying people don’t believe in God at all. Rather, their belief is corrupted by shirk. Many people throughout history have said “I believe in God” - but what does that belief look like? Is it pure monotheism, or is it tainted with false concepts?
